## Overview
Seth Hutchinson is a senior researcher and professor of robotics at **Northeastern University**, leading a research group focused on advancing the theory and practice of robot perception, planning, and control. His lab bridges rigorous algorithmic development with real-world robotic systems, emphasizing **visual servoing**, **motion planning**, and multi-robot coordination. Hutchinson's work is distinguished by its blend of foundational textbooks and influential tutorials alongside applied projects (from sensing and tracking to biomimetic flight), and it consistently emphasizes <u>robust perception-to-action</u> pipelines and <u>real-time control</u> for autonomous systems. His publications and textbooks serve as canonical references in the field, and his research agenda places particular value on *principled, implementable methods* that connect theory to practice.

---

## Research Areas
Hutchinson's research spans several core areas of robotics, with sustained contributions to both theory and systems. He is best known for foundational work in **visual servo control** (e.g., "A tutorial on visual servo control" (2002), "Visual servo control. I. Basic approaches" (2006), and "Visual servo control. II. Advanced approaches" (2007)), which develops control laws that close the loop on image measurements for manipulation and mobile platforms. Complementing perception-driven control, he has authored influential texts on kinematics and dynamics ("Robot Modeling and Control" (2006); "Robot modeling and control, 2nd edition" (2020)) and coauthored broad treatments of motion planning ("Principles of Robot Motion" (2005)). His work also addresses multi-robot coordination and planning under uncertainty ("Optimal motion planning for multiple robots having independent goals" (2002); "Path planning for permutation-invariant multirobot formations" (2006)), sensing and exploration strategies ("Planning exploration strategies for simultaneous localization and mapping" (2006); "Planning Sensing Strategies in a Robot Work Cell with Multi-Sensor Capabilities" (1989)), and practical performance issues such as real-time tracking and estimator design ("Real-time vision, tracking and control" (2000); "Estimating uncertainty in SSD-based feature tracking" (2002)). More recent and applied contributions include biomimetic flight platforms ("A biomimetic robotic platform to study flight specializations of bats" (2017); "Bat Bot (B2), a biologically inspired flying machine" (2016)) and long-duration autonomy and task-allocation for heterogeneous teams ("Robot ecology: Constraint-based control design for long duration autonomy" (2018); "A resilient and energy-aware task allocation framework for heterogeneous multirobot systems" (2021)).

## Notable Works
- "Robot Modeling and Control" (2006) — a widely used textbook that synthesizes kinematics, dynamics, and control for manipulators and mobile robots; it has become a standard reference for both teaching and research in robot modeling and control, and its later edition (2020) continues to influence curriculum and practice.  
- "A tutorial on visual servo control" (2002) — a seminal tutorial paper that established a coherent conceptual framework for image-based and position-based visual servoing, clarifying prerequisites from robotics and computer vision and guiding two decades of follow-on research.  
- "Principles of Robot Motion: Theory, Algorithms, and Implementations" (2005) — a comprehensive treatment of motion planning that connects rigorous theory to algorithmic implementation, widely cited for its role in making planning algorithms accessible and practical for robotic systems.

## Academic Background
Seth Hutchinson has a multi-decade academic career with research and teaching roles spanning leading robotics institutions. Over the years he has collaborated with and published alongside researchers at the University of Illinois and Georgia Tech, and he is currently affiliated with **Northeastern University**, where he continues to supervise students and lead research projects. Hutchinson is the author and coauthor of foundational textbooks and highly cited tutorials that have shaped robotics education and research; his work has accrued over 33,000 citations, reflecting broad influence across control, perception, planning, and multirobot systems. His publication record (from early work on sensing strategies and planning in the late 1980s and 1990s to recent contributions on robotic grasping and long-duration autonomy) demonstrates a sustained impact on both theoretical foundations and real-world robotic applications.

Cyber Forensics, Systems Security, Malware Analysis, Memory Forensics, Android Forensics, Software Supply Chain Security, Package Manager Attacks, Log-based Attack Reconstruction, Symbolic Root Cause Analysis, Web Application/CMS Abuse

## Overview
Brendan D. Saltaformaggio is an **Associate Professor** in the School of Electrical and Computer Engineering at the **Georgia Institute of Technology**, where he leads a research group within the ECE department focused on systems- and evidence-driven security research. His work centers on **cyber forensics**, **systems security**, and **malware analysis**, with an emphasis on building tools and methodologies that help defenders and investigators reason about complex, real-world attacks. In particular, his group advances techniques for *memory forensics*, *log-based attack reconstruction*, and *supply-chain and software ecosystem security*, addressing challenges that span from low-level binary instrumentation to large-scale empirical measurement. Key technical themes in his work include <u>attack provenance and reconstruction</u>, <u>automated root-cause analysis</u>, and <u>abuse of web and package ecosystems</u>.

---

## Research Areas
Saltaformaggio’s research spans the intersection of systems research and applied security. He has developed methods for reconstructing multi-stage attacks from dispersed telemetry (e.g., the graph-based reconstruction techniques in "Hercule"), and advanced the state of the art in memory forensics and binary analysis (work on GUI and photographic evidence recovery from memory images, RetroScope-style screen recovery, and platform-agnostic binary rewriters like RevARM). He studies the privacy and information-leakage risks of mobile systems, including traffic-analysis attacks that infer fine-grained app activities over encrypted channels, and device-wide user-activity sequencing from Android memory images. His recent and highly cited work investigates ecosystem-level threats: empirical measurement and characterization of _supply-chain attacks against package managers_, large-scale studies of malicious plugins and web-app abuse used by malware, and automated remediation techniques for vulnerable open-source components embedded in application binaries. Across these threads, he emphasizes practical systems that scale to real-world workloads and support forensic investigation and incident response.

## Notable Works
- "Towards Measuring Supply Chain Attacks on Package Managers for Interpreted Languages" (2021, 255 citations) — A large-scale empirical study that quantified how package managers are abused by adversaries, demonstrating attack patterns and proposing measurement methodologies to detect and mitigate supply-chain compromises in language ecosystems.  
- "Hercule: Attack story reconstruction via community discovery on correlated log graph" (2016, 248 citations) — Introduced a correlated-log-graph approach to stitch dispersed log footprints into coherent multi-stage attack narratives, enabling automated attack-story reconstruction for intrusion analysis.  
- "Eavesdropping on Fine-Grained User Activities Within Smartphone Apps Over Encrypted Network Traffic" (2016, 148 citations) — Demonstrated that passive observers can infer fine-grained in-app user actions from encrypted network traffic, exposing privacy risks in modern mobile apps and motivating countermeasures for sensitive application flows.

## Academic Background
Brendan Saltaformaggio completed advanced graduate training in computing and has established a research program at Georgia Tech that bridges systems engineering and digital forensics. His publications appear in top security and systems venues and collectively have accrued over **1,855** citations, reflecting broad impact across academia and practice. His work has been supported through collaborations with industry partners and by competitive research funding, and he regularly mentors graduate students and postdoctoral researchers who pursue forensic, malware, and systems-security topics. He is an active member of the security research community, contributing tools, datasets, and empirical studies that have informed defenses against supply-chain compromise, mobile- and cloud-based abuse, and stealthy multi-stage attacks.

## Overview
Peter A. Freeman is a senior researcher and academic leader affiliated with **Georgia Tech** and **UC Irvine**, with a long career spanning research, education, and science policy. He has led and contributed to labs and groups focused on **software engineering**, *design theory*, and the intersection of computing with public policy and workforce studies. Freeman’s work is characterized by sustained contributions to the theory and practice of <u>software reuse</u>, <u>requirements engineering</u>, and the application of <u>artificial intelligence</u> techniques to software development problems, as well as engagement with *broader science and technology policy* questions. His homepage (http://www.cc.gatech.edu/people/peter-freeman) provides a gateway to his publications and professional activities.

---

## Research Areas
Freeman’s research spans core and applied topics in **software engineering** and computing policy. Technically, he has advanced the study of <u>software reusability</u>—developing faceted classification schemes and reusable engineering concepts that make components and development knowledge easier to locate and apply (see "Classifying software for reusability", 1987; "Reusable software engineering: Concepts and research directions", 1983). He has made foundational contributions to *requirements engineering* and validation, introducing and formalizing viewpoint-based approaches to detect and resolve conflicting stakeholder needs ("Requirements validation through viewpoint resolution", 1991). Freeman also worked on models of design and functional reasoning that shaped thinking about the design process in software-intensive systems ("A model for functional reasoning in design", 1971; "The nature of design", 1980). Beyond technical SE topics, his work addresses computing education and workforce issues—proposing curricula and pedagogy for software engineering and analyzing national IT workforce supply—and engages with science policy and the institutional history of computing (e.g., studies on IT worker supply and the role of the NSF). In recent years he has participated in interdisciplinary collaborations that connect software tooling and bioinformatics (contributing to variant-formatting tools and packages), illustrating a career-long pattern of *bridging theory, tools, education, and policy*.

## Notable Works
- "Classifying software for reusability" (1987) — A highly influential paper (1,172 citations) proposing a faceted classification and retrieval approach that helped define early research agendas for making software assets discoverable and reusable across projects and organizations.  
- "Requirements validation through viewpoint resolution" (1991) — Introduced the Viewpoint Resolution (VPR) technique (367 citations) for early validation of complex-system requirements by eliciting, comparing, and reconciling stakeholder viewpoints.  
- "The Supply of Information Technology Workers in the United States." (1999) — A major policy-oriented study (355 citations) analyzing the politics, measurement, and dynamics of the IT labor market; this work highlights Freeman’s engagement with *science policy* and workforce planning in computing.

## Academic Background
Over several decades Freeman has combined faculty roles, leadership, and advisory activity at institutions including **Georgia Tech** and **UC Irvine**, producing a broad corpus of work recognized across software engineering, design research, computing education, and policy. He has authored and edited influential tutorials and curricula (e.g., proposed curricula for software engineering, tutorials on design and reusability) and contributed to national conversations on computing’s institutional foundations and workforce needs (including historical and organizational studies involving the NSF). His publication record (total citations ~4,895) and repeated invitations to write tutorials, reviews, and position pieces reflect recognition as a *thought leader* in both technical and policy dimensions of computing. Freeman’s career demonstrates sustained interdisciplinary collaboration (including later contributions to bioinformatics tooling), leadership in shaping software engineering education, and active participation in shaping the research agenda connecting computing practice to societal and policy concerns. For further details on publications, affiliations, and recent activities, see his professional homepage: http://www.cc.gatech.edu/people/peter-freeman.
software engineering, science policy, articificial intelligence

## Overview
Lien Diaz is the Director of the **Constellations Center for Equity in Computing** at **Georgia Tech**, where she leads work at the intersection of research, policy, and practice to advance equitable access to computer science. Her lab/group centers on translating evidence into scalable supports for K–12 and higher-education partners, with a primary research focus on **equity in CS education**, *coaching for inclusive pedagogy*, and the role of curricular pathways such as the **Advanced Placement** program in shaping participation. Her scholarship and leadership emphasize <u>broadening participation in computing</u>, <u>inclusive pedagogy</u>, and <u>equitable CS instruction</u> across diverse school contexts.

---

## Research Areas
Lien Diaz’s research spans empirical and design-focused work in K–12 and early undergraduate computing education. She studies how curricular reforms and credentialing mechanisms—most notably the development and rollout of **AP Computer Science Principles (AP CSP)**—affect classroom practice, educator preparation, and policy (see "CS principles: development and evolution of a course and a community" (2013) and "Does the Advanced Placement Computer Science (CS) Principles course drive equitable and inclusive CS pedagogy…" (2024)). Diaz investigates operationalizing equity beyond rhetoric, developing shared visions for justice-driven practice ("Going beyond the platitudes of equity" (2019)), and provides frameworks and recommendations for conducting high-quality, equity-focused K–12 CS research ("Building Recommendations for Conducting Equity-Focused, High Quality K-12 Computer Science Education Research" (2023)). She also examines practical mechanisms to support teachers—such as *instructional coaching* ("Coaching to Improve CS Teaching and Learning" (2022)) and leadership models for broadening participation ("Setting the table for equity" (2022))—and explores applications of service learning, assessment design (AP CSP performance tasks), and partnerships (e.g., College Board, BootUp, We.org) to promote inclusion.

## Notable Works
- "Does the Advanced Placement Computer Science (CS) Principles course drive equitable and inclusive CS pedagogy, curriculum, and policy as a means to broaden participation in …" (2024) — A recent empirical and policy-oriented examination of AP CSP’s impact on classroom pedagogy and its potential to serve as a lever for <u>broadening participation</u> in computing.
- "Going beyond the platitudes of equity: Developing a shared vision for equity in computer science education" (2019) — A conceptual and community-building contribution that articulates concrete visions and practices for operationalizing **equity** in CS education research and practice.
- "CS principles: development and evolution of a course and a community" (2013) — Documentation and analysis of the AP CSP project’s design, community-building, and early implementation, laying groundwork for subsequent AP-driven efforts to diversify pathways into computing.

## Academic Background
Diaz’s publication record and leadership roles indicate long-standing engagement with national CS education initiatives and professional communities (College Board AP development efforts, SIGCSE venues, and partnerships with organizations such as BootUp and We.org). As Director of the Constellations Center for Equity in Computing at Georgia Tech, she combines institutional leadership with scholarship focused on actionable equity interventions, teacher supports, and policy implications. Her sustained contributions to AP curriculum design and rollout (papers dating from 2009 onward) and to equity-focused research guidance (2023 recommendations paper) position her as a recognized leader in efforts to expand and improve K–12 CS access. Her work is cited across the CS education community (total citations ~62) and reflects both scholarly influence and practical impact on curriculum, coaching, and collaborative K–12/university initiatives.

## Overview
John Stasko is **Professor Emeritus of Interactive Computing** at the **Georgia Institute of Technology**, where his long career has been centered in the intersection of **Information Visualization**, **Visual Analytics**, and **Human–Computer Interaction**. He led and collaborated with research groups studying how people interact with visual representations of data and how visual tools can amplify analytic reasoning. His work blends systems and empirical methods to address practical and theoretical problems — from <u>fault localization</u> in software (e.g., visualization of test information) and <u>algorithm animation</u> frameworks (e.g., Tango) to the study of <u>analytic interaction</u> and the generation of <u>insight</u> — with an emphasis on the role of *interaction*, *evaluation*, and *usability* in visualization systems.

---

## Research Areas
Stasko’s research spans core topics in information visualization and its applications. He has made foundational contributions to the design and evaluation of visual representations and interactive techniques (e.g., radial space‑filling displays, focus+context methods) and to understanding how interaction supports analytic activity (see "Toward a deeper understanding of the role of interaction in information visualization" (2007) and "Low‑level components of analytic activity in information visualization" (2005)). He has advanced the use of visualization for software engineering problems, especially debugging and fault localization ("Visualization of test information to assist fault localization" (2002); "Visualization for fault localization" (2001)), and has been an active developer of visualization systems and frameworks (Tango (1990) and later systems such as Jigsaw (2008) and DataMeadow (2008)). His work also addresses pedagogical and cognitive dimensions of visualization — algorithm animation and learning ("A meta‑study of algorithm visualization effectiveness" (2002); XTANGO/Tango work) — as well as newer themes like natural‑language interfaces for visual analysis (NL4DV (2020)), automated narrative/fact generation for charts ("Augmenting visualizations with interactive data facts" (2018)), casual and ambient information displays, and evaluation methodologies that go beyond raw task performance to measure *value*, *insight*, and *user experience*.

---

## Notable Works
- "Visualization of test information to assist fault localization" (2002) — introduced visualization techniques to help developers locate software faults by surfacing suspicious statements and test information, influencing work at the intersection of software engineering and visualization.  
- "Toward a deeper understanding of the role of interaction in information visualization" (2007) — provided conceptual frameworks and taxonomies that shaped how the community thinks about interaction as a first‑class component of InfoVis systems and studies.  
- "Tango: A framework and system for algorithm animation" (1990) — an early and influential framework for algorithm animation that advanced research and teaching practices in algorithm visualization and helped define a research agenda on dynamic visual representations.

---

## Academic Background
John Stasko has had a multi‑decade academic career at the Georgia Institute of Technology in the School of Interactive Computing / College of Computing, advancing research, systems, and evaluation practices in information visualization and human–computer interaction. Over his career he produced a wide portfolio of influential papers, systems, and edited volumes, supervised generations of students, and contributed extensively to the visualization research community through program and editorial service. His scholarship has been widely cited (total citations ~29,225) and spans early work on algorithm animation and program visualization through contemporary contributions to visual analytics, natural‑language interfaces, and visualization evaluation. More information about his publications, projects, and students is available on his professional homepage: https://faculty.cc.gatech.edu/~stasko

## Overview
Victor Fung is an **Assistant Professor** in **Computational Science and Engineering** at the *Georgia Institute of Technology*, where he leads the **Fung Group** (https://www.fung-group.org/). His lab combines first-principles electronic-structure methods, atomistic modeling, and modern machine learning to accelerate the design of functional materials. Fung's primary research focus lies at the intersection of **heterogeneous catalysis**, **nanomaterials**, and **materials informatics** — with particular emphasis on <u>single-atom and atomically precise catalysts</u>, <u>structure–property relationships in metal nanoclusters and perovskites</u>, and <u>AI-driven materials discovery</u>. His work spans detailed mechanistic studies of catalytic systems and the development of predictive, physically informed machine learning models that enable high-throughput screening and inverse design.

---

## Research Areas
Fung’s research portfolio integrates computational catalysis and materials machine learning. On the catalysis side, he has advanced understanding of atomically dispersed metals and atomically precise nanoclusters — for example in studies such as "Golden single-atomic-site platinum electrocatalysts" (2018) and "Single rhodium atoms anchored in micropores for efficient transformation of methane under mild conditions" (2018) where the interplay between local geometry, electronic structure, and catalytic function is elucidated. His group has applied density functional theory (DFT) and mechanistic modeling to perovskite and oxide supports ("Exploring perovskites for methane activation from first principles", 2018; "Perovskite-supported Pt single atoms for methane activation", 2020), addressing issues of stabilization, support–metal interactions and reaction selectivity. In nanochemistry, Fung has contributed to atom-by-atom growth and fluxionality studies ("Understanding seed-mediated growth of gold nanoclusters at molecular level", 2017; "Exploring Structural Diversity and Fluxionality of Ptn (n = 10–13) Clusters from First-Principles", 2017), connecting synthesis routes to emergent properties. On the data-driven side, he has helped bridge physics-based descriptors and machine learning — notable works include "Benchmarking graph neural networks for materials chemistry" (2021), "Machine learned features from density of states for accurate adsorption energy prediction" (2021), and more recent efforts in invertible networks and LLM-enabled materials design ("Inverse design of two-dimensional materials with invertible neural networks", 2021; "Llmatdesign: Autonomous materials discovery with large language models", 2024). These efforts emphasize the importance of <u>physically informed features</u>, benchmarking of model architectures, and strategies for scalable, high-throughput property prediction.

---

## Notable Works
- "Golden single-atomic-site platinum electrocatalysts" (2018) — Demonstrated colloidal strategies and atomic-level control of bimetallic sites to tune geometric and electronic effects in electrocatalysts, offering a model platform for correlating site structure with catalytic performance.
- "Insights into interfaces, stability, electronic properties, and catalytic activities of atomically precise metal nanoclusters from first principles" (2018) — Provided a comprehensive first-principles framework for understanding ligand-protected nanoclusters, linking atomic precision to electronic structure and catalytic trends.
- "Benchmarking graph neural networks for materials chemistry" (2021) — Systematically evaluated GNN architectures for materials applications, establishing performance baselines and best practices that have guided subsequent ML-driven materials screening and model selection.

---

## Academic Background
Victor Fung leads an interdisciplinary research program that bridges *computational materials science*, *chemistry*, and *artificial intelligence for science*. His publication record (total citations ~6,141) reflects sustained, high-impact contributions across catalysis, nanomaterials, and materials informatics. At Georgia Tech he heads the Fung Group in Computational Science and Engineering and collaborates broadly with experimental and theoretical teams in catalysis, surface science, and machine learning. His research has been published in high-profile journals and has influenced both fundamental understanding (e.g., single-atom catalysis, nanocluster dynamics, perovskite supports) and methodological advances (physically informed ML features, GNN benchmarking, inverse design). Fung’s work positions him as a leading investigator in the use of computational and data-driven approaches to accelerate materials discovery and to translate atomic-scale insight into design principles for next-generation catalysts and functional materials.

## Overview
Larry Heck is a senior researcher based at the **Georgia Institute of Technology**, affiliated with the College of Computing, where he leads a research group focused on *building robust, scalable conversational and spoken-language systems*. His work sits at the intersection of **speech and language processing**, **deep learning**, and *interactive AI* — with enduring contributions to <u>dialogue systems</u>, <u>speaker recognition</u>, and <u>semantic modeling for retrieval and understanding</u>. Across industry and academia he has combined signal- and model-based approaches with modern neural architectures to tackle real-world conversational problems.

---

## Research Areas
Heck's research spans a broad set of topics in spoken and conversational AI informed by both early signal-processing work and contemporary deep learning. Core themes include neural semantic modeling for search (notably deep structured semantic models), sequence modeling for *slot filling* and intent classification using recurrent architectures, and hierarchical/contextual sequence models (e.g., CLSTM) for large-scale NLP. He has extensive work on speech-centric problems — from prosody and pitch modeling for speaker recognition to toolkits for speaker-ID research — and on practical, scalable components for dialogue systems: session and context modeling, multi-domain dialogue state tracking, unsupervised/zero-shot semantic parsing and domain adaptation, and methods for bootstrapping task-oriented agents (e.g., dialogue self-play / M2M). More recently his work also addresses model robustness and grounding: methods for visual grounding in vision+language models (HINT) and *incremental learning* techniques for updating deployed models without catastrophic forgetting.

---

## Notable Works
- "Learning deep structured semantic models for web search using clickthrough data" (2013) — Introduced large-scale neural approaches that project queries and documents into a common semantic space (DSSM), substantially advancing neural retrieval and influencing later neural ranking and representation work.
- "Using recurrent neural networks for slot filling in spoken language understanding" (2014) — Pioneered RNN architectures tailored to the slot-filling problem in SLU, improving sequence labeling for intent/argument extraction in conversational systems.
- "Building a conversational agent overnight with dialogue self-play" (2018) — Presented the M2M framework that combines automation and crowdsourcing to rapidly bootstrap goal-oriented dialogue agents, enabling scalable domain transfer and fast dialog agent development.

---

## Academic Background
Larry Heck's career bridges long-standing industrial research and academic leadership: prior to his current affiliation at Georgia Tech he contributed extensively within industry research labs (notably Microsoft Research), producing influential patents, toolkits (e.g., the MSR Identity Toolbox), and a large body of peer-reviewed publications. His publication record covers signal processing, HMM-based monitoring, prosody and pitch modeling for speaker recognition (1990s–2000s), and a sustained transition into deep learning for language, retrieval, and dialogue (2010s–2020s). His work has been widely adopted and cited (total citations: **15,328**), reflecting both methodological impact and practical systems deployment. He has held leadership and collaborative roles across interdisciplinary teams, mentoring students and researchers and contributing to the transition of research prototypes into production-capable conversational technologies.

## Overview
Greg Eisenhauer is a senior researcher and faculty member in the **College of Computing** at **Georgia Institute of Technology**, where he leads a research group focused on high-performance system software and data-intensive computing (see his homepage: http://www.cc.gatech.edu/~eisen). His work spans the design and implementation of scalable middleware, runtime systems, and tools that enable *online and interactive analysis* of large simulations and live service infrastructures. Across decades of work he has advanced <u>online monitoring and steering</u>, <u>scalable data staging and I/O</u>, and <u>flexible communication and streaming</u> abstractions that bridge traditional enterprise information flows and extreme-scale scientific computing.

---

## Research Areas
Eisenhauer’s research centers on the systems and middleware required to make modern high-performance and data-intensive applications usable and efficient. He has repeatedly addressed the tension between computation and data movement: from frameworks that enable *interactive steering and observation* of parallel codes (e.g., "Falcon: On-line monitoring and steering of large-scale parallel programs" and "Falcon—toward interactive parallel programs") to scalable data staging approaches for petascale workflows (e.g., "Datastager: scalable data staging services for petascale applications") and unified I/O frameworks for scientific data management ("ADIOS 2: The adaptable input output system"). He has developed in situ and streaming analytics approaches (e.g., "Goldrush", "Flexpath", "FlexIO", "I/O containers", "Just in time: adding value to the io pipelines...") to reduce I/O bottlenecks and enable real-time insight. Complementary lines of work include high-performance publish/subscribe and event delivery for HPC ("Publish-subscribe for high-performance computing", "Event services for high performance computing", "The ECho Event Delivery Systems"), efficient wire formats and portable binary representations ("Efficient Wire Formats for High Performance Computing", "Native data representation"), and enterprise-scale operational information flows and self-managing systems ("imanage", "Operational information systems"). More recent contributions extend these ideas to heterogeneous and accelerator-rich environments (e.g., "Lynx", "Landrush") and to transitions from file-based to streaming pipelines ("Transitioning from file-based HPC workflows to streaming data pipelines with openPMD and ADIOS2").

## Notable Works
- Falcon — "Falcon: On-line monitoring and steering of large-scale parallel programs" (1995): a foundational framework for *online monitoring and steering* of parallel applications that demonstrated how interactive control and observation can improve application performance and correctness; one of Eisenhauer’s most-cited and influential early contributions.  
- Datastager — "Datastager: scalable data staging services for petascale applications" (2009): a seminal paper addressing the I/O and noise challenges of petascale systems by introducing scalable data-staging services to reduce I/O costs and variability for checkpointing and output workflows.  
- ADIOS 2 — "Adios 2: The adaptable input output system. a framework for high-performance data management" (2020): leadership in a modern, widely adopted I/O framework that unifies APIs for scalable I/O, in situ analysis, and streaming across supercomputers, clouds, and personal systems.

## Academic Background
Greg Eisenhauer is a long-standing member of the Georgia Tech College of Computing faculty and a prolific contributor to the high-performance computing and distributed systems communities, with over 4,068 citations across his publications. His publication record (spanning the mid-1990s to the present) shows sustained leadership in middleware, runtime systems, and end-to-end data pipelines for both scientific and enterprise applications. His projects have produced widely used software (notably ADIOS2) and influenced practice at national-scale HPC centers and enterprise deployments, reflecting extensive collaborations with systems groups and domain scientists. Eisenhauer has served as principal investigator or co-investigator on multiple funded research efforts that bridge academia, national labs, and industry; he is also active in mentoring graduate students and postdoctoral researchers who advance topics in *scalable I/O*, *in situ analytics*, and *streaming data infrastructures*.

## Overview
Jan van den Brand is a faculty researcher affiliated with **Georgia Tech**, where he leads a research agenda and group working at the intersection of **algorithms**, *data structures*, and *optimization*. His work focuses on the design and analysis of fast exact and approximate algorithms for fundamental problems in combinatorial optimization and linear algebra, with particular emphasis on <u>linear programming</u>, <u>minimum-cost flow</u>, and <u>dynamic matrix and graph algorithms</u>. He combines tools from **algebraic algorithms**, **convex optimization**, and advanced data-structural techniques to obtain state-of-the-art runtimes and deterministic or near-deterministic guarantees.

---

## Research Areas
Van den Brand’s research spans several tightly connected areas: the development of fast solvers for convex programs (notably interior-point and primal–dual methods for linear programs), nearly-linear-time algorithms for flows and matchings, and the design of robust algebraic data structures that maintain matrix- and graph-related quantities under updates. Representative topics include deterministic and randomized linear-program solvers (e.g., “A deterministic linear program solver in current matrix multiplication time”), near-linear algorithms for minimum-cost flow and related problems (e.g., “Minimum cost flows, MDPs, and ℓ1-regression in nearly linear time for dense instances”), fast algorithms for bipartite matching on moderately dense graphs, and dynamic linear-algebraic primitives such as the dynamic matrix inverse (e.g., “Dynamic matrix inverse: Improved algorithms and matching conditional lower bounds”). He also explores applications of these techniques beyond classical combinatorial problems — for example, fast training approaches for overparameterized neural networks and algorithmic questions about attention in large language models — demonstrating a practice of unifying algebraic, combinatorial, and convex-optimization perspectives across both static and dynamic settings.

## Notable Works
- “A deterministic linear program solver in current matrix multiplication time” (2020) — Introduced a deterministic interior-point style solver that leverages fast matrix operations to push linear-program solving runtimes toward the current matrix-multiplication barrier, substantially advancing the theory of exact LP solvers.  
- “Minimum cost flows, MDPs, and ℓ1-regression in nearly linear time for dense instances” (2021) — Developed randomized algorithms that achieve improved runtimes for minimum-cost flow and related optimization problems on dense instances, bridging convex-programming techniques and flow-specific structure.  
- “Bipartite matching in nearly-linear time on moderately dense graphs” (2020) — Gave a nearly-linear-time randomized algorithm for maximum cardinality bipartite matching in the moderately dense regime, with broader implications for transshipment, optimal transport, and negative-weight shortest paths.

## Academic Background
Jan van den Brand’s doctoral thesis, “Dynamic Matrix Algorithms and Applications in Convex and Combinatorial Optimization” (2021), crystallizes a line of work on maintaining algebraic structures under updates and applying these primitives to dynamic graph and optimization problems. Since then he has been affiliated with **Georgia Tech**, building a research program that has produced highly cited contributions across algorithms, data structures, and optimization (total citations ≈ 1,537). His publication record includes influential papers on interior-point methods, dynamic algebraic data structures, and near-linear-time combinatorial algorithms, published in leading theory outlets and attracting substantial attention from the algorithms community. Van den Brand’s work is recognized for blending rigorous worst-case guarantees with practically motivated algorithmic primitives, and for producing deterministic and randomized breakthroughs that have reshaped the landscape of fast algorithms for linear algebra and combinatorial optimization.

## Overview
Will Perkins is a professor at the **Georgia Institute of Technology**, where he leads an active research group at the intersection of **mathematics**, **theoretical computer science**, and *statistical physics*. His work focuses on rigorous analysis of probabilistic combinatorics and random structures, with particular emphasis on the mathematical foundations of <u>information-theoretic and algorithmic thresholds</u>, <u>approximate counting and sampling</u>, and the geometry of high-dimensional solution spaces. Perkins' research program blends combinatorial methods, probabilistic techniques, and ideas from the physics-inspired cavity and replica methods to understand when inference and optimization problems on random graphs and constraint systems are tractable or computationally hard.

---

## Research Areas
Perkins' research spans several tightly related areas: the theory of random constraint satisfaction problems and planted models (e.g., random k-SAT and planted-SAT ensembles), rigorous validation and application of the physics-derived <u>cavity method</u> and *replica symmetry* predictions, and algorithmic approaches to approximate counting and sampling in spin and hard-core models. He has developed tools for analyzing spectral and information-theoretic thresholds (as in the stochastic block and bipartite block models), and for bridging abstract statistical-physics frameworks (Pirogov–Sinai, polymer models, cluster expansion) with efficient algorithms for sampling and counting on lattices, expanders, and random regular graphs. Other recurring themes in his papers include the study of independent sets and matchings (occupancy fractions, container approaches), low- and high-temperature behavior of Potts and Ising models, spatial mixing and uniqueness of Gibbs measures, and geometric and combinatorial questions such as sphere packings and kissing numbers in high dimensions. Across these topics he combines probabilistic combinatorics, complex-analytic and cluster-expansion techniques, and algorithmic analyses (FPTAS, Markov chains, and perfect sampling).

---

## Notable Works
- "Information-theoretic thresholds from the cavity method" (2018) — established rigorous formulas for mutual information in a broad family of inference problems induced by random graphs, vindicating the non-rigorous *cavity method* from physics and pinpointing <u>information-theoretic thresholds</u> in stochastic block-type models.  
- "On the complexity of random satisfiability problems with planted solutions" (2018) — identified and formalized large *algorithmic gaps* in planted k-SAT models, characterizing regimes where planted assignments are information-theoretically recoverable but algorithmically elusive.  
- "Algorithmic Pirogov–Sinai theory" (2020) — developed an efficient, general algorithmic framework using contour representations from Pirogov–Sinai theory to obtain approximate counting and sampling algorithms in the low-temperature regime for a broad class of lattice spin systems.

---

## Academic Background
Perkins' publication record and citation impact (over **2,100** citations) reflect a sustained research program at the confluence of probability, combinatorics, and theoretical computer science. He holds a faculty appointment at Georgia Tech and collaborates widely with researchers in mathematics, computer science, and statistical physics. His body of work includes influential contributions to rigorous probability and algorithm design for statistical-mechanical models, random graphs, and constraint satisfaction problems, and has led to algorithmic advances (FPTAS and efficient samplers), new analytic tools for Gibbs measures, and resolution of conjectures and thresholds predicted by physics. Perkins' work is supported by a combination of interdisciplinary grants and collaborations, and he is active in mentoring students and postdocs, organizing programs and workshops that bridge the mathematical and computational aspects of random structures and statistical mechanics.

## Overview
Jon Duke is a researcher based at **Georgia Tech**, working at the intersection of **clinical informatics**, **data science**, and *computational methods for healthcare*. He is an active contributor to the international Observational Health Data Sciences and Informatics (**OHDSI**) community and collaborates extensively with academic medical centers and research networks. His work centers on developing methods and tools that enable scalable, reproducible observational research from routine clinical data, with particular emphasis on <u>electronic health records (EHR) interoperability and transformation</u>, <u>privacy-preserving synthetic data</u>, and <u>explainable clinical prediction</u>.

---

## Research Areas
Duke’s publications span methodological and applied topics in health data science. He has helped articulate and operationalize the OHDSI vision for large-scale observational research (“Observational Health Data Sciences and Informatics (OHDSI): opportunities for observational researchers”, 2015), worked on transformation and quality assurance of real-world data into a common data model (e.g., OMOP/ CDM work on CDM transformation and data-quality tooling such as Achilles Heel), and contributed to large-scale comparative effectiveness and safety studies of antihypertensive and other drug classes. On the methodological side he has advanced *synthetic EHR generation* using deep generative models (GANs and conditional VAEs; “Generating multi-label discrete patient records using generative adversarial networks”, 2017; “EVA: Generating longitudinal electronic health records using conditional variational autoencoders”, 2021), and developed approaches for *explainable clinical NLP* to predict medical codes from clinical text (“Explainable prediction of medical codes from clinical text”, 2018). He has also published on clinical decision support and human-centered visualization for ICU and medication-review tasks, drug–drug interaction discovery and evaluation, and the integration of interoperability standards (e.g., FHIR) into public health reporting pipelines.

---

## Notable Works
- “Observational Health Data Sciences and Informatics (OHDSI): opportunities for observational researchers” (2015) — a foundational, highly cited piece that helped define a global collaborative ecosystem and set priorities for large-scale, reproducible observational analyses across standardized clinical data sources.  
- “Generating multi-label discrete patient records using generative adversarial networks” (2017) — introduced and validated GAN-based approaches for producing synthetic EHRs to enable data sharing while mitigating privacy risk, influencing subsequent work on privacy-preserving clinical data generation.  
- “Explainable prediction of medical codes from clinical text” (2018) — advanced methods for mapping free-text clinical notes to structured medical codes with interpretability, addressing both automation of coding and the need for transparent model outputs in clinical settings.

---

## Academic Background
Jon Duke is affiliated with **Georgia Tech** and is a prominent collaborator within large multi-institution consortia (notably OHDSI) and clinical informatics centers (frequent co-authorship with groups such as Regenstrief and other academic medical centers). His publication record demonstrates a sustained focus on translational informatics problems that bring together epidemiology, computer science, and clinical practice. With a total citation count on the order of **6,487**, his work is widely cited and has influenced standards and tools for observational research, synthetic data generation, and clinical decision support. Duke’s career emphasizes interdisciplinary partnership, community-oriented open-source tool development, and methodological rigor in deploying data-driven solutions for real-world healthcare problems.

## Overview
Dana Randall is a Professor of Computer Science at the **Georgia Institute of Technology**, leading a research program at the intersection of **algorithms** and **statistical physics-inspired computation**. Her group focuses on rigorous analysis and design of randomized algorithms, with particular emphasis on <u>Markov chain</u> methods, <u>mixing time</u> analysis, and distributed stochastic processes for *self-organizing systems*. Randall’s work spans theory and applications: from foundational results in online algorithms and sampling to algorithmic models for *programmable matter* and *colloidal robotics*, she blends probabilistic techniques, combinatorial constructions, and complexity-aware algorithm design.

---

## Research Areas
Randall’s research develops and applies probabilistic and combinatorial methods for algorithmic sampling, counting, and dynamics. A central theme is the mathematical analysis of <u>Markov chains</u> — proving rapid mixing or demonstrating torpid mixing via spectral, coupling, and decomposition techniques (see e.g., “Markov chain decomposition for convergence rate analysis” (2002) and “Analyzing Glauber dynamics by comparison of Markov chains” (2000)). She has produced deep results on lattice models and tilings (“Markov chain algorithms for planar lattice structures” (1995, 2001)), and on classical statistical-mechanics models such as the Ising and hard-core models (e.g., “Sampling spin configurations of an Ising system” (1999); “Phase Coexistence and Slow Mixing for the Hard-Core Model on ℤ2” (2013)). Her interests also include randomized online algorithms and competitive analysis (“Dynamic TCP acknowledgement and other stories about e/(e-1)” (2001)), as well as emerging algorithmic models for programmable matter and robotic systems studied via stochastic local rules (“A Markov chain algorithm for compression in self-organizing particle systems” (2016); “Colloidal robotics” (2023)). Across these topics she emphasizes rigorous bounds on convergence, algorithmic implementability, and connections between discrete probability and computational practice.

## Notable Works
- “Dynamic TCP acknowledgement and other stories about e/(e-1)” (2001) — Introduced optimal randomized online algorithms for the TCP acknowledgement and related problems, extending and refining the classical ski-rental framework and yielding tight competitive guarantees for a family of online decision problems.  
- “Markov chain decomposition for convergence rate analysis” (2002) — Developed a broadly applicable toolkit for decomposing reversible Markov chains to obtain spectral gap and mixing time bounds, enabling analysis of complex chains by reducing them to easier components.  
- “Markov chain algorithms for planar lattice structures” (1995/2001) — Established algorithmic approaches and mixing-time analyses for sampling domino tilings and related planar lattice configurations, linking combinatorial tiling problems with efficient Monte Carlo sampling.

## Academic Background
Dana Randall has a long and highly cited career in **theoretical computer science** and algorithms, reflected in a publication record spanning foundational algorithmic theory to multidisciplinary work on programmable matter and robotics (total citations ~2667). She is a senior faculty member at the Georgia Institute of Technology, where she directs research at the interface of algorithms, probability, and physical models. Over decades of scholarship she has supervised students and postdocs, contributed to editorial and program-committee leadership, and obtained competitive research support to pursue both foundational questions (mixing times, sampling, counting) and novel applications (self-organizing particle systems, colloidal robotics). Her sustained influence is visible in widely used techniques for analyzing Markov chains, influential surveys synthesizing mixing-time results, and a stream of collaborative work connecting computer science to statistical physics and robotics.

## Overview
Nabil Imam is a researcher affiliated with the **Georgia Institute of Technology** and **Cornell University**, whose work sits at the intersection of **machine learning** and **systems neuroscience**. He is best known for contributions to the engineering and algorithmic foundations of *neuromorphic computing*—designing brain-inspired hardware and algorithms that enable <u>energy-efficient, event-driven computation</u> with spiking neural networks. His body of work spans large-scale neurosynaptic chips, manycore neuromorphic processors with on-chip learning, and biologically inspired algorithms for rapid online learning and sensory processing. Across these efforts he emphasizes scalable communication architectures, low-power VLSI implementations, and the translation of neurobiological motifs (e.g., olfactory bulb circuits, spike-timing dynamics) into practical computing substrates.

---

## Research Areas
Imam’s research covers a blend of hardware, algorithms, and neuroscience-inspired machine learning. On the hardware side he has worked on the design and tool flows for large-scale neurosynaptic chips and digital neurosynaptic cores, addressing challenges such as hierarchical connectivity, scalable on-chip routing, and event-driven/asynchronous circuit implementations. On the algorithmic and systems-neuroscience side he has explored spiking neural network models, spike-timing-dependent plasticity (STDP) instantiations for network traversal and learning, and biomimetic circuits for olfactory processing that enable *rapid online learning and robust recall*. He also investigates system-level tradeoffs—throughput, latency, and energy efficiency—in implementations such as manycore neuromorphic processors and million-neuron substrates, and has extended these ideas to tasks like nearest-neighbor search and active sensing with predictive coding and uncertainty minimization. Collectively his papers connect VLSI circuit techniques, communication and memory-efficient routing methods, and neuro-inspired algorithms to realize <u>scalable, low-power neural computation</u>.

---

## Notable Works
- "A million spiking-neuron integrated circuit with a scalable communication network and interface" (2014) — demonstration of a billion-plus-transistor neurosynaptic chip architecture that established scalable communication and integration strategies for large spiking networks (≈5,126 citations).  
- "Loihi: A neuromorphic manycore processor with on-chip learning" (2018) — coauthored design and functional specification of a 14 nm manycore neuromorphic processor that integrates hierarchical connectivity, dendritic compartments, synaptic delays, and on-chip learning mechanisms for SNNs (≈5,063 citations).  
- "Truenorth: Design and tool flow of a 65 mw 1 million neuron programmable neurosynaptic chip" (2015) — engineering and tool-chain exposition behind a low-power, programmable million-neuron neurosynaptic system, addressing real-time, low-energy cognitive computing constraints (≈2,273 citations).

---

## Academic Background
Imam’s publication record indicates deep, long-standing engagement with interdisciplinary teams that bring together VLSI design, computer architecture, and computational neuroscience; his work on flagship neuromorphic projects and foundational neurosynaptic cores has accumulated over **14,000 citations** across papers. He is affiliated with both **Georgia Tech** and **Cornell**, and has collaborated extensively with large-scale industry and academic neuromorphic teams (as evidenced by coauthorship on major Loihi and TrueNorth publications). His trajectory reflects training and practice across electrical engineering, computer science, and systems neuroscience; he has contributed to high-impact chip design, toolflow development, and biologically inspired learning algorithms, and is recognized in the neuromorphic community for bridging hardware implementation and neural computation.

## Overview
Hyesoon Kim is a **Professor** in the School of Computer Science at **Georgia Tech**, where she leads a research group that spans *computer architecture*, *compilers*, and systems research. Her work centers on modeling, analyzing, and optimizing modern high-performance and heterogeneous computing platforms with a practical emphasis on GPUs, CPUs+GPUs, and memory-centric accelerators. Her group develops tools and techniques that expose and mitigate performance and energy bottlenecks by combining principled analytical models, compiler support, and system-level mechanisms to address <u>memory-level parallelism</u>, <u>thread-level parallelism</u>, and <u>processing-in-memory</u> challenges.

---

## Research Areas
Hyesoon Kim's research covers a broad spectrum in **computer architecture** and **compilers**, with particular depth in GPU/system performance modeling, heterogeneous-multicore mapping, memory-system management, and accelerator-aware compilation. Her highly-cited work on GPU performance (e.g., the 2009 analytical GPU model and 2010 integrated GPU power/performance model) provides a foundation for understanding how <u>memory-level and thread-level parallelism</u> interact on many-core devices. She has advanced techniques for scheduling and mapping on *heterogeneous multiprocessors* (notably Qilin), strategies for cache and prefetch management across many threads (multiple prefetching and TLP-aware cache policies), and practical simulation infrastructure (MacSim) for evaluating CPU–GPU systems. More recently, her group has driven research in security (SGX branch-shadowing side channels), near-memory and processing-in-memory (PIM) offloading with compiler assistance (Cairo) and hardware/software co-design for graph analytics (GraphPIM, GraphBIG), as well as studies on deploying deep neural networks at the edge and minimizing the costs of unified GPU memory and large-scale graph traversal. Across these topics she blends *analytic modeling*, *profiling-driven optimization*, and *compiler/runtime support* to produce reproducible tools and impactful system insights.

---

## Notable Works
- "An analytical model for a GPU architecture with memory-level and thread-level parallelism awareness" (2009) — a foundational analytical framework that quantifies how <u>MLP</u> and <u>TLP</u> shape GPU performance and guides both architecture and software optimizations for many-core GPUs.  
- "Inferring fine-grained control flow inside {SGX} enclaves with branch shadowing" (2017) — a high-impact security study that demonstrates a practical microarchitectural side channel against Intel SGX, influencing subsequent work on enclave defenses and microarchitectural attack mitigations.  
- "Qilin: exploiting parallelism on heterogeneous multiprocessors with adaptive mapping" (2009) — a seminal systems paper that proposes adaptive mapping strategies to exploit diverse compute units in heterogeneous processors, advancing automated hardware/software co-optimization for energy- and performance-efficient execution.

---

## Academic Background
Hyesoon Kim is a long-standing leader in architecture and compiler research based at **Georgia Tech**, with a publication portfolio that has accrued over 10,000 citations and spans more than a decade of influential contributions. Her research program combines analytic modeling, tool and simulator development (e.g., MacSim), and compiler/runtime mechanisms to address practical problems in performance, power, security, and programmability for modern accelerators. She has mentored numerous Ph.D. students and collaborators, produced multiple widely used artifacts and evaluation frameworks, and regularly publishes in top-tier venues in architecture, systems, and programming languages. Her work has attracted support and collaboration from both academic and industry partners, and she is recognized as a leader shaping how GPU and heterogeneous systems are analyzed, programmed, and secured.

## Overview
Alexey Tumanov is an **Associate Professor** at the **Georgia Institute of Technology**, where he leads a research group focused on building practical, high-performance systems for machine learning. His lab sits at the intersection of **distributed systems** and **machine learning systems**, concentrating on designing infrastructure that enables *soft real-time ML* and scalable <u>LLM inference</u>. Tumanov's work spans end-to-end pipeline orchestration, adaptive scheduling, and inference-serving optimizations that prioritize both *latency SLOs* and *high utilization* across heterogeneous hardware.

---

## Research Areas
Tumanov's research addresses core challenges in deploying and operating ML at scale. He has contributed foundational work on distributed frameworks for AI (e.g., the design principles behind **Ray**) and on characterizing real-world cloud heterogeneity through large-scale trace analysis, which informed scheduler design for mixed workloads. He has advanced the state of the art in *serverless ML* and stateful FaaS (e.g., **Cloudburst**, critiques and extensions of first-generation serverless platforms), and developed systems for latency-aware ML serving and pipeline composition (e.g., **Inferline**). More recently his focus has shifted toward the unique systems problems of large language models — including end-to-end inference evaluation (**Etalon**), simulation for LLM inference (**Vidur**), and practical throughput–latency tradeoffs in decoding (**Sarathi-Serve**, work on KV cache compression and long-context serving like **Medha** and **RocketKV**). Across these topics he emphasizes heterogeneous-resource scheduling (e.g., **TetriSched**, **Proteus**, **3sigma**) and techniques for improving GPU utilization and tail-latency guarantees in production ML services.

---

## Notable Works
- Ray: A distributed framework for emerging AI applications (2018) — a widely-cited systems framework that shaped how modern interactive and reinforcement-learning applications are built and deployed.
- Heterogeneity and dynamicity of clouds at scale: Google trace analysis (2012) — a seminal empirical study revealing cluster heterogeneity and workload dynamics that influenced resource management and scheduler research.
- Taming Throughput-Latency tradeoff in LLM inference with Sarathi-Serve (2024) — a focused contribution on decoding-phase optimization for large language models that balances *prefill* and *decode* phases to improve end-to-end latency and GPU efficiency.

---

## Academic Background
Alexey Tumanov has been an active researcher in distributed systems and ML systems for well over a decade, with publication records dating back to the late 2000s and a total citation count exceeding **8,700**. As an Associate Professor at Georgia Tech he combines academic leadership with extensive collaborative work alongside industry and cloud-provider partners, contributing to both open-source frameworks and production-oriented evaluation tools. His portfolio demonstrates sustained impact across topics including cluster scheduling, serverless computing, ML workflow orchestration, and the rapidly evolving landscape of LLM inference. He has mentored students and collaborators through multiple system-building projects and continues to serve the community through conference program roles, workshops, and cross-disciplinary collaborations that translate systems research into deployable ML infrastructure.

## Overview
Melody Moore Jackson is a Professor of Computer Science at **Georgia Tech**, where she leads research at the intersection of **human-centered sensing**, **neural interfaces**, and **animal–computer interaction**. Her lab (often referenced as the *Animal Interaction Lab* in her publications) pursues projects that combine engineering, behavioral science, and design to enable new forms of communication and assistance. Her work spans both invasive and non‑invasive approaches to neural sensing and practical, deployed interaction systems for animals and people, with particular emphasis on <u>steady state visual evoked potentials (SSVEP)</u>, <u>wearable canine interfaces</u>, and <u>non‑invasive EEG methods</u> to support *assistive technology* and *working-animal communication*.

---

## Research Areas
Jackson’s research program bridges two strongly related domains: **Brain–Computer Interfaces (BCIs)** and **Animal–Computer Interaction (ACI)**. In BCIs she has examined fundamental signal paradigms and system design for real-world, assistive use — for example, characterizing and advancing SSVEP‑based control (<u>steady state visual evoked potentials</u>) and proposing frameworks to evaluate and compare diverse brain‑interface studies. Her BCI work addresses practical barriers such as gaze dependence, artifact sensitivity, and the translation of lab prototypes into *pervasive* and usable assistive systems. In ACI she has developed a broad portfolio of empirical, design, and systems work to enable bidirectional communication between working dogs and handlers: from wearable, snout‑ and collar‑sensed interfaces and haptic channels to touchscreen prototypes and instrumented toys that quantify behavior for suitability assessment. Across both domains she emphasizes rigorous evaluation methods, user‑centered design, and solutions that scale from controlled studies to operational settings (e.g., search‑and‑rescue and medical‑alert contexts).

## Notable Works
- "Towards an independent brain–computer interface using steady state visual evoked potentials" (2008) — A foundational study that advanced understanding of SSVEP BCIs for users with severe motor impairments, investigating dependence on gaze and proposing strategies toward *independent* BCI operation.
- "FIDO — facilitating interactions for dogs with occupations: wearable dog‑activated interfaces" (2013/2015) — A multi‑paper project that defined design principles and prototype wearables enabling working dogs to activate devices and communicate intent, synthesizing wearable hardware, training methods, and deployment considerations.
- "Applications for brain‑computer interfaces" (2010) — A comprehensive survey and applied perspective on BCI applications highlighting barriers (speed, error, artifact) and charting pathways to translate BCI research into assistive and pervasive technologies.

## Academic Background
Professor Jackson’s scholarly footprint spans two decades of interdisciplinary work integrating computing, neuroscience, and design. Based at **Georgia Tech** as a faculty member in Computer Science, she has built a research group that routinely collaborates with neuroscientists, rehabilitation specialists, animal behaviorists, and practitioners of working‑dog programs. Her publication record (total citations ≈ **1707**) includes highly cited methodological contributions, systems papers, and applied evaluations that have helped define evaluation frameworks for brain‑interface research and establish new experimental paradigms in animal‑centered computing. Her research has been supported and recognized across the HCI, BCI, and ACI communities through competitive funding and influential conference and journal presentations; she is also known for mentoring students and practitioners who translate her lab’s prototypes into operational tools for assistive and working‑animal contexts.

## Overview
Peter Swire is the **Huang Professor of Law and Ethics** at the Georgia Institute of Technology, where he pursues *interdisciplinary* scholarship at the intersection of **law, policy, and computer science**. His work centers on practical and theoretical issues in **privacy** and **cybersecurity**, with a sustained focus on cross-border data governance, surveillance law, and the regulatory design of information systems. At Georgia Tech he participates in collaborative research and teaching that bring legal analysis together with engineering and policy methods to address <u>data flows</u>, <u>lawful access</u>, and <u>privacy-protective security</u> mechanisms.

---

## Research Areas
Swire’s research spans a set of tightly connected topics that bridge doctrinal legal scholarship and technical/policy analysis. He has written foundational work on international <u>data flows</u> and the European privacy regime (see “None of your business: world data flows, electronic commerce, and the European privacy directive”), and on the economics and design of privacy rules such as the <u>right to data portability</u> (“Why the right to data portability likely reduces consumer welfare”). Swire is also a leading voice on surveillance and intelligence law, including analyses of <u>FISA</u> and post‑9/11 information sharing (“The system of foreign intelligence surveillance law”; “Privacy and information sharing in the war on terrorism”), and on how encryption and changing technologies shift government access to data (“Encryption and globalization”; “From real‑time intercepts to stored records”). His collaborations with engineers—on topics such as automated text mining, ambiguity in regulatory requirements, and legal cross‑reference taxonomies—demonstrate a recurring emphasis on making laws actionable in software systems and on integrating **privacy-by-design** principles into technical development.

---

## Notable Works
- "None of your business: world data flows, electronic commerce, and the European privacy directive" — a seminal analysis of how the EU’s data‑protection rules affect cross‑border transfers and the governance of international electronic commerce, shaping debates about adequacy, safe harbors, and regulatory fragmentation.
- "Why the right to data portability likely reduces consumer welfare: antitrust and privacy critique" — a critical economic and legal examination of proposed portability rights that highlights tradeoffs among **competition**, **privacy**, and **consumer welfare**.
- "The system of foreign intelligence surveillance law" — a comprehensive account of U.S. surveillance law (FISA) that situates statutory frameworks, constitutional doctrine, and executive practices in debates over liberty, security, and oversight.

---

## Academic Background
Swire’s publication record stretches back to the mid‑1980s and reflects sustained engagement with both legal doctrine and technical policy questions; he has accumulated over **4,100 citations** across work on privacy, cybersecurity, surveillance, and regulatory design. Before his appointment at Georgia Tech he served on the faculty at the Moritz College of Law (noted in his authorship as the C. William O’Neill Professor of Law), and he has been an active participant in national policy processes—most prominently as an author of the President’s Review Group report on intelligence and communications technologies. His scholarship has appeared in leading law reviews and interdisciplinary venues, and he routinely collaborates with computer scientists and engineers on methods for translating legal requirements into technical specifications (e.g., work on automated text mining, regulatory ambiguity, and compliance taxonomies).

## Overview
Alexandra Boldyreva is a **Professor** in the **School of Cybersecurity and Privacy** at **Georgia Institute of Technology**, where she leads a research group focused on *applied cryptography* and *privacy-preserving systems*. Her work spans both theoretical foundations and practical constructions for secure data management and communication, with particular emphasis on <u>order-preserving and deterministic encryption</u>, <u>searchable and searchable-encryption schemes</u>, and <u>threshold / multisignature primitives</u>. She combines rigorous *provable-security* analyses with attention to real-world deployment challenges in databases, network protocols, and outsourced/cloud settings.

---

## Research Areas
Boldyreva’s research covers a broad range of topics in **cryptography** and **information security**, bridging symmetric- and public-key techniques with systems-level concerns. She helped found the cryptographic study of *order-preserving symmetric encryption* (OPE) and its security limitations and alternatives (e.g., “Order-preserving symmetric encryption”, Eurocrypt 2009; “Order-preserving encryption revisited”, 2011). She has developed foundational treatments of *deterministic and efficiently searchable* public-key and symmetric encryption that enable sub-linear search over encrypted data while providing strong privacy definitions (e.g., “Deterministic and efficiently searchable encryption”, 2007; related works on deterministic encryption security notions, 2006–2008). In the public-key domain she has contributed efficient constructions for identity-based encryption with revocation and for threshold/multisignature and blind signature schemes (e.g., “Threshold signatures, multisignatures and blind signatures…”, 2002; “Identity-based encryption with efficient revocation”, 2008). Her work also extends to applied provable security for network and authentication protocols (analyses of QUIC, TLS 1.3, FIDO2), secure outsourced databases and searchable/fuzzy search on encrypted data, multi-recipient encryption and randomness reuse, and nuanced models for implementation-level issues such as decryption failures and ciphertext fragmentation.

---

## Notable Works
- “Threshold signatures, multisignatures and blind signatures based on the gap-Diffie-Hellman-group signature scheme” (2002) — introduced efficient and robust constructions for **threshold** and **multisignature** primitives in GDH groups, influencing multiparty signing and delegation mechanisms used in distributed systems and secure-routing applications.  
- “Order-preserving symmetric encryption” (2009) — initiated the formal cryptographic treatment of **OPE**, defining security limits of order-revealing primitives and motivating alternative designs that balance query efficiency and leakage.  
- “Deterministic and efficiently searchable encryption” (2007) — provided strong privacy definitions and constructions for **deterministic public-key encryption** that enable fast, sub-linear search over encrypted databases while characterizing the achievable security guarantees.

---

## Academic Background
Professor Boldyreva is a long-standing contributor to the cryptography and security research community with a publication record spanning foundational theory and applied analyses since the early 2000s. Now based at Georgia Tech’s School of Cybersecurity and Privacy, she has produced numerous highly-cited papers (total citations ~10,867) and has driven influential lines of work in searchable encryption, OPE, signature schemes, and provable analyses of real-world protocols. Her scholarship is characterized by formal security definitions, practical constructions, and careful investigation of deployment-oriented subtleties (e.g., multi-recipient encryption, ciphertext fragmentation, and decryption-failure distinguishability). She is an active participant in the broader cryptographic community—publishing in top venues, mentoring students and collaborators, and shaping research directions at the intersection of **cryptography** and **information security**.

## Overview
Xiuwei Zhang is a faculty researcher at the **Georgia Institute of Technology** (College of Computing) where she leads a computational biology group focused on algorithmic methods for single‑cell and spatial omics. Her work sits at the intersection of **algorithms** and **computational biology**, with an emphasis on developing quantitative frameworks to disentangle *technical from biological variation*, build realistic <u>simulators of single‑cell data</u>, and design integrative methods for <u>multi‑modal and spatial single‑cell analysis</u>. Zhang’s lab produces open‑source tools and methodological contributions that bridge statistical modeling, machine learning, and systems biology to enable robust downstream biological inference.

---

## Research Areas
Zhang’s research spans several tightly connected areas in single‑cell and computational systems biology. She developed foundational statistical approaches to model and correct for *technical noise* in **single‑cell RNA‑seq** experiments, enabling more reliable measurements of cell‑to‑cell variability. She has led the design of realistic generative simulators (e.g., SymSim, scMultiSim, VeloSim, TedSim) that explicitly model sequencing processes, RNA velocity, lineage barcodes, and cell–cell interactions to benchmark analysis pipelines. A major thread of her work is on <u>data integration</u> across modalities and batches — including methods for integrating unmatched scRNA‑seq and scATAC‑seq (scDART) and mosaic multi‑modal integration (scMoMaT) — often using deep learning and algorithmic unrolling approaches (GRNUlar, Grnular). Zhang also contributes to inference of <u>gene regulatory networks</u>, trajectory reconstruction using RNA velocity, and refinement of regulatory and cell–cell interaction models from spatially resolved transcriptomics (CLARIFY, TemSOMap). Earlier work applies phylogenetic and evolutionary network models to improve regulatory network inference, reflecting a long‑standing interest in algorithmic formulations of biological network evolution.

---

## Notable Works
- "Accounting for technical noise in single-cell RNA-seq experiments" (2013) — Introduced a quantitative statistical framework to separate *technical noise* from true biological variability in single‑cell RNA‑seq, a methodological advance that underpins more accurate detection of cell state heterogeneity and has been widely cited and adopted.
- "Simulating multiple faceted variability in single cell RNA sequencing" (2019, SymSim) — Delivered a comprehensive simulator that models the generative processes behind observed single‑cell data (biological variability, capture/sequencing biases, and technical noise), providing a standard benchmarking platform for method development and evaluation.
- "scDART: integrating unmatched scRNA-seq and scATAC-seq data and learning cross-modality relationship simultaneously" (2022) — Proposed a principled deep‑learning approach to integrate unmatched single‑cell transcriptomic and chromatin‑accessibility datasets while learning cross‑modality relationships, addressing a key challenge in multi‑omic single‑cell studies.

---

## Academic Background
Zhang’s publication record reflects a trajectory from algorithmic and evolutionary models of biological networks to a focused program in single‑cell and spatial omics methodology. Affiliated with the College of Computing at Georgia Tech, she combines training in algorithm design and statistical modeling to tackle problems in high‑throughput biology. Her work is highly interdisciplinary — collaborating with immunology and experimental groups (e.g., studies on T helper cells and CD4+/CD8+ cytotoxic responses) and contributing both theoretical advances and widely used software. With a total citation count of 2,496, Zhang is recognized in the computational biology community for producing rigorous, reproducible tools and for mentoring students and collaborators in the development of open‑source methods. She maintains an active lab homepage (https://www.cc.gatech.edu/~xzhang954/) describing ongoing projects in simulation, integration, network inference, and spatial single‑cell analysis.
