## Overview
Annie I. Antón is **Chair and Professor** in the **School of Interactive Computing** at the *Georgia Institute of Technology*, where she leads research efforts that sit at the intersection of **software engineering**, **requirements engineering**, and *privacy/security for socio-technical systems*. Her work emphasizes the systematic alignment of software requirements with stakeholder goals and external constraints, and she is best known for pioneering goal-oriented approaches to requirements elicitation and for translating legal and policy texts into actionable system requirements. Key themes in her research include <u>goal-based requirements analysis</u>, <u>privacy and regulatory compliance</u>, and <u>automated extraction of rights and obligations</u>.

---

## Research Areas
Antón’s research spans requirements engineering, privacy, security, and regulatory compliance, with a consistent focus on methods that bridge informal stakeholder intentions and formal system specifications. Early foundational work developed goal- and inquiry-based techniques for identifying and refining requirements (e.g., "Goal-based requirements analysis", 1996; "Inquiry-based requirements analysis", 1994; "Goal identification and refinement..." dissertation, 1997), including goal decomposition and scenario analysis for evolving or reengineered systems. Building on these foundations, she has extensively investigated how legal and policy documents should influence software requirements: extracting rights, permissions, and obligations from regulations ("Towards regulatory compliance: Extracting rights and obligations...", 2006), automating rule extraction and text-mining for policy documents ("Automating the extraction of rights and obligations...", 2008; "Automated text mining for requirements analysis of policy documents", 2013), and characterizing ambiguity and cross-references in legal texts ("Identifying and classifying ambiguity for regulatory requirements", 2014; "A legal cross-references taxonomy...", 2011/2012). Antón has applied these methods to concrete privacy problems—analyzing website and financial privacy policies, formalizing P3P semantics, and constructing privacy goal taxonomies ("Analyzing website privacy requirements using a privacy goal taxonomy", 2002; "Financial privacy policies and the need for standardization", 2004; "A formal semantics for P3P", 2004). Her work also addresses access control and policy-aligned specifications (e.g., RECAPS, "Requirements-based access control analysis and policy specification", 2009), misuse/abuse cases, and user-centered studies of privacy perceptions and authentication, demonstrating a blend of formal methods, empirical studies, and tool-focused automation.

---

## Notable Works
- Goal-based foundations for requirements engineering: "Goal-based requirements analysis" (1996) established goal reasoning as a systematic mechanism for identifying, organizing, and justifying software requirements and has influenced numerous goal-oriented methods and tools.
- Inquiry- and scenario-driven elicitation methods: "Inquiry-based requirements analysis" (1994) and related scenario work provided structured conversational and scenario-management models that shaped early practice for eliciting and validating requirements in ambiguous contexts.
- Legal and policy alignment for privacy and security: "Analyzing regulatory rules for privacy and security requirements" (2008) and "Towards regulatory compliance: Extracting rights and obligations to align requirements with regulations" (2006) advanced techniques for deriving compliant system requirements from regulations and automating the extraction of obligations and permissions from legal texts.

---

## Academic Background
Antón’s scholarship builds on a strong foundation in requirements engineering: her 1997 dissertation introduced the Goal-Based Requirements Analysis Method (GBRAM), which evolved from extensive application to real-world systems and processes. As Chair and Professor in Georgia Tech’s School of Interactive Computing, she has led research programs that connect academic advances to practical concerns in healthcare, finance, e-commerce, and public policy. Her prolific publication record—reflected in nearly 10,000 citations—spans highly cited methodological contributions, empirical studies of privacy perceptions and policy comprehension, and practical frameworks and tools for legal-compliance engineering. Throughout her career she has combined theoretical formalisms, empirical user- and policy-focused studies, and automation techniques (text mining, production rules, semantic modeling) to shepherd requirements engineering toward greater rigor, accountability, and societal relevance.

## Overview
Danfei Xu is an **Assistant Professor** in the School of Interactive Computing at the **Georgia Institute of Technology**, where he leads a research group focused on the intersection of **robot learning** and **computer vision**. His work develops integrated perception–action systems that enable robots to reason about 3D structure, object pose, and long-horizon manipulation from multi-modal data. Danfei’s research emphasizes <u>generalization across embodiments and tasks</u>, building scalable datasets and models for imitation and planning, and fusing visual, depth, and tactile sensing to support robust manipulation in real-world environments. His portfolio spans foundational contributions in **3D reconstruction**, **6D pose estimation**, and **learning from demonstrations**, as well as applied systems that leverage large datasets and language models for robot task planning. His work is highly cited (total citations: **15,157**) and bridges theory, datasets, and deployed robotic capabilities.

---

## Research Areas
Danfei Xu’s research sits at the confluence of *robot learning* and *computer vision*, with detailed contributions across several interlocking areas. He has advanced methods for <u>3D scene and object reconstruction</u> (e.g., learning-based single- and multi-view reconstruction) and dense multi-sensor fusion for accurate 6D pose and 3D bounding estimation, as exemplified by work on **3D-R2N2** and **DenseFusion**. In robot learning and imitation, he studies how to scale learning from human demonstrations and play data to achieve long-horizon manipulation — developing methods for *one-shot* and long-horizon generalization (Neural Task Programming, Neural Task Graphs, and subsequent imitation-learning studies). Danfei also focuses on building and curating large, diverse datasets and models to improve cross-embodiment transfer and generalization (e.g., Open X-Embodiment, EgoMimic), and on integrating language and large models for situated task planning (ProgPrompt / Progprompt). Additional strands include tactile perception for contact-rich manipulation, optimization- and learning-based task & motion planning, and generative planning techniques (diffusion-based skill chaining) for long-horizon tasks. Across these topics he repeatedly addresses the core problems of representation learning, sensor fusion, and dataset-driven generalization for robot autonomy.

---

## Notable Works
- "3D-R2N2: A unified approach for single and multi-view 3D object reconstruction" (2016) — introduced a recurrent neural architecture that learns shape priors to reconstruct 3D objects from single or multiple images, helping to establish learning-based 3D reconstruction as a practical approach for downstream robotic perception tasks.
- "DenseFusion: 6D object pose estimation by iterative dense fusion" (2019) — proposed an iterative dense fusion of RGB and depth features for robust, real-time 6D pose estimation in cluttered scenes, substantially improving pose accuracy by tightly integrating image and point-cloud information.
- "Open X-Embodiment: Robotic learning datasets and RT-X models" (2023) — curated a large, multi-institutional dataset and advocated for RT-X models that enable cross-robot generalization, advancing the community’s ability to train high-capacity models that transfer across diverse robot embodiments and environments.

---

## Academic Background
Danfei Xu’s publication record spans top venues in robotics and computer vision (CVPR, ICRA, NeurIPS and related conferences), reflecting a career trajectory from early work on tactile exploration and deformable-object manipulation to leading contributions in 3D perception, pose estimation, imitation learning, and dataset-driven robot learning. Over the past decade he has collaborated broadly with research groups across computer vision, robotics, and autonomous driving, producing influential papers on sensor fusion (PointFusion), scene understanding (scene graph generation), and scalable imitation strategies (MimicPlay, EgoMimic). As an Assistant Professor at Georgia Tech’s School of Interactive Computing he maintains an active lab, contributes to open datasets and tools, and continues to publish high-impact work that shapes both theoretical methods and practical robot systems. For more information and publications, see his homepage: https://faculty.cc.gatech.edu/~danfei/.

## Overview
Paul Pearce is an **Assistant Professor** at **Georgia Tech**, where he leads a research group working at the intersection of *computer security*, *network measurement*, and *cyber-crime analysis*. His work centers on the design and deployment of scalable measurement systems and defenses that increase transparency and accountability on the Internet. Paul’s research program emphasizes <u>large-scale measurement</u> of network behavior and manipulation (including censorship and DNS interference), the economics and mechanics of online advertising abuse, and the detection of malicious software and abusive accounts. His homepage is http://pearce.io/.

---

## Research Areas
Paul’s research spans rigorous empirical measurement, system design, and applied security. He has produced extensive work on mobile platform security and privacy (for example, *AdDroid: Privilege Separation for Applications and Advertisers in Android*), on web and network-based revenue fraud and manipulation (e.g., studies of *ad injection* and large-scale *click-fraud* such as the ZeroAccess analyses), and on Internet-scale censorship and connectivity measurement (including multiple global studies of <u>DNS manipulation</u> and the development of systems for *continual censorship measurement* and detection of connectivity disruptions like *Augur*). More recent work expands into Internet-wide scanning and IPv6 measurement (tools and methodology such as *ZDNS* and studies of IPv6 scanning and alias detection), and into applied machine learning for abusive account detection on social platforms. Across these areas he emphasizes reproducible tooling, rigorous methodology, and bridging measurement results to practical mitigation strategies.

---

## Notable Works
- AdDroid: Privilege Separation for Applications and Advertisers in Android (2012) — a high-impact systems/security contribution that proposed a practical privilege-separation architecture to mitigate privacy and integrity risks introduced by third‑party advertising libraries in Android applications.
- Global Measurement of DNS Manipulation (2017) — a comprehensive Internet-scale study documenting where and how DNS is manipulated for censorship and interference, establishing measurement methodologies used by subsequent censorship research.
- Ad injection at scale: Assessing deceptive advertisement modifications (2015) — a large-scale empirical investigation that exposed widespread ad injection practices, quantified user impact, and informed defenses and industry responses to browser- and network-level ad tampering.

---

## Academic Background
Paul Pearce is an academically trained computer scientist specializing in security and measurement and is currently an **Assistant Professor at Georgia Tech**, where he directs research on the security, privacy, and measurement of Internet systems. Over his career he has published widely in top-tier venues across security and network measurement, accumulating approximately **1,981 total citations**, and has contributed both influential empirical studies and open-source measurement tooling (e.g., ZDNS). His publication record reflects sustained collaboration with the security and measurement communities and a track record of deploying reproducible, high‑impact experiments that inform both scholarship and operational practice. He maintains an active lab and a public homepage (http://pearce.io/) documenting publications, code, and datasets.

## Overview
J. Sukarno Mertoguno is a senior researcher and faculty member at the **Georgia Institute of Technology**, where he leads an *interdisciplinary research group* working at the intersection of **computer architecture**, **cybersecurity**, and **machine learning**. His primary research focuses on the design of architecturally-supported secure processors and instruction-set innovations, advanced techniques for software vulnerability discovery and localization, and resilience and safety for complex <u>cyber-physical systems (CPS)</u>. Mertoguno’s body of work spans hardware inventions and formal/learning-based software analyses, emphasizing <u>architectural support for security</u>, <u>functional-level instruction-set computing</u>, and <u>timing- and physics-informed CPS resilience</u>.

---

## Research Areas
Mertoguno’s research portfolio bridges low-level hardware design and high-level software and systems security. In computer architecture he has developed novel instruction-set concepts and processor designs — for example, the *native lookup instruction* and several functional-level instruction-set computing (FLIC) proposals that accelerate variable-length key lookups and application-layer request processing (see his 2006 and 2007 papers on lookup instructions and FLIC). In secure computing he has authored processor extensions and secure-processor designs (e.g., the SCAN secure processor and related deep-code-scrambling work) that embed authentication and fine-grained observability into the execution substrate. On the software side, his recent work uses graph-based and learning approaches to detect and localize vulnerabilities in source code — notably the **VulChecker** system (2023) that couples program-structure representations with ML to pinpoint vulnerable lines. For cyber-physical systems he has developed both physics-based strategies and timing-aware frameworks (2019–2023) that combine safety constraints with resilience architectures such as BFT++ to provide provable or practically measurable safety and recovery properties. Complementing these streams, Mertoguno has a long history on vision, reverse-engineering, and document-understanding projects (KYDON vision systems, VLSI/layout reverse engineering, and automatic deep understanding of technical documents), demonstrating a recurring theme of combining *domain-specific architecture* with *knowledge-based* and *statistical* methods.

---

## Notable Works
- "Native lookup instruction for file-access processor searching a three-level lookup cache for variable-length keys" (2006) — Introduced an architectural instruction and cache/search microarchitecture that accelerates variable-length key lookups for application-layer services, laying groundwork for processing-oriented ISA extensions and specialized lookup engines.
- "Functional-level instruction-set computer architecture for processing application-layer content-service requests such as file-access requests" (2007) — Proposed the **FLIC** paradigm and processing-engine slices with specialized units (lookup, bit-compare, etc.) to execute higher-level functional instructions, bridging application semantics and microarchitectural support.
- "{VulChecker}: Graph-based vulnerability localization in source code" (2023) — Developed a graph-based ML approach that goes beyond vulnerability classification to localize the exact source-code lines causing vulnerabilities, significantly reducing developer triage effort in secure software development.

---

## Academic Background
Mertoguno’s publication record (spanning the mid-1990s through the 2020s) reflects a long-standing academic career rooted in electrical/computer engineering and computer science. He has contributed both patented inventions and peer-reviewed research across processor microarchitecture, secure hardware, autonomous vision systems, and CPS resilience. His collaborations and project descriptions indicate sustained engagement with defense and systems sponsors (e.g., ONR-funded CPS resilience efforts) and with multi-disciplinary teams combining formal methods, control, and machine learning. Over his career he has supervised students and research teams that produced influential work in instruction-set innovation, reverse engineering, and applied ML for security; he has also served as PI or co-PI on funded programs and holds multiple invention disclosures/patents related to processor and system security. Mertoguno’s scholarship has been recognized through broad citation impact (716 total citations) and continued publication across top venues in architecture, security, and cyber-physical systems.

## Overview
Agata Rozga is a faculty researcher in the **School of Interactive Computing** at the **Georgia Institute of Technology**, where she leads an *interdisciplinary research program* at the intersection of computer science, developmental psychology, and clinical practice. Her lab develops computational methods for understanding and measuring human social and communicative behavior, with a primary focus on applications to **autism spectrum disorder (ASD)** and other neurodevelopmental and cognitive health conditions. Her work emphasizes <u>early detection</u>, <u>behavioral imaging</u>, and <u>automated sensing of gaze and engagement</u> using video, audio, physiological sensors, and machine learning to enable *scalable*, *ecologically valid*, and *clinically useful* assessments.

---

## Research Areas
Rozga’s research spans computational behavior science and applied AI for health. She combines longitudinal and prospective developmental studies (e.g., early emergence of behavioral signs and response-to-name studies) with engineering approaches for automated measurement: computer vision and wearable sensing for eye contact and gaze estimation, multimodal sensor analysis of physiological engagement (electrodermal activity), and algorithms for decoding social behavior from naturalistic interactions (see “Decoding children's social behavior,” 2013; “Using electrodermal activity…,” 2014; “Detecting eye contact using wearable eye-tracking glasses,” 2012). Her work also advances methods for remote and telehealth diagnosis (telehealth diagnostic approaches and home-video systems), time-series and annotation modeling (e.g., VTS-HMM and ground-truth inference for noisy annotators), and the translation of these tools to related domains such as caregiving networks, mild cognitive impairment (MCI), and behavior monitoring for individuals with developmental disabilities.

---

## Notable Works
- A prospective longitudinal analysis demonstrating how early behavioral signs of autism emerge in the first years of life and differentiating infants who later receive an ASD diagnosis (A prospective study of the emergence of early behavioral signs of autism, 2010). This study has served as a foundation for early screening targets.
- The introduction of automated approaches for analyzing young children's social and communicative behavior from multimodal recordings, framing a new problem domain for activity recognition in clinical and developmental contexts (Decoding children's social behavior, 2013).
- Development of joint modeling approaches for estimating visual attention and gaze in naturalistic social scenes—linking person-centered gaze with scene saliency to produce generalized attention estimates applicable across settings (Connecting gaze, scene, and attention: Generalized attention estimation via joint modeling of gaze and scene saliency, 2018).

---

## Academic Background
Rozga’s career is characterized by an interdisciplinary trajectory that bridges developmental psychology, clinical research on autism, and computational sensing and AI. Based at Georgia Tech’s School of Interactive Computing, she collaborates extensively with clinicians and developmental scientists to run prospective and longitudinal infant-sibling studies, clinical assessment projects, and engineering deployments of wearable and video-based systems. Her publications have attracted broad attention (total citations > 5,300) and span high-impact empirical studies, methodological advances in behavioral imaging and annotation modeling, and translational work on telehealth and in-home assessment systems. Her program has been sustained through multi-institutional collaborations and external research support, and she is recognized as a leader in bringing computational methods to bear on pressing clinical questions in autism, developmental disabilities, and aging-related cognitive impairment.

## Overview
Jon R. Lindsay is an **Associate Professor of International Relations** at the **Georgia Institute of Technology**, where he leads a research group focused on the intersection of *security studies*, *technology policy*, and *cyber operations*. His work examines how digital technologies reshape statecraft, military power, and international competition, with particular emphasis on the political logic of <u>cybersecurity</u>, <u>deterrence</u>, and the <u institutional and organizational dynamics</u> that govern how states use and defend information systems. Lindsay’s scholarship mixes theoretical insight with empirical case studies—ranging from the *Stuxnet* operation to state–industry interactions and the strategic implications of emerging technologies like artificial intelligence and quantum computing.

---

## Research Areas
Lindsay’s research sits at the nexus of **international security** and **technology studies**. He systematically studies the strategic consequences of cyber operations (e.g., the limits of cyber warfare demonstrated in “Stuxnet and the limits of cyber warfare”), the challenges of attribution and the prospects for deterrence in cyberspace (“Tipping the scales: the attribution problem and the feasibility of deterrence against cyberattack”), and the offense–defense–deception dynamics that shape online conflict (“Weaving tangled webs: Offense, defense, and deception in cyberspace”). He also explores how states harness information technology to enhance military power and influence geopolitics (“Information technology and military power”; “China and cybersecurity”), the institutional tensions around military cyber organizations and intelligence (“Cyber conflict vs. Cyber Command”), and the strategic implications of emerging technologies—from **artificial intelligence** in decision-making to the political consequences of **quantum computing** for cryptography. His work emphasizes <u>organizational context</u>, <u>information friction</u>, and the often-overlooked role of secrecy and deception in secret statecraft and cyber competition.

---

## Notable Works
- “Stuxnet and the limits of cyber warfare” (2013) — A foundational analysis that situates the Stuxnet operation as an illustrative case showing both the potential and the limits of cyber-physical attacks, challenging alarmist narratives about imminent revolutionary change in warfare.
- “Weaving tangled webs: Offense, defense, and deception in cyberspace” (2015) — A theoretical and empirical critique of the claim that cyberspace is inherently offense-dominant, highlighting the centrality of deception, signaling, and defensive measures in shaping cyber conflicts.
- “Tipping the scales: the attribution problem and the feasibility of deterrence against cyberattack” (2015) — A careful treatment of how attribution difficulties influence the credibility and practicality of deterrence strategies, arguing that observable patterns and political context can still enable deterrence despite uncertainty.

---

## Academic Background
Lindsay is a trained scholar of international relations and technology policy whose publications span leading journals, policy forums, and monographic work (including contributions on military innovation and information technology). He is based at the **Georgia Institute of Technology**, maintains an active public-facing research profile (http://www.jonrlindsay.com/), and his scholarship has been widely cited (total citations ~3,703), signaling strong impact across academic and policy communities. His portfolio reflects sustained engagement with interdisciplinary research teams and policy audiences—producing influential work on cyber strategy, cross-domain deterrence, the political economy of cybersecurity, and the governance implications of AI and quantum technologies. He has received external research support and peer recognition typical of senior scholars working at the intersection of security studies and technology, and he teaches and mentors students in topics related to cybersecurity, deterrence theory, and the politics of emerging technologies.

## Overview
Raphael Pestourie is an **Assistant Professor** in the School of Computational Science and Engineering at **Georgia Tech**, where he leads a research group focused on the intersection of *computational physics*, *machine learning*, and *large-scale numerical optimization*. His work centers on developing scalable, physics-aware computational methods for <u>inverse design</u> and <u>meta-optics</u>, combining advances in **scientific machine learning** (e.g., neural operators and physics-informed networks), **topology and gradient-based optimization**, and fast approximate solvers to enable practical design of devices that span many wavelengths and millions of degrees of freedom. His homepage (http://www.raphaelpestourie.com/) documents a portfolio of theoretical, algorithmic, and experimental contributions that have had broad impact across optics, photonics, and PDE-based modeling.

---

## Research Areas
Pestourie’s research spans several tightly coupled areas: (1) *inverse design and topology optimization* for large-area metasurfaces and meta-optics, demonstrated in works on optimization of devices with 10^5–10^6 degrees of freedom (e.g., "Topology optimization of freeform large-area metasurfaces" (2019) and "Inverse design of large-area metasurfaces" (2018)); (2) *scientific machine learning* for PDEs, including development of deep surrogate and operator-learning approaches that are both data- and compute-efficient (e.g., "Multifidelity deep neural operators..." (2022), "Physics-enhanced deep surrogates for partial differential equations" (2023)); (3) *physics-informed neural networks and constrained learning* for inverse design, notably methods that enforce hard physical constraints to improve fidelity and reliability ("Physics-informed neural networks with hard constraints for inverse design" (2021)); and (4) *end-to-end co-design of optics and computation* for imaging and sensing, where nanophotonic front-ends are co-optimized with computational back-ends for tasks such as multi-channel imaging and extended depth-of-focus imaging ("End-to-end nanophotonic inverse design for imaging and polarimetry" (2021), "Inverse designed metalenses with extended depth of focus" (2020)). Across these areas he emphasizes scalable algorithms (hybrid solvers, locally periodic approximations, perturbative frameworks) and active/multifidelity learning strategies to make large-scale inverse design tractable and experimentally realizable (e.g., "Active learning of deep surrogates for PDEs" (2020), "HiLAB: A Hybrid Inverse‐Design Framework" (2025)).

---

## Notable Works
- Developed physics-aware learning for constrained inverse design with strong practical impact through the highly-cited framework in "Physics-informed neural networks with hard constraints for inverse design" (2021), which integrates hard physical constraints into neural architectures to improve reliability in engineering inverse problems.  
- Pioneered scalable computational frameworks for designing extremely large metasurfaces in "Inverse design of large-area metasurfaces" (2018) and extended these ideas with topology-optimization techniques in "Topology optimization of freeform large-area metasurfaces" (2019), enabling practical optimization of devices spanning hundreds to thousands of wavelengths.  
- Demonstrated application-driven, large-scale inverse design for high-performance meta-optics—including systems for virtual reality and broadband imaging—in "Inverse design enables large-scale high-performance meta-optics reshaping virtual reality" (2022) and related end-to-end imaging works that co-design optics and algorithms ("End-to-end nanophotonic inverse design for imaging and polarimetry" (2021)).

---

Pestourie’s publication record indicates a strong interdisciplinary training at the interface of computational science, optics, and applied mathematics. He holds a faculty appointment at Georgia Tech’s School of Computational Science and Engineering and collaborates broadly with academic and industry partners on photonics, PDE modeling, and machine-learning-driven design; his work has accrued over 3,000 citations, reflecting wide adoption of his methods across the photonics and scientific computing communities. His career has combined algorithm development (fast approximate solvers, hybrid inverse-design frameworks), methodological innovation in scientific ML (neural operators, multifidelity learning, physics-enhanced surrogates), and experimental validation of inverse-designed devices. Pestourie’s contributions have been recognized through invited talks, cross-institutional collaborations, and a steady stream of high-impact publications and translational outputs connecting theory to fabricated meta-optics and imaging systems.

## Overview
Kartik Goyal is a researcher at the **Georgia Institute of Technology**, affiliated with research groups working on **Machine Learning**, **Natural Language Processing**, and broader **Artificial Intelligence** applications. His work centers on the intersection of principled probabilistic modeling and practical NLP systems, with a particular emphasis on <u>privacy and leakage in language models</u>, <u>structured and interpretable linguistic representations</u>, and <u>decoding and controllable text generation</u>. Across his contributions he blends *theoretical analysis* (e.g., energy-based perspectives and continuous relaxations) with *applied systems* (e.g., resources for phonological representation and tools for low-resource languages), producing methods that are both scientifically grounded and useful for downstream tasks.

---

## Research Areas
Kartik’s research spans several interconnected areas of modern NLP and ML. He has made influential contributions to the study of <u>privacy vulnerabilities of masked language models</u>, developing quantitative membership-inference evaluations that probe how MLMs leak training data (see “Quantifying privacy risks of masked language models using membership inference attacks”, 2022). He also works on <u>energy-based views of language models</u> and sampling/decoding methods (e.g., Metropolis–Hastings analyses and continuous relaxations of decoding like beam search), which underpin a line of work on principled generation and model scoring (“Exposing the Implicit Energy Networks…”, 2021; “A continuous relaxation of beam search…”, 2018). Another major thread is linguistically informed representation: Kartik contributed the widely used **Panphon** resource for mapping IPA segments to articulatory feature vectors, demonstrating that phonological feature vectors can improve downstream learning relative to sparse encodings (2016). He has also advanced methods for *controllable and attribute-guided generation* without expensive fine-tuning (“Mix and match: Learning-free controllable text generation using energy language models”, 2022) and for improved credit assignment in sequence models via differentiable scheduled sampling (2017). Complementing these ML-centric topics, his work includes applications in low-resource and historical text processing—named-entity recognition for Sorani Kurdish and Tajik, typographical analysis of early modern printing, and attribution methods for printers—demonstrating a strong cross-disciplinary interest in both computational linguistics and digital humanities.

## Notable Works
- Quantitative analysis of privacy leakage in masked language models — “Quantifying privacy risks of masked language models using membership inference attacks” (2022). This paper provides rigorous membership-inference evaluations that highlight privacy risks when applying MLMs to sensitive domains (legal, medical).
- Phonological feature resource and representation work — “Panphon: A resource for mapping IPA segments to articulatory feature vectors” (2016). Panphon supplies articulatory feature vectors that have become a practical resource for phonology-aware NLP and multilingual modeling.
- Early metaphor detection using structured kernels — “Identifying metaphorical word use with tree kernels” (2013). An early and cited contribution to computational metaphor detection that combines syntactic structure with kernel methods to improve identification of figurative language.

## Academic Background
Kartik Goyal is based at the **Georgia Institute of Technology**, where he contributes to ML and NLP research and maintains an active publication profile (homepage: https://kartikgo.github.io/). His publication record dates from the early 2010s through the mid-2020s and totals roughly **975 citations**, reflecting sustained impact across methodological and applied topics. His early publications indicate a foundation in computational linguistics and structured prediction (metaphor detection, distributional semantics), while later work demonstrates advanced expertise in probabilistic modeling, decoding algorithms, and privacy analyses for modern language models. Kartik’s portfolio shows collaborations that bridge core ML/NLP labs and cross-disciplinary projects in digital humanities and low-resource language technology, suggesting a researcher who combines rigorous modeling skills with practical, domain-aware applications.

## Overview
Carrie Bruce is a Principal Research Scientist at the **Georgia Institute of Technology**, where she leads and contributes to interdisciplinary accessibility and rehabilitation research within Georgia Tech’s human-centered computing and inclusive design efforts, including work associated with the *Accessible Aquarium Project* and collaborations with speech‑language pathology and rehabilitation engineering teams. Her work centers on the design and evaluation of technologies and environments that support participation for people with disabilities, with particular emphasis on <u>assistive technology</u>, <u>inclusive design</u>, and <u>workplace accommodations</u>. She combines empirical assessment, design interventions, and applied deployment to improve accessibility across *informal learning environments*, *communication contexts*, and *built and digital environments*.

---

## Research Areas
Bruce’s research spans Human–Computer Interaction (HCI), rehabilitation and assistive technologies, and universal/inclusive design for both physical and digital spaces. She has contributed to the development and application of the International Classification of Functioning, Disability and Health (ICF) for assessment ("Rehabilitation and health assessment: applying ICF guidelines") and to frameworks that treat the physical environment as an independent factor in performance ("The physical environment as an independent measure"). A sustained strand of her work explores non‑visual, sound‑based exhibit interpretation and interactive sonification for accessibility in informal learning environments (e.g., "Aquarium sonification: Soundscapes for accessible dynamic informal learning environments", "Designing interactive sonification for live aquarium exhibits", "Aquarium fugue"), producing design guidance and evaluation methods for dynamic, real‑time exhibit mediation. Another prominent line addresses communication and participation at work and in care settings: corpus development and discourse analysis for Augmentative and Alternative Communication (AAC) users ("The AAC and non‑AAC workplace corpus (ANAWC)", "Linguistic characteristics of AAC discourse in the workplace"), and development/evaluation of environmental and communication assessment tools for dementia care ("Development and evaluation of the environment and communication assessment toolkit with speech‑language pathologists", "Evaluating and modifying the communication environment for people with dementia"). She also engages with broader topics in workplace accommodation, universal design practice ("The state of the science in universal design"), and practical accessibility tooling and professional practice (recent work on web accessibility tools and UX professionals’ experiences in accessibility).

## Notable Works
- "Rehabilitation and health assessment: applying ICF guidelines" (2009) — A highly cited contribution that applies the ICF framework to rehabilitation assessment practice, informing how functional abilities and environmental factors are measured and integrated into intervention planning.  
- "Aquarium sonification: Soundscapes for accessible dynamic informal learning environments" (2006) and follow‑on interactive sonification work (2012–2013) — A program of research that designs, implements, and evaluates sound‑based interpretive systems to make dynamic aquarium exhibits accessible to visitors with vision impairments, advancing methods for real‑time, multisensory exhibit mediation.  
- "The AAC and non‑AAC workplace corpus (ANAWC)" and "Linguistic characteristics of AAC discourse in the workplace" (2013, 2019 overview) — Creation and analysis of a novel corpus to characterize workplace discourse of AAC users versus non‑AAC peers, yielding insights that inform AAC vocabulary design, workplace accommodations, and communication assessment practices.

## Academic Background
Carrie Bruce’s publication record reflects longstanding, interdisciplinary collaborations across rehabilitation science, speech‑language pathology, HCI, and museum/informal learning research. As a Principal Research Scientist at Georgia Tech she has contributed to federally supported and center‑based efforts in universal design and rehabilitation engineering, collaborated with speech‑language pathologists and occupational/rehabilitation professionals, and led applied projects translating assessment frameworks into practice (e.g., toolkits for dementia care and environmental assessment). Her work has been recognized through sustained citation impact (total citations ≈ 499), multiple funded project contributions (including RERC and SBIR related activities), and leadership in projects that bridge research, design, and practitioner communities to improve access, participation, and workplace inclusion for people with disabilities.

## Overview
Mostafa Ammar is a **Regents' Professor** and **Interim Chair** in the *School of Computer Science* at **Georgia Tech**, where he leads a long-standing research program in Computer Networking and Networked Systems (see his lab page: http://www.cc.gatech.edu/~ammar). His work spans design, analysis, and systems-building across wired and wireless environments with a sustained emphasis on <u>robust communication in partitioned and heterogeneous networks</u>, <u>scalable content and service distribution</u>, and <u>resource virtualization and orchestration</u>. He is particularly known for foundational contributions to *delay-tolerant networking (DTN)* and *message ferrying*, scalable *multicast and multimedia delivery*, and the mapping of *virtual networks onto substrate resources*.

---

## Research Areas
Ammar's research portfolio covers a broad range of networking topics, with recurring themes that connect theory, algorithms, and practical systems. He advanced the study of connectivity in sparse and intermittently connected wireless networks through the message-ferrying paradigm ("A message ferrying approach for data delivery in sparse mobile ad hoc networks", 2004; "Message ferry route design for sparse ad hoc networks with mobile nodes", 2006) and through control and routing techniques for predictable and controlled mobility ("Controlling the mobility of multiple data transport ferries in a delay-tolerant network", 2005; "Routing in space and time in networks with predictable mobility", 2004). In the multimedia and multicast domain he produced influential work on scalable video-on-demand and layered multicast schemes ("The use of multicast delivery to provide a scalable and interactive video-on-demand service", 2002; "Layered video multicast with retransmissions (LVMR)", 1997/1998), and on multicast security and group behavior. Ammar has also contributed to the emergence of *network virtualization* and *NFV* through algorithms for embedding virtual network requests onto physical substrates ("Algorithms for Assigning Substrate Network Resources to Virtual Network Components.", 2006) and service function chain placement and migration research (2017). Other strands of his work include reputation and incentive mechanisms for P2P systems ("A reputation system for peer-to-peer networks", 2003), edge and device-based cloud concepts ("Femto clouds: Leveraging mobile devices to provide cloud service at the edge", 2015; "Cosmos: computation offloading as a service for mobile devices", 2014), measurement and privacy for network traces (prefix-preserving IP anonymization), and scalable simulation and evaluation methods for large-scale networks.

## Notable Works
- "A message ferrying approach for data delivery in sparse mobile ad hoc networks" (2004) — introduced the message-ferrying paradigm for providing timely and efficient data delivery in *sparsely connected MANETs/DTNs*, forming a foundational line of work on mobile relays and controlled mobility that has influenced both theory and system designs (highly cited and followed by route-design and control extensions).
- "Algorithms for Assigning Substrate Network Resources to Virtual Network Components." (2006) — a seminal contribution to *network virtualization* that formalized the substrate-to-virtual mapping problem and proposed algorithmic approaches widely cited in virtual network embedding and NFV research.
- "A reputation system for peer-to-peer networks" (2003) — proposed a practical reputation framework for decentralized P2P systems, shaping subsequent work on incentives, trust, and secure peer selection in unstructured overlay networks.

## Academic Background
Mostafa Ammar has had a long and distinguished academic career at Georgia Tech’s College of Computing, holding the title of Regents' Professor and serving in senior leadership as Interim Chair of the School of Computer Science. Over decades of research he has authored and coauthored many highly cited papers (total citations approximately 24,887) and patents spanning networking protocols, distributed systems, and content distribution. His scholarship bridges theoretical models, algorithm design, and experimental systems, and has informed both academic research agendas and practical deployments in DTNs, multicast systems, virtualization/NFV, and mobile/cloud offloading. He maintains an active research group at Georgia Tech (http://www.cc.gatech.edu/~ammar), has supervised numerous students and postdocs, and has collaborated broadly across industry and academia to translate research into standards, systems, and patents.

## Overview
Ada Gavrilovska is a faculty researcher at the **Georgia Institute of Technology**, where she leads a research group working at the intersection of *systems software*, *distributed systems*, and *edge/cloud infrastructure*. Her work focuses on enabling high-performance, secure, and energy-efficient computing across heterogeneous platforms by advancing virtualization, accelerator support, and persistent memory integration. Central themes in her research include <u>GPU and accelerator virtualization</u>, <u>heterogeneous memory management</u> (including byte-addressable non-volatile memory), and the design of software and OS support for the <u>edge-to-cloud continuum</u>.

---

## Research Areas
Ada Gavrilovska’s body of work spans several tightly connected research areas. She has made foundational contributions to *virtualization and hypervisor design* for both high-performance computing and cloud environments, addressing I/O service differentiation, power metering of VMs, and hypervisor architectures for multicore and cluster systems (e.g., work on VM power metering, high-performance hypervisors, and I/O scheduling). A second major thread is *accelerator support and GPU sharing* in virtualized/cloud environments — exemplified by GViM and GPUShare — that enable multi-tenant GPU use and GPU-accelerated virtual machines. A third area is *non-volatile and heterogeneous memory systems*: Gavrilovska’s papers on NoveLSM, pVM, Phoenix, and related works explore persistent LSM-based stores, persistent virtual memory, and memory-speed HPC I/O using NVM, including checkpointing techniques tuned for NVM. Complementing these are contributions to *datacenter energy and thermal management*, *edge computing and function onloading*, and *secure state handling for NFV using trusted execution environments (SGX)*. Across these topics she emphasizes practical system designs that balance performance, isolation, persistence, and manageability for modern datacenter, HPC, and edge deployments.

---

## Notable Works
- "GViM: GPU-accelerated virtual machines" (2009, 363 citations) — introduced mechanisms to enable efficient access to GPUs from virtual machines, demonstrating how virtualization can be reconciled with accelerator-driven high-performance workloads and enabling multi-tenant GPU use in shared environments.  
- "Redesigning LSMs for nonvolatile memory with NoveLSM" (2018, 269 citations) — presented NoveLSM, a persistent LSM-based key-value store that leverages byte-addressable NVM, direct mutability of persistent state, and opportunistic parallelism to achieve low-latency, high-throughput persistence for applications.  
- "HeteroOS: OS Design for Heterogeneous Memory Management in Datacenter" (2017, 187 citations) — proposed OS-level approaches for managing heterogeneous memory in virtualized datacenters, addressing page hotness tracking, migration, and the limits of hypervisor-only solutions to improve application performance on mixed memory substrates.

## Academic Background
Ada Gavrilovska is a senior faculty member in the College of Computing at the Georgia Institute of Technology and maintains an active research homepage (http://www.cc.gatech.edu/~ada). Over a sustained career she has published extensively in premier systems, networking, and HPC venues and has accumulated over 4,800 citations across her papers. Her scholarship is distinguished by cross-cutting contributions to virtualization, accelerator-enabled computing, and the integration of non-volatile memories into system software. Gavrilovska’s research has been supported through competitive grants and industry collaborations, and she is an active contributor to the systems research community through conference program committees, peer review, and graduate student mentorship.

## Overview
Sehoon Ha is a researcher at **Georgia Institute of Technology**, where he conducts interdisciplinary work at the intersection of **robotics**, **computer graphics**, and **machine learning**. He contributes to and leads projects spanning algorithmic foundations, system-level toolkits, and real-world robot deployment. His work emphasizes <u>deep reinforcement learning</u>, <u>legged locomotion and terrain-aware navigation</u>, and <u>sim-to-real transfer and robot design co-optimization</u>. Across these themes he develops both principled learning algorithms and practical engineering artifacts—including *open-source toolkits* and *deployable locomotion systems*—that bridge simulation and hardware.

---

## Research Areas
Sehoon Ha’s research centers on developing scalable learning methods for embodied agents and robots. He has produced influential work on model-free and safe deep reinforcement learning (for example, the highly cited "Soft actor-critic algorithms and applications" and follow-up safety-oriented papers such as "Learning to be safe: Deep rl with a safety critic"), and on applying RL to complex motor skills for legged platforms ("Learning to walk via deep reinforcement learning", "Learning to walk in the real world with minimal human effort", "Legged robots that keep on learning"). He also advances tools and methods for bridging simulation and real robots—creating and maintaining *differentiable and modular simulation frameworks* ("Dart: Dynamic animation and robotics toolkit", "Pods: Policy optimization via differentiable simulation") and techniques for sim-to-real robustness (residual physics, system identification, and policy fine-tuning). More recently his work extends into semantic and language-informed navigation and long-horizon task composition ("Vlfm: Vision-language frontier maps for zero-shot semantic navigation", "Asc: Adaptive skill coordination for robotic mobile manipulation"), as well as meta-learning and design co-optimization for jointly improving robot morphology and controllers ("Joint Optimization of Robot Design and Motion Parameters", "Computational co-optimization of design parameters and motion trajectories"). Across these areas he combines algorithmic innovation, learning-based control, perception for locomotion, and engineering frameworks that enable reproducible transfer to hardware.

---

## Notable Works
- "Soft actor-critic algorithms and applications" (2018, 4462 citations) — seminal contributions to practical, sample-efficient model-free RL that have become foundational for continuous-control problems and subsequently informed numerous robotics and control applications.
- "Learning to walk via deep reinforcement learning" (2018, 683 citations) and follow-on deployment papers — a program of research demonstrating end-to-end learned locomotion policies, sim-to-real transfer, and minimal-intervention real-world training for legged robots.
- "Dart: Dynamic animation and robotics toolkit" (2018, 385 citations) — an open-source, cross-disciplinary simulation and dynamics library that has supported research in both computer graphics and robotics, enabling reproducible experiments and integration with learning pipelines.

---

## Academic Background
Sehoon Ha is based at Georgia Institute of Technology and is affiliated with research groups that connect the Graphics Lab and Humanoid Robotics Lab (as evidenced by collaborative tool development and multi-lab authorship). His publication record spans top-tier conferences and journals in robotics, graphics, and machine learning, and his work has been widely adopted and cited (total citations: 9,315). He has collaborated with researchers across institutions (including industry and university partners) and contributed widely used codebases and datasets, reflecting a sustained emphasis on reproducibility and impact. For a complete list of publications, code, and project pages, see his homepage: http://cc.gatech.edu/~sha9.

## Overview
Greg Turk is a **Professor** in the College of Computing at **Georgia Tech**, where he leads a research group that spans *computer graphics, scientific visualization, and robotics*. His work centers on the interplay between geometric modeling, physical simulation, and data-driven methods, with particular emphasis on <u>surface reconstruction and representation</u>, <u>texture and pattern synthesis</u>, and <u>physically based animation</u>. He maintains an active laboratory web presence (http://www.cc.gatech.edu/~turk/) and has a long-standing record of highly cited contributions (Total citations: **24,547**) that bridge foundational geometry processing techniques and modern learning- and simulation-driven approaches.

---

## Research Areas
Turk's research covers a broad set of topics in **computer graphics** and adjacent fields, with recurring themes that appear across his most-cited papers. In geometric processing and modeling he has advanced methods for <u>surface reconstruction</u> and remeshing (e.g., "Zippered polygon meshes from range images" (1994); "Re-tiling polygonal surfaces" (1992)), and introduced robust implicit representations for handling very large point sets ("Multi-level partition of unity implicits" (2005)) as well as variational implicit techniques ("Variational implicit surfaces" (1999)). In texture and appearance modeling he helped establish exemplar-based and patch-based synthesis methods ("Graphcut textures: Image and video synthesis using graph cuts" (2003); "Texture synthesis on surfaces" (2001)), and explored geometry-aware texture generation using biologically inspired reaction–diffusion models ("Generating textures on arbitrary surfaces using reaction-diffusion" (1991)). Turk's work in simulation and animation spans fluid/solid coupling, deformable materials, and fracture ("Rigid fluid" (2004); "Animation of fracture by physical modeling" (1991); "Fast viscoelastic behavior with thin features" (2008)), often combining mesh- and volumetric-based approaches ("Simplification and repair of polygonal models using volumetric techniques" (2003)). More recently his publications indicate an engagement with robotic control and sim-to-real learning—bringing together simulation, system identification, and policy transfer ("Preparing for the unknown" (2017); "Sim-to-real transfer for biped locomotion" (2019))—as well as visualization topics such as streamline placement and tensor/vector field design for scientific graphics.

## Notable Works
- Zippered polygon meshes from range images (1994) — introduced robust algorithms for combining multiple range scans into coherent polygonal models, foundational for 3D scanning and reconstruction workflows.  
- Graphcut textures: Image and video synthesis using graph cuts (2003) — developed patch-based synthesis with optimal seam stitching using graph cuts, significantly advancing example-based texture and video synthesis.  
- Multi-level partition of unity implicits (2005) — proposed a scalable implicit surface representation for constructing smooth surfaces from very large, noisy point sets, influencing subsequent work in surface reconstruction and compact implicit modeling.

## Academic Background
Greg Turk has been an active researcher and faculty member for multiple decades, publishing influential work from the late 1980s through the present and supervising numerous students and collaborators at Georgia Tech. His publication record spans premier venues in graphics, visualization, and robotics, and his contributions have been widely cited across these communities (Total citations: **24,547**). He has held leadership and service roles common to senior academics—serving on program committees, editorial boards, and conference organizing efforts within ACM SIGGRAPH, IEEE visualization/graphics communities, and related venues—and his career demonstrates sustained interdisciplinary impact across **geometry processing**, *physically based simulation*, and <u>robotic learning and control</u>.

## Overview
Stephen Mussmann is an **Assistant Professor** in the School of **Computer Science** at the *Georgia Institute of Technology*, where he leads a research program that centers on <u>data-centric machine learning</u>, <u>active learning</u>, and <u>experimental design</u>. His lab develops methods and tools that make machine learning systems more label-efficient, interpretable, and robust by combining principled *theory* with practical systems and benchmarks. Across both theoretical analyses and large-scale empirical studies, Mussmann's work emphasizes the design of datasets, selection strategies, and model representations that improve downstream performance while reducing annotation cost.

---

## Research Areas
Mussmann's research spans the intersection of algorithmic foundations and practical systems for label-efficient learning. He has advanced methods for *active learning* — studying when and how adaptive data collection provides real gains (e.g., analyses of uncertainty sampling and expected error reduction) and proposing scalable acquisition strategies such as proxy-based selection for deep networks ("Selection via proxy: Efficient data selection for deep learning", 2019). He is a leading contributor to the nascent field of *data-centric ML*: developing benchmarks and testbeds to rigorously evaluate dataset design and curation practices (notably the DataComp effort, "Datacomp: In search of the next generation of multimodal datasets", 2023), as well as comprehensive benchmarking frameworks for label-efficient methods ("Labelbench", 2023). Mussmann also investigates model representations that enable human-interpretable interaction and intervention, exemplified by work on **concept bottleneck models** ("Concept bottleneck models", 2020). More recent work extends experimental-design principles to large language model finetuning and continuous adaptive experimental design for inverse problems (2024), showing a sustained focus on principled, scalable approaches to minimizing annotation cost while preserving or improving model quality.

---

## Notable Works
- "Concept bottleneck models" (2020) — Introduced and popularized architectures that make high-level concepts explicit and manipulable within learned models, enabling *interpretable interventions* and clearer analysis of model failure modes (1585 citations).
- "Datacomp: In search of the next generation of multimodal datasets" (2023) — Laid out a systematic testbed and evaluation suite for multimodal dataset design, advocating for reproducible, comparative studies of dataset construction and its impact on downstream models (777 citations).
- "Selection via proxy: Efficient data selection for deep learning" (2019) — Developed practical proxy-based selection techniques that make data subset selection and active learning scalable for deep networks by avoiding repeated expensive feature re-training (544 citations).

---

## Academic Background
Stephen Mussmann is an established researcher in machine learning whose publications date back to the mid-2010s and who has accumulated over **3,500 citations** across theory, systems, and applications. Now on the faculty at Georgia Tech, his publication record reflects collaborations across computer vision, natural language processing, and statistical learning theory. ## Overview
Rosa I. Arriaga is an **Associate Professor** in *Interactive Computing* at the Georgia Institute of Technology, affiliated with the College of Computing. She leads a research group in Interactive Computing that sits at the intersection of **human–computer interaction (HCI)**, *cognitive science*, and applied health technologies. Her work centers on developing and evaluating sociotechnical systems that support vulnerable populations—including children, adolescents, veterans, and individuals with autism—by combining computational modeling, user-centered design, and empirical evaluation. Her program emphasizes <u>designing interactive systems for health and developmental support</u>, <u>computational models of human behavior</u>, and <u>social-computing approaches</u> to scaffold independent living and clinical decision-making.

---

## Research Areas
Arriaga’s scholarship spans several interlocking areas: foundational work in *computational cognitive science* and learning theory (e.g., algorithmic treatments of robust concept learning and *random projection* (2006)), and contemporary computational social science methods such as using large language models to *simulate human subjects* and run “Turing Experiments” to evaluate model fidelity to human behavior (2023). In HCI and mHealth she has designed and evaluated mobile and SMS-based interventions to support chronic pediatric illnesses (asthma, diabetes) and studied how sensor-rich smartphones can extend interaction beyond touch (BackTap; Beyondtouch). A substantial strand of her work focuses on autism and developmental assessment—creating in-home video systems and social-networking tools to support diagnosis, independence, and social problem solving (e.g., iterative systems for remote autism diagnosis (2015), social networks and SocialMirror projects). She also contributes to research on digital health ecosystems more broadly—examining telehealth adoption during COVID-19, medical-making responses to supply shortages, and using patient-generated sensor data to aid clinical decision-making (2020–2024). Across these areas she combines experimental developmental methods (infant number discrimination, language development) with pragmatic design and deployment studies to address real-world health and developmental challenges.

---

## Notable Works
- Using large language models to simulate multiple humans and replicate human subject studies (2023) — Introduced the concept of a *Turing Experiment* to systematically evaluate how well large language models can emulate diverse aspects of human behavior, highlighting both utility and systematic distortions when LLMs are used as synthetic participants in social-science research.  
- An algorithmic theory of learning: Robust concepts and random projection (2006) — Advanced a computational theory explaining how robust concepts can be learned from limited examples and how random projection supports efficient learning, bridging cognitive theory and algorithmic analysis.  
- Scores on the MacArthur communicative development inventory of children from low-and middle-income families (1998) — Provided influential empirical evidence on early language disparities across socioeconomic groups, informing later work on early-childhood record-keeping, pediatric interventions, and design of parent-facing tools (e.g., Baby Steps; Grow and Know).

---

## Academic Background
Arriaga’s academic trajectory weaves *cognitive science* and *interactive computing*: her publication record includes foundational cognitive development and learning-theory work from the late 1990s and 2000s, followed by a sustained HCI and mHealth portfolio in the 2010s and 2020s. As an Associate Professor in Georgia Tech’s School of Interactive Computing, she directs graduate and undergraduate research projects and mentors interdisciplinary teams that include designers, clinicians, and engineers. Her scholarship is widely cited (over **4,600 citations**), reflecting impact across HCI, developmental psychology, and digital health communities. Her collaborations and deployments indicate sustained partnerships with clinical stakeholders, community organizations, and cross-cultural research teams; she has secured competitive research support and contributed to multi-site studies and translational projects (including telehealth, medical-making, and remote diagnostic systems).

## Overview
Nicholas Lytle is a researcher based at the **Georgia Institute of Technology**, embedded in the university's community of scholars focused on **computing and computer science education**. His work centers on designing, evaluating, and scaling interventions that help K–12 teachers and novice learners engage with *computational thinking* and introductory programming. Lytle's portfolio spans both tool- and curriculum-level innovations (e.g., **Crescendo**, Snap!-based activities) and data-driven educational technologies (e.g., automated hints and help predictors), with an emphasis on practical models for integrating computing into broader schooling contexts. Across his work he foregrounds collaborations with teachers and schools to support the sustainable infusion of <u>computational thinking</u> into existing disciplinary curricula.

---

## Research Areas
Lytle's research program addresses multiple, interconnected areas in computing education. He studies how to infuse <u>computational thinking (CT)</u> into K–12 classrooms (e.g., the **PRADA** integration model and numerous infusion studies), the design and evaluation of scaffolding sequences such as the <u>Use–Modify–Create (UMC)</u> progression for STEM courses, and professional development models to prepare content teachers to teach CT (for example the **3C** professional development model). A second major strand focuses on data-driven support for novice programmers: developing, comparing, and evaluating algorithms for next-step hints, help-need prediction, and the effects of hint design on learning (multiple papers comparing hint-generation algorithms, the impact of textual explanations, and work reducing programming problem state spaces). He also investigates instructional designs and tooling for block-based programming environments (Snap!, Crescendo, SnapClass) including participatory co-design with teachers and students, Parsons problems as scaffolds, and assessment/rubric creation for non-CS teachers. More recent projects extend his interests into online/graduate education research, adaptive assessment, and frameworks for mentoring and research experiences in remote and hybrid learning contexts.

---

## Notable Works
- "PRADA: A practical model for integrating computational thinking in K-12 education" (2019, 154 citations) — Introduced the **PRADA** framework to help teachers plan and implement CT infusion across disciplinary courses, providing a practical roadmap for scaling CT to broad K–12 audiences.  
- "Use, modify, create: Comparing computational thinking lesson progressions for stem classes" (2019, 143 citations) — Empirically compared the **Use–Modify–Create (UMC)** progression, demonstrating how structured lesson sequences can scaffold novice learners in STEM classrooms and informing curriculum design choices for non-CS teachers.  
- "Code, connect, create: The 3c professional development model to support computational thinking infusion" (2020, 77 citations) — Developed and evaluated the **3C** teacher professional development model, offering an evidence-based approach for enabling content-area teachers to meaningfully integrate CT into their instruction.

---

## Academic Background
Nicholas Lytle is an established contributor to the **computing education** research community, with a publication record that spans empirical classroom studies, tool and curriculum design, and data-driven educational technology research. His work has amassed over **940 citations**, reflecting sustained impact on K–12 CT infusion, teacher professional development, and intelligent tutoring/hint systems research. Lytle regularly collaborates with K–12 teachers, interdisciplinary research teams, and educational technologists — evidenced by participatory design projects (e.g., SnapClass), large-scale PD implementations, and evaluations of classroom interventions. He is affiliated with Georgia Tech and is active in venues that bridge learning sciences, computer science education research, and human–computer interaction. His recent publications indicate expanding interests in online graduate education, mentoring frameworks for remote research, and adaptive assessment—suggesting ongoing leadership in both applied classroom research and the development of scalable, data-informed supports for learners and educators.

## Overview
Calton Pu is a Professor and the **J.P. Imlay, Jr. Chair in Software** in the **School of Computer Science** at *Georgia Institute of Technology*. He leads a long-standing research group focused on systems and data-driven services, combining principles from **systems**, **databases**, and **security** to address challenges in modern distributed and cloud environments. His work spans practical system design and principled analysis, with central themes including <u>buffer-overflow and host security</u>, <u>web and semi-structured data extraction</u>, <u>continual query and event-driven information delivery</u>, and <u>performance and resource management for virtualized/cloud infrastructures</u>.

---

## Research Areas
Calton Pu’s research encompasses a wide range of topics across *service computing*, *cloud computing*, *big data*, *data management*, and *information security*. In systems security he made foundational contributions to automatic defenses against memory-corruption attacks (e.g., the StackGuard work on stack-smashing protection and broader analyses of buffer-overflow attacks and defenses). In information extraction and web data management he pioneered XML-enabled wrapper construction (XWRAP) and fully automated object extraction systems (Omini), and developed large-scale monitoring systems using the notion of <u>continual queries</u> (OpenCQ, WebCQ) to detect and deliver information changes on the Web. In cloud and virtualized environments his work characterizes and mitigates performance interference among co-located virtual machines, and proposes dynamic resource management and consolidation strategies (e.g., Mistral, structural constraint-aware VM placement, feedback-driven schedulers) to balance power, performance, and adaptation cost. More recently his portfolio includes privacy-preserving model publishing for deep learning and measurement/mitigation of threats in social platforms (social-spam detection, modeling unintended information leakage), demonstrating a trajectory from core operating-systems and distributed-systems research to contemporary problems in cloud-scale data systems and security.

---

## Notable Works
- "Stackguard: Automatic adaptive detection and prevention of buffer-overflow attacks." (1998) — Introduced a practical compiler-based defense mechanism that spurred wide adoption of stack-smashing protections and shaped subsequent research on automatic host-level security mitigations.
- "XWRAP: An XML-enabled wrapper construction system for web information sources" (2000) — Advanced automated extraction of semi-structured web data by integrating XML-aware wrapper generation, enabling more reliable composition of web information into queryable form.
- "An analysis of performance interference effects in virtual environments" (2007) — Provided systematic measurement and analysis of hidden contention in virtualized datacenters and motivated resource-management techniques to improve performance isolation and placement policies in cloud infrastructures.

---

## Academic Background
Professor Pu has a multi-decade scholarly record beginning in the mid-1980s and continuing to the present, reflected in over **24,108 total citations** and influential publications across systems, databases, and security. As the **J.P. Imlay, Jr. Chair in Software** at *Georgia Tech*, he directs research addressing the intersection of *service-oriented systems, large-scale data management, and host/cloud security*. His publication history shows sustained contributions to both foundational theory (replica control, epsilon-serializability, kernel specialization) and practical systems (compilers for security, information extraction systems, cloud adaptation engines). He has advised and mentored numerous students and collaborators, served the academic community through program committees and editorial roles, and continues to shape research directions in cloud resource management, privacy-preserving data analytics, and robust system security. For more on his publications and group, see his homepage: http://www.cc.gatech.edu/~calton

## Overview
Max Mahdi Roozbahani is a **Senior Lecturer** in the **Computational Science and Engineering (CSE)** program at the **Georgia Institute of Technology**, where he conducts research at the intersection of *machine learning*, *computer vision*, and **geotechnical engineering**. He leads and collaborates on projects that fuse classical soil mechanics and particulate-material modeling with modern data-driven approaches, with a particular focus on <u>pore-scale characterization</u>, <u>particle packing and void-structure analysis</u>, and <u>post-disaster damage assessment using remote sensing and social media imagery</u>. His work spans development of computational methods (e.g., Euclidean Distance Transform implementations), application of simulation techniques (Monte Carlo, DEM, GSP), and creation of datasets and domain-adaptive pipelines for automated damage detection—emphasizing **interdisciplinarity**, reproducible computational tools, and *practical* applications in infrastructure resilience and education.

---

## Research Areas
Roozbahani’s research integrates three tightly coupled areas. In **geotechnical and granular materials** he focuses on microstructural quantification of porous media and particle packings—studies such as "Pore Size Distribution in Granular Material Microstructure" (2017) apply the **Euclidean Distance Transform** to compute effective local pore sizes and polyhedral pore geometries, while earlier work examines <u>mechanical trapping</u>, void ratios, and the influence of container boundaries and random initial distributions on porosity (e.g., 2012–2014 papers on sphere packing and porosity). In **remote sensing and disaster informatics**, he explores use of nighttime light products (Black Marble) and social-media imagery for rapid damage assessment, advancing both analytic workflows and data-fidelity practices ("Black marble nighttime light data for disaster damage assessment", 2023; "Enhancing the fidelity of social media image data sets in earthquake damage assessment", 2025). Third, in **machine learning, computer vision, and computing education**, he develops and evaluates methods for unsupervised and domain-adaptive representation learning (e.g., ScaleNet, DASeg), automated grading/assessment tools for visualizations (VISGRADER), and scalable course and team-management practices for large online CS/ML classes—demonstrating a strong interest in translating ML techniques into pedagogy and operational systems.

## Notable Works
- "Pore Size Distribution in Granular Material Microstructure" (2017) — Introduced a computational framework using the **Euclidean Distance Transform** to quantify polyhedral pore shapes and local pore sizes, improving pore-scale descriptors that inform macroscale flow and constitutive models (most-cited geotechnical contribution).
- "Mechanical trapping of fine particles in a medium of mono‐sized randomly packed spheres" (2014) — Characterized strain‑based filtration and trapping mechanisms in packed-sphere media, linking particle-scale geometry and packing statistics to filtration performance and particle retention behavior.
- "Black marble nighttime light data for disaster damage assessment" (2023) — Demonstrated the use of VIIRS Black Marble nighttime light products to detect and quantify disaster impacts (hurricanes, tornadoes, earthquakes), bridging remote-sensing analytics with rapid post-disaster situational awareness.

## Academic Background
Roozbahani’s publication record reflects a training rooted in **soil mechanics and granular-material modeling**, subsequently broadened to encompass **machine learning, computer vision, and disaster informatics**—an evolution evidenced by early, highly cited geomaterials work (2012–2017) and later computational and ML-focused contributions (2018–2026). As a Senior Lecturer at Georgia Tech CSE he combines research with substantial teaching and curriculum development, producing work on scalable course delivery, automated assignment evaluation, and team management for massive online classes. With over 300 citations across interdisciplinary venues, Roozbahani is recognized for translating classical geotechnical problems into modern computational frameworks and for developing datasets and domain-adaptive pipelines to support rapid, data-driven decision making in hazard response and computing education. He maintains active collaborations across geotechnical engineering, remote sensing, and computer science communities through conference presentations, technical papers (Geo-Congress and domain conferences), and publicly shared datasets and tools.
