## Overview
Amanda Meng is a researcher at the **Georgia Institute of Technology** working at the intersection of **human-centered computing**, **civic technology**, and **data-for-social-good** practice. Her work is situated in research groups that foreground participatory design, community-engaged methods, and critical approaches to data — broadly connecting to Georgia Tech’s communities in *human–computer interaction*, *social computing*, and *computational social science*. Across projects she centers a feminist and relational approach to data work, foregrounding a <u>feminist ethic of care</u> in the design, collection, analysis, and public use of data and advancing practices of <u>data activism</u> and <u>critical data literacy</u>.

---

## Research Areas
Meng’s research spans critical, practice-oriented investigations of how communities produce, mobilize, and are affected by data. She investigates the dynamics of <u>collaborative data work</u> with ethnographic and participatory methods (e.g., “Collaborative Data Work Towards a Caring Democracy,” 2019), documents grassroots <u>counter-data action</u> and resource mobilization in community advocacy (e.g., “Grassroots resource mobilization through counter-data action,” 2018), and theorizes how care can be operationalized in data science applied to social good (e.g., “Care and the Practice of Data Science for Social Good,” 2018). Meng also develops pedagogical methods for integrating ethics into data and computer science education (e.g., “Re-Shape: A Method to Teach Data Ethics for Data Science Education,” 2020; “Using Role-Play to Scale the Integration of Ethics Across the Computer Science Curriculum,” 2021). Other strands of her work include evaluations of the social impact of open government data, analyses of civic and electoral monitoring using social media, and technical–social studies such as longitudinal characterizations of Internet outages and shutdowns (“Destination Unreachable,” 2023). Methodologically, her portfolio combines long-term ethnography, workplace studies, participatory design, mixed-methods evaluation, and applied data-science collaborations with community partners.

---

## Notable Works
- “Collaborative Data Work Towards a Caring Democracy” (2019) — An ethnographic study that advances a *feminist ethic of care* for collaborative data practices and articulates how democratic caring can be enacted through sustained, community-centered data work.
- “Re-Shape: A Method to Teach Data Ethics for Data Science Education” (2020) — A methodological contribution proposing scalable, classroom-oriented approaches to embed data ethics across data science curricula, with implications for pedagogy and practitioner training.
- “Grassroots resource mobilization through counter-data action” (2018) — A participatory-ethnographic analysis of affordable-housing advocacy in Atlanta showing how grassroots groups create and use counter-data to mobilize resources and contest official narratives.

---

## Academic Background
Meng’s publication record (spanning community-engaged ethnographies, pedagogical innovations, and applied data analyses) places her squarely within the interdisciplinary traditions of **HCI**, **CSCW**, and *data-for-good* scholarship. With a consistent presence in collaborative, community-partnered research since at least the mid-2010s (and publications dating back to 2006 reflecting local engagement), she has built a profile combining qualitative methods, critical theory, and applied data science. Her work has accrued over **580 total citations**, reflecting influence across ethics-in-data-education, civic technology, and critical data studies. Meng’s career at Georgia Tech includes sustained collaborations with local civic organizations (notably in Atlanta), contributions to open government data critique, and sustained engagement with teaching and curriculum development in data ethics — suggesting doctoral training and formal training in human-centered computing or a closely related field and continuing affiliation with interdisciplinary research centers and education initiatives at Georgia Tech. Her ongoing publications (including work through 2025) indicate an active research trajectory that continues to shape practice-oriented scholarship on care, activism, and critical data literacy.

## Overview
Irfan Essa is a **Distinguished Professor of Computing** at the Georgia Institute of Technology and a **Research Scientist** at Google. He leads and contributes to research groups that span vision, graphics, robotics, and ubiquitous computing (including Georgia Tech’s *Future Computing Environments* and Living Laboratories efforts) and collaborates broadly on industry–academia projects. His work centers on **Computer Vision**, **Artificial Intelligence**, and **Machine Learning**, with a strong emphasis on practical systems for *video and image synthesis*, *human activity and facial behavior analysis*, and *embodied perception and interaction*. Across decades of work he has advanced methods for <u>example-based texture and video synthesis</u>, <u>robust facial and human activity modeling</u>, and <u>perception for interactive and ubiquitous environments</u>—and more recently has contributed to large-scale generative and embodied AI systems.

---

## Research Areas
Essa’s research covers the intersection of visual perception, generative modeling, and interactive environments. Early foundational work developed robust systems for observing and modeling faces and facial actions (e.g., “A vision system for observing and extracting facial action parameters,” 1994; “Coding, analysis, interpretation, and recognition of facial expressions,” 1997), and for model-based head and eye tracking. In visual synthesis and graphics he introduced practical, patch-based and optimization-driven approaches to texture and video synthesis (“Graphcut textures,” 2003; “Texture optimization for example-based synthesis,” 2005; “Video textures,” 2023), and developed scalable spatiotemporal segmentation techniques for long video sequences (“Efficient hierarchical graph-based video segmentation,” 2010). In ubiquitous computing and assistive environments he helped define and evaluate living-lab approaches for sensing and behavior-aware homes (“The aware home,” 1999; “Ubiquitous sensing for smart and aware environments,” 2002), and applied wearable and ambient sensing to activity discovery and health-related tasks (e.g., food intake detection, eating recognition). More recent contributions extend to low-shot and one-shot dense prediction (“One-shot learning for semantic segmentation,” 2017), self-supervised and contrastive representations for human activity recognition, and large-scale generative video and embodied agents (DD-PPO for navigation; VideoPoet and MAGVIT for video generation), reflecting a trajectory from geometric and statistical vision toward deep, multimodal, and embodied AI.

---

## Notable Works
- "Graphcut textures: Image and video synthesis using graph cuts" (2003) — introduced a practical patch-based synthesis method that stitches transformed patches along optimal seams to produce high-quality image and video textures; this work established techniques widely used in example-based synthesis and collage-style editing.
- "The aware home: A living laboratory for ubiquitous computing research" (1999) — defined a research paradigm and infrastructure for deploying sensing, perception, and human-centered experiments in instrumented home environments, catalyzing long-term research in assistive and pervasive computing.
- "Coding, analysis, interpretation, and recognition of facial expressions" (1997) — presented a robust vision pipeline combining optical flow, geometric and physical models to produce reliable parametric representations of facial motion, substantially advancing automated facial expression analysis.

---

## Academic Background
Irfan Essa has a long-standing academic career at the Georgia Institute of Technology, where he holds the title of **Distinguished Professor of Computing** and has been a driving force behind Georgia Tech’s research on living laboratories and the Future Computing Environments (FCE) group. He also maintains an active research role in industry as a **Research Scientist at Google**, reflecting sustained collaboration across academia and practice. Essa’s publication record spans foundational work in face modeling and action recognition in the 1990s, major contributions to texture and video synthesis and spatiotemporal segmentation in the 2000s, and leadership in modern deep-learning and embodied AI topics in the 2010s–2020s (including low-shot segmentation, large-scale RL for navigation, and multimodal generative video models). His scholarly impact is substantial (over 29,000 citations across his corpus), and he has mentored numerous students and led interdisciplinary teams across computer vision, graphics, robotics, and ubiquitous computing. For more information, see his homepage: http://www.irfanessa.com/.

## Overview
William Gelder is a researcher at the **Georgia Institute of Technology**, embedded in the university's **Human–Computer Interaction (HCI)** and computing-education communities (affiliated with the School of Interactive Computing and related research groups). His work centers on designing and evaluating educational technologies and curricula that support *meaningful, equitable learning* about computing and artificial intelligence for young learners and their teachers. Across projects he pursues <u>AI education</u>, <u>culturally relevant CS pedagogy</u>, and <u>sustainable ubiquitous computing</u> as interconnected research strands, bringing together participatory design with teachers and communities to produce practical classroom materials and evaluation methods.

---

## Research Areas
Gelder's research spans several overlapping areas in HCI and computing education. He investigates design and co-design processes for middle-school AI curricula and teacher professional development (e.g., designing a 9‑week AI elective and PD in partnership with school districts and the Georgia Department of Education). He develops and studies place‑based, culturally responsive educational technologies for language-immersion and Indigenous-context classrooms (work with Hawaiian Kaiapuni schools explores *assets-based approaches* to teacher support and generative educational systems). In parallel he explores sustainable and embedded computing platforms (batteryless Arduino systems and code-transformation techniques) that enable low-resource, environmentally conscious deployments of interactive technologies. Finally, he studies assessment and evaluation strategies for K–12 AI knowledge, focusing on age-appropriate evaluation instruments and classroom‑scale measures of both technical and ethical understanding.

---

## Notable Works
- "From lecture hall to homeroom: co-designing an AI elective with middle school CS teachers" (2025, 5 citations) — co-designed a 9‑week middle‑school elective and associated teacher PD, documenting iterative teacher-centered design practices for introducing AI concepts in formal K–12 settings.
- "" Those don't work for us": An Assets-Based Approach to Incorporating Emerging Technologies in Viable Hawaiian Teacher Support Tools for Culturally Relevant CS Education" (2024, 4 citations) — articulates an assets‑based, place‑sensitive approach to designing teacher tools for Hawaiian bilingual immersion (Kaiapuni) contexts, emphasizing cultural continuity and teacher readiness.
- "User-directed Assembly Code Transformations Enabling Efficient Batteryless Arduino Applications" (2024, 4 citations) — presents methods for transforming low-level code to enable more efficient, battery‑free Arduino deployments, contributing to sustainable ubiquitous computing platforms suitable for educational and community settings.

---

## Academic Background
Gelder's publication record indicates an interdisciplinary trajectory bridging **HCI**, **computing education**, and *sustainable embedded systems*. His collaborative projects involve partnerships with multiple universities, the Georgia Department of Education, K–12 school districts, and Hawaiian immersion schools—reflecting a strong emphasis on applied, community‑engaged research. With a body of work focused on co‑design, curriculum development, tool creation, and assessment, Gelder is positioned within Georgia Tech's ecosystem of HCI and computing-education scholarship. His work to date (total citations: 18) highlights an emerging scholar establishing a portfolio of practitioner-facing studies, design artifacts, and evaluative methods that support equitable, place‑based computing education.

## Overview
Vijay Ganesh is a **Professor** in the School of Computer Science at the **Georgia Institute of Technology** in Atlanta, where he leads a research group focused on advancing automated reasoning, program analysis, and the practical application of formal methods. His lab develops high-impact tools and algorithms at the intersection of *software engineering*, *mathematical logic*, and *artificial intelligence*, with particular emphasis on <u>symbolic execution</u>, <u>SMT/SAT solving</u>, and <u>string constraint solving</u>. Ganesh’s work spans both foundational decision procedures and pragmatic bug-finding systems, producing widely used artifacts (e.g., STP, EXE, HAMPI and several string-solving extensions for Z3) and bridging theory and practice in automated analysis. His body of work has accumulated over **9,700 citations**, reflecting broad influence across programming languages, security, verification, and solver engineering.

---

## Research Areas
Ganesh’s research advances automated reasoning techniques and their application to real-world software and security problems. He has made foundational contributions to the design and implementation of decision procedures for low-level program models—most notably efficient solvers for the theory of bit-vectors and arrays (STP) and specialized string/regex/length solvers (HAMPI, Z3str2/3/4). He has pioneered and popularized practical *symbolic-execution–based* bug-finding and concolic-fuzzing systems (EXE, BuzzFuzz, jFuzz), demonstrating how <u>symbolic execution</u> and <u>taint-based directed fuzzing</u> can systematically generate crash- and vulnerability-triggering inputs. In parallel, Ganesh has explored solver heuristics and ML-driven solver selection—introducing the view of branching as an optimization problem (learning-rate and LRB heuristics), ML-based restart policies, and algorithm-selection frameworks for SMT (MachSMT). His recent work applies machine learning and explainability techniques to solver and model behavior, and extends automated-reasoning methods into domains as diverse as smart-contract analysis, cryptanalysis, combinatorial search, and even aspects of quantum foundations and interpretable physics models.

---

## Notable Works
- EXE: Automatically generating inputs of death (2008) — Introduced a practical symbolic-execution based bug-finder that systematically generates crashing inputs for real-world programs, catalyzing subsequent work in concolic testing and whitebox fuzzing.  
- A decision procedure for bit-vectors and arrays (STP) (2007) — Presented the STP solver optimized for large-scale software-analysis problems, establishing a high-performance word-level decision procedure widely used in verification and security.  
- HAMPI / Z3-based string solvers and solver heuristics (HAMPI 2009; Z3str3 2017; Learning-rate branching heuristic 2016) — Developed specialized string constraint solvers and theory-aware heuristics, and introduced ML-informed branching strategies that materially improved SAT/SMT solver performance on industry benchmarks.

---

## Academic Background
Vijay Ganesh is a senior academic with a long record of tool-building, publications, and community leadership in formal methods and software-reliability research. As a faculty member at Georgia Tech he has supervised numerous students and collaborators, maintained an active public repository of tools and resources (homepage: https://vganesh1.github.io/), and contributed to cross-disciplinary projects spanning academia and industry. His work has been recognized by extensive citation impact, repeated adoption of his tools in verification and security pipelines, and sustained involvement in program committees and community standards (e.g., SMT-LIB and solver benchmarking efforts). Ganesh’s publication portfolio shows continuous contributions from systems and architecture description languages in the late 1990s to contemporary advances in ML-augmented solvers and automated program analysis, reflecting a career that blends theoretical depth with practical engineering and broad collaborative engagement.

## Overview
Mark Riedl is a **Professor of Computing** at the **Georgia Institute of Technology**, where he leads an active research group focused on the intersection of **artificial intelligence**, **machine learning**, and narrative technologies (see http://www.cc.gatech.edu/~riedl/). His work centers on enabling machines to *create, understand, and communicate stories* and on making AI systems more transparent, trustworthy, and aligned with human values. Central to his agenda are the <u>computational modeling of narrative</u>, <u>interactive narrative and game-centered AI</u>, and <u>human-centered explainable AI (XAI)</u>—investigations that span from symbolic planning approaches for plot and character to data-driven neural methods and socio-technical analyses of explainability.

---

## Research Areas
Riedl’s research bridges several interrelated areas in AI and HCI. In narrative intelligence and interactive storytelling he develops systems for <u>narrative planning</u>, authorable interactive narratives, and multi-agent story generation (e.g., work on balancing plot and character, plan-driven behavior for game environments, and transitioning from linear story generation to branching story graphs). In creative and procedural content generation he explores story realization, plot-graph based crowd-sourced story generation, and procedural game-world and level generation from data (including gameplay video and co-creative level design). In explainability and human-centered AI he advances a socio-technical view of XAI—introducing concepts such as *human-centered explainable AI (HCXAI)*, *social transparency*, and automated rationale/rationalization techniques that translate agent internals into natural-language explanations. Riedl also investigates value alignment and safe-AI themes, using stories and narratives as mechanisms for conveying values to agents and for studying downstream effects and pitfalls of explanations in real sociotechnical settings.

---

## Notable Works
- "Narrative planning: Balancing plot and character" (2010) — seminal work establishing planning-based methods for generating stories that reconcile global plot coherence with believable character behavior, foundational to subsequent interactive narrative and story-generation research.  
- "Expanding explainability: Towards social transparency in ai systems" (2021) — advances a socio-organizational perspective on explainability, arguing that explanations must account for the social context in which AI systems operate and proposing directions for socially aware XAI design.  
- "Automated rationale generation: a technique for explainable AI and its effects on human perceptions" (2019) — introduces and evaluates a method for producing human-like natural language rationales from agent state/action data, empirically studying how such rationales shape user perceptions of autonomous systems.

---

## Academic Background
Riedl’s publication record traces back to the early 2000s with a sustained focus on interactive narrative, game AI, and later human-centered explainability. As a senior faculty member at Georgia Tech, he operates at the nexus of the College of Computing, game research, and HCI communities; his collaborations include co-authors and contributors from academic (e.g., Cornell, IBM Research) and industry partners visible in multi-author projects. His body of work has been widely cited (over **15,600** total citations), reflecting influence across AI, machine learning, human-computer interaction, and game research. Riedl’s portfolio combines theory, systems-building, empirical human-subject studies, and socio-technical critique, and he regularly contributes to conferences and journals in AI, HCI, and computational creativity.

## Overview
Munmun De Choudhury is a faculty member at the **Georgia Institute of Technology**, where she leads a research group focused on the intersection of **computational methods** and *mental health* (maintained at http://www.munmund.net/). Her lab develops and applies large-scale, data-driven approaches to understand and predict psychological states from online behavior, with particular emphasis on <u>mental health detection</u>, <u>social media as a population-level sensor</u>, and <u>human-centered machine learning</u>. De Choudhury's work combines **natural language processing**, **social computing**, and **computational social science** to produce actionable insights for clinicians, public health researchers, and platform designers.

---

## Research Areas
De Choudhury's research spans several tightly connected areas: automated detection and forecasting of psychiatric conditions from digital traces (notably *depression* and *suicidal ideation*), the dynamics of mental health discourse and help-seeking on platforms such as Twitter, Reddit, Facebook, Instagram, and Tumblr, and methodological and ethical questions that arise when inferring health states from passively collected data. Her publications investigate linguistic and behavioral markers of mood and illness (e.g., "Predicting depression via social media"; "Characterizing and predicting postpartum depression from shared facebook data"), platform-specific disclosure and anonymity effects ("Mental health discourse on reddit"), and transitions to acute risk ("Discovering shifts to suicidal ideation from mental health content in social media"). She also addresses broader system-level topics — from the design and evaluation of predictive pipelines ("Methods in predictive techniques for mental health status on social media: a critical review") to clinical translation and governance of AI in mental health care ("Artificial intelligence for mental health care: clinical applications, barriers, facilitators, and artificial wisdom" and "From promise to practice: towards the realisation of AI-informed mental health care"). Across these efforts she interrogates issues of fairness, privacy, and ethical responsibility (e.g., "A taxonomy of ethical tensions in inferring mental health states from social media"), while extending computational techniques to allied domains such as disordered eating, minority stress, misinformation, and hate speech.

## Notable Works
- "Predicting depression via social media" (2013) — seminal work demonstrating that linguistic, temporal, and social features from social media can reliably identify individuals with major depression, laying groundwork for subsequent computational screening approaches.
- "Mental health discourse on reddit: Self-disclosure, social support, and anonymity" (2014) — a detailed characterization of how platform affordances (notably anonymity and community norms) shape disclosure and support dynamics in large-scale online mental health communities.
- "Discovering shifts to suicidal ideation from mental health content in social media" (2016) — longitudinal and event-focused analyses that identify markers and transition patterns associated with emergent suicidal ideation, advancing risk-detection strategies and intervention timing.

## Academic Background
De Choudhury is an established interdisciplinary researcher whose corpus (over 26,800 citations to date) bridges computer science, public health, and clinical collaboration. She trained as a computational researcher and has built a portfolio of externally funded projects and cross-disciplinary partnerships with clinicians, public health experts, and policy stakeholders. Her scholarship has been widely published in top-tier venues across computer science, social computing, and health, and she has been recognized through competitive research grants and invitations to contribute to policy and clinical discourse on AI and mental health. She remains active in academic service and mentorship, directing student research at Georgia Tech and collaborating with interdisciplinary teams to translate computational findings into ethically informed practice.

## Overview
Helen Xu is a researcher at the **Georgia Institute of Technology**, where she conducts research in a group focused on systems and algorithms for large-scale data processing. Her work centers on the design and analysis of high-performance, write- and update-friendly data structures for graphs and ordered sets, with particular emphasis on **parallel algorithms**, **caching**, and **performance engineering**. She investigates practical and theoretical tradeoffs for <u>dynamic graph representations</u>, <u>packed memory arrays</u>, and <u>cache-adaptive/cache-oblivious</u> techniques to enable *efficient updates*, *low-overhead traversal*, and *high locality* in multicore and memory-constrained environments.

---

## Research Areas
Helen's research spans the intersection of data structures, parallelism, and memory systems. She has developed and evaluated new graph containers and compressed representations (e.g., *Packed Compressed Sparse Row*, *Batch-parallel CSR*) that balance update performance with locality for graph analytics. A recurring theme is the adaptation and optimization of the *Packed Memory Array (PMA)* paradigm — producing variants for parallelism, compression (CPMA), and search-optimized layouts (SPMA) — to support fast inserts, range scans, and cache-efficient traversals. Her work also addresses skewed, streaming graph workloads (e.g., *Terrace*), write-optimized ordered dictionaries (e.g., *write-optimized skip lists*, B-skiplist variants), and the interplay between caching policies and multicore concurrency (cache-adaptive analyses and multicore paging results). She combines theoretical analysis (competitive/cost models for caching and adaptivity) with extensive empirical evaluation and benchmarking (e.g., *BYO: a unified framework for benchmarking large-scale graph containers*), and explores applications ranging from SpGEMM optimizations and sparse tensor formats to novel uses of neuromorphic and RL-driven system optimizations.

---

## Notable Works
- "Packed compressed sparse row: A dynamic graph representation" (2018) — Introduced a compact, update-friendly variant of CSR that preserves the traversal and lookup performance of CSR while enabling efficient insertions and deletions, significantly influencing later dynamic graph containers.  
- "Terrace: A hierarchical graph container for skewed dynamic graphs" (2021) — Proposed a hierarchical container designed for streaming, degree-skewed graphs, enabling fast updates and analytics on real-world social and web graphs with heavy-tailed degree distributions.  
- "A parallel packed memory array to store dynamic graphs" (2021) — Adapted the Packed Memory Array for parallel, cache-efficient storage of dynamic graphs, demonstrating how PMA-style layouts can deliver both fast concurrent updates and locality for graph traversals.

---

## Academic Background
Helen Xu has built a substantial body of work at the confluence of algorithms, data structures, and systems, accumulating over **400 total citations** for contributions that span both theory and practice. Affiliated with the **Georgia Institute of Technology**, she has collaborated with researchers across institutions (including earlier collaborations evident in projects involving Stony Brook and national laboratory coauthors) and published extensively on dynamic graph containers, PMA-based structures, caching theory, and performance engineering. Her publications appear in leading venues for parallel computing, data structures, and systems; she maintains a public research homepage at https://itshelenxu.github.io/ where code, papers, and benchmarks accompanying her work are documented. Her record demonstrates a consistent emphasis on creating portable, empirically validated data-structure designs that address real-world constraints of concurrency and memory hierarchy.

## Overview
Shamkant B. Navathe is a Professor of Computer Science at the **Georgia Institute of Technology**, a long‑standing leader in the field of **database systems**. He directs and contributes to research and educational efforts that bridge *fundamental theory*, *practical system design*, and *applied data management*. His work spans core topics such as <u>database design and modeling</u>, <u>schema integration</u>, <u>data fragmentation and distribution</u>, and <u>data mining for association and negative associations</u>. Navathe is also co‑author of the widely used textbook **Fundamentals of Database Systems**, a *foundational* resource in database education with broad international adoption. Over his career his scholarship has been highly influential (total citations ~34,500), reflecting both deep theoretical contributions and practical tools for database designers and practitioners.

---

## Research Areas
Navathe’s research agenda emphasizes principled methods for designing, integrating, partitioning, and querying database systems. In conceptual modeling and design he has advanced the practice of <u>entity‑relationship modeling</u> (see "Conceptual database design: an entity-relationship approach" (1991)) and developed interactive tools and methodologies for <u>integrating user views and schemas</u> ("Integrating user views in database design" (1986); "A tool for integrating conceptual schemas and user views" (1988)). In schema integration and automation he contributed formal foundations and reasoning approaches ("A theory of attributed equivalence in databases with application to schema integration" (2002); "On automatic reasoning for schema integration" (1993); "A comparative analysis of methodologies for database schema integration" (1986)). Navathe has also produced influential work on physical design and fragmentation: a suite of papers on <u>vertical partitioning</u> and objective functions for fragmenting relations ("Vertical partitioning algorithms for database design" (1984); "Vertical partitioning for database design: a graphical algorithm" (1989); "An objective function for vertically partitioning relations in distributed databases and its analysis" (1994)). His interests extend into temporal extensions to the relational model ("A temporal relational model and a query language" (1989); "Temporal Extensions to the Relational Model and SQL." (1993)), automated index selection and query optimization ("Adaptive and automated index selection in RDBMS" (1992)), and data mining methods including algorithms for mining <u>association rules</u> and <u>negative associations</u> ("An Efficient Algorithm for Mining Association Rules in Large Databases" (1995); "Mining for strong negative associations in a large database of customer transactions" (1998)). Navathe’s work has also interfaced with applied domains — notably bioinformatics — through database construction efforts such as the MITOMAP mitochondrial genome database ("MITOMAP: a human mitochondrial genome database" (1996) and subsequent updates).

## Notable Works
- "Fundamentals of database systems" (2008) — A widely adopted textbook co‑authored by Navathe that synthesizes foundational concepts in data modeling, languages, and DBMS implementation; this work has been translated and issued in multiple editions and languages and stands as a cornerstone of database education.
- "An Efficient Algorithm for Mining Association Rules in Large Databases" (1995) — A highly cited algorithmic contribution that advanced scalable methods for discovering association rules in transaction data and influenced subsequent research in frequent pattern mining.
- "A comparative analysis of methodologies for database schema integration" (1986) — A seminal study that systematically evaluated schema integration techniques, shaping research and practice in view integration, schema mapping, and the theoretical limits of automated integration.

## Academic Background
Navathe’s academic career spans multiple decades of scholarship, teaching, and mentorship at the **Georgia Institute of Technology**, where he has established a research profile at the intersection of database theory and system design. His publication record begins in the late 1970s and continues through recent work, reflecting sustained contributions to core database problems and their applications. He is the co‑author of multiple editions of a leading database textbook and of influential research articles on schema integration, partitioning, temporal databases, and data mining; several of these works have been translated into other languages, indicating broad international impact. Navathe has collaborated widely across academia and applied domains (including bioinformatics projects such as MITOMAP), supervised graduate students, and contributed to curriculum development in database systems. His body of work — cumulative citations approximately 34,500 — attests to his role as a *foundational* and *enduring* figure in database research and education.

## Overview
Alexander Adams is a faculty researcher at the **Georgia Institute of Technology**, where he leads the **Uncommon Sense Lab** and directs a research program at the intersection of *ubiquitous computing*, human–computer interaction, and digital health. His work centers on developing <u>unobtrusive sensing</u> and <u>passive intervention</u> technologies that deploy everyday devices (smartphones, wearables, low-cost sensors) to measure and support physiological and behavioral health in real-world settings. He is best known for systems that combine hardware prototyping, signal processing, and human-subject studies to create practical, low-friction tools for monitoring sleep, respiration, body sounds, affect, and substance-use behaviors.

---

## Research Areas
Adams’ research spans several tightly related areas within **ubiquitous computing** and mobile health. He has pioneered contactless physiological sensing using commodity radar and motion sensors (e.g., DoppleSleep and related demos) and developed acoustic and wearable approaches to capture non-speech body sounds for dietary, respiratory, and affective sensing (BodyBeat, Sensing non-speech body sounds). A recurring theme is the design of *mindless* or minimally demanding interventions—systems that subtly influence affect and behavior without requiring conscious effort—exemplified by EmotionCheck and his work on affective touch and peripheral breathing cues (BreathePulse). He also advances low-cost, smartphone-based diagnostic and spectrometric tools (SpectraPhone / Nutrilyzer / μ-Phone) and builds research platforms for tracking emerging behaviors such as e‑cigarette use (PuffPacket). Across these topics he emphasizes robustness and equity (for example, skin-tone–calibrated SpO2 measurement in OptoBeat), real-world deployment, and cross-disciplinary validation with clinical and behavioral measures.

---

## Notable Works
- "Dopplesleep: A contactless unobtrusive sleep sensing system using short-range doppler radar" (2015) — introduced a single-sensor, contactless approach to continuous sleep and physiological tracking using commercial radar modules and demonstrated rich sleep-related measurements in everyday environments.  
- "BodyBeat: a mobile system for sensing non-speech body sounds." (2014) — developed wearable and phone-based methods to capture and classify non-speech body sounds (eating, coughing, breathing, laughter), opening new pathways for dietary, respiratory, and behavioral monitoring.  
- "EmotionCheck: leveraging bodily signals and false feedback to regulate our emotions" (2016) — combined wearable actuation and perceptual manipulation of bodily signals to demonstrate *mindless* emotion-regulation interventions that reduce anxiety through subtle feedback.

---

## Academic Background
Adams trained as an interdisciplinary researcher in **ubiquitous computing** and human–computer interaction, and his publication record reflects long-standing engagement with top conferences and journals in HCI, mobile systems, and pervasive health. He is a tenured/tenure-track faculty member at Georgia Tech (homepage: https://www.alexandertadams.com/) and directs the Uncommon Sense Lab, which collaborates closely with clinicians and engineers on applied sensing problems. His body of work has accrued over **1,200 citations**, evidencing sustained impact on mobile sensing, wearable interventions, and low-cost diagnostics. He has mentored numerous graduate students and produced multiple open research platforms and prototypes used by the community. His projects emphasize reproducibility, real-world evaluation, and attention to fairness and accessibility in sensing technologies.

## Overview
Suguman Bansal is an **Assistant Professor** in the **School of Computer Science** at the **Georgia Institute of Technology**, where he leads an independent research group focused on the intersection of **formal methods**, **programming languages**, and **artificial intelligence**. His work develops principled, algorithmic techniques that connect classical verification and synthesis—such as <u>reactive synthesis</u> and automata-theoretic methods—with modern *reinforcement learning* and multi-agent decision making. A recurring theme in his group is the use of <u>logical specifications</u> to both *specify* and *guide* learning and control, enabling the automatic construction and verification of controllers that satisfy high-level temporal and quantitative objectives.

---

## Research Areas
Bansal’s research spans several tightly connected areas: (1) the theory and practice of **reactive synthesis** from temporal specifications (LTL, LTLf) and the algorithmic bottlenecks that arise when converting specifications to automata; (2) **specification-guided reinforcement learning**, which studies how to transform high-level logical objectives into learning-friendly representations and reward structures; and (3) **quantitative verification** and automata-based techniques for comparing and reasoning about system costs and rewards (e.g., discounted-sum inclusion and comparator automata). Across these threads he emphasizes *compositional* and *scalable* methods — for example, DAG- and automata-based compositional approaches to mitigate the state-explosion in LTLf→DFA conversion, hybrid compositional reasoning for finite-horizon synthesis, and frameworks that translate temporal specifications into forms amenable to RL algorithms and reactive synthesis.

---

## Notable Works
- "Compositional reinforcement learning from logical specifications" (2021) — introduced a compositional approach to learn control policies from high-level logical specifications by automatically deriving suitable reward structures and using compositional RL to scale to complex tasks.
- "Hybrid compositional reasoning for reactive synthesis from finite-horizon specifications" (2020) — identified and addressed the primary scalability bottleneck in LTLf synthesis (the LTLf→DFA conversion) via hybrid, compositional techniques that significantly improve synthesis performance for finite-horizon specifications.
- "A framework for transforming specifications in reinforcement learning" (2022) — proposed a principled methodology to bridge reactive synthesis and RL by transforming temporal logic specifications into representations and objectives that enable learning in unknown MDPs while retaining correctness guarantees.

## Academic Background
Bansal’s publication record (total citations: **441**) reflects a sustained program of work at the nexus of *formal methods*, *programming languages*, and *machine learning*. His early work (2016–2018) focused on algorithmic problems in games, automata, and quantitative verification, progressing to influential contributions in specification-guided learning and compositional synthesis (2018–2023). As an Assistant Professor at Georgia Tech he continues to publish in top venues on topics such as automata-based quantitative verification, temporal-specification synthesis, and reinforcement learning from specifications. He maintains an active research homepage (https://suguman.github.io/) and collaborates widely across verification, AI, and programming-languages communities.

## Overview
Aibek Musaev is a researcher at the **Georgia Institute of Technology**, where he conducts interdisciplinary work at the intersection of **Social Network Analysis**, **Big Data Analytics**, and **Computer Vision**. He leads and contributes to research efforts that combine data from *social sensors* and physical sensing systems to support real-time situational awareness and decision making. His work emphasizes <u>social sensing and event detection</u>, <u>multisource (physical + social) information fusion</u>, and the computational methods needed to scale these systems to high-volume streams of user-generated content and video. His projects span applied domains such as disaster informatics (notably landslide detection), transportation safety and perception (including public perception of autonomous vehicles), and public-health–related social media analysis. (Homepage: http://aibek.cs.ua.edu/)

---

## Research Areas
Musaev’s research focuses on automated detection and characterization of real-world events by integrating heterogeneous data sources. He has developed systems and algorithms for: late-time and real-time detection of natural hazards using social media (notably the LITMUS family of papers: "LITMUS: a multi-service composition system for landslide detection" and related works on multilingual reporting and clustering composition); automated event detection in streaming social media with attention to <u>concept drift</u> and relevance ranking; and the fusion of physical sensor streams (e.g., traffic cameras, satellite/weather feeds) with social sensor data to improve coverage and timeliness ("Multi-hazard detection by integrating social media and physical sensors", "Towards a real-time service for landslide detection"). He has also contributed methods for fast text classification and multilingual/zero-shot approaches (e.g., *randomized explicit semantic analysis*), applied sentiment- and content-analysis methods to public perception of technology (notably "Effects of the autonomous vehicle crashes on public perception of the technology"), and explored computer-vision applications for traffic monitoring and in-store multi-person tracking. Across these topics he emphasizes scalable algorithms, service-oriented systems, and evaluation on real-world datasets drawn from Twitter, traffic camera streams, and domain-specific online communities (e.g., r/Dentistry).

---

## Notable Works
- "Effects of the autonomous vehicle crashes on public perception of the technology" (2021, 129 citations) — A widely cited study applying before-and-after sentiment analysis to assess how high-profile autonomous vehicle incidents shape public opinion and discourse; demonstrates the value of social sensors in transportation research and technology assessment.
- "LITMUS: a multi-service composition system for landslide detection" (2014, 68 citations) — Introduction of a compositional service architecture that integrates multiple physical and social information sources to detect landslides, forming the basis for subsequent LITMUS papers and deployed / prototype services for disaster informatics.
- "Detection of Damage and Failure Events of Critical Public Infrastructure using Social Sensor Big Data" (2016, 36 citations) — A cross-disciplinary contribution that uses social media as a complementary sensing modality to identify infrastructure failures (e.g., road damage), highlighting methods for filtering, ranking, and validating citizen-reported information for civil-engineering applications.

---

## Academic Background
Aibek Musaev is an established interdisciplinary researcher affiliated with the **Georgia Institute of Technology** and with a public academic presence maintained via a University of Alabama homepage, indicating prior appointments and collaborations across institutions. His publication record (total citations: 581) spans computer science, civil and environmental engineering, and public affairs, and reflects sustained collaboration with domain experts in disaster management, transportation, and public health. Musaev’s work has produced both methodological contributions (e.g., fast and multilingual text-classification methods, concept-drift–aware detectors) and applied systems (the LITMUS landslide detection service, infrastructure-monitoring approaches, and traffic-camera analytics). He frequently publishes in venues focused on services computing, big-data analytics, and applied machine learning, and partners with interdisciplinary teams across schools within Georgia Tech (including Civil & Environmental Engineering and policy-focused units) to translate computational research into operational tools for emergency response and transportation monitoring.

## Overview
Kim Luong MacLeod is a researcher at the **Georgia Institute of Technology** whose work sits at the intersection of **information security**, *privacy*, and the societal impacts of technology. Her scholarship focuses on the human, legal, and policy dimensions of digital risk — particularly <u>identity theft</u> and its non‑financial consequences — and she is associated with Georgia Tech’s broader community of scholars addressing **security, privacy, and technology policy**. Her work emphasizes interdisciplinary analysis that connects technical phenomena to consumer protections, regulatory frameworks, and social harms.

---

## Research Areas
MacLeod’s research centers on <u>identity theft</u> as a multifaceted social and policy problem rather than solely a financial crime. Drawing on her 2006 paper, her topics include the *non‑financial harms* of identity compromise (psychological stress, reputational damage, time costs), the evolution and effectiveness of *legislation and consumer protections*, mechanisms of occurrence and detection, and recommendations for institutional responses. Her approach integrates legal and policy review with attention to real‑world impacts on victims, aiming to inform both technical mitigation strategies and policy design that better address the breadth of harms identity theft produces.

## Notable Works
- "The other side of identity theft: Not just a financial concern" (2006) — a comprehensive examination of the *non‑financial* dimensions of identity theft and their implications for policy and consumer rights.
- "The other side of identity theft: Not just a financial concern" (2006) — synthesis of historical and contemporary **legislative** responses to identity theft, highlighting gaps in protections and proposing directions for regulatory improvement.
- "The other side of identity theft: Not just a financial concern" (2006) — framing identity theft as a *societal* problem that requires interdisciplinary research and coordinated responses from technical, legal, and social service communities.

## Academic Background
MacLeod is affiliated with the Georgia Institute of Technology and has been active in scholarship since at least 2006, when she published her influential review of identity theft. Her documented work has been cited by other scholars (total citations: 7), indicating engagement with academic and policy conversations about consumer protection and information security. While specific degrees and honors are not listed in the public record provided, her publication record suggests an interdisciplinary orientation bridging technology, law, and social impact—positioning her within Georgia Tech’s ecosystem of researchers addressing security, privacy, and technology policy.

## Overview
Harish Ravichandar is an **Assistant Professor** at the **Georgia Institute of Technology**, where he leads the STAR Lab (see https://star-lab.cc.gatech.edu/). His work sits at the intersection of **robot learning**, **human-robot collaboration**, and **multi-robot systems**. He focuses on developing principled, data-driven methods for enabling robots to acquire and generalize complex skills—ranging from whole-arm and bimanual manipulation to heterogeneous team coordination—often by leveraging *learning from demonstration*, model-learning techniques, and intent-aware control. Central to his research agenda are the <u>learning of stable nonlinear dynamics</u>, <u>intention inference for anticipatory interaction</u>, and <u>task allocation and scheduling in heterogeneous teams</u>—themes that recur across his body of work and lab projects.

---

## Research Areas
Ravichandar’s research spans several tightly connected areas in robotics and machine learning. He has advanced methods for *learning from demonstration (LfD)* and for representing motion skills as stable dynamical systems (e.g., contracting dynamical system primitives) that enable robust reproduction of point-to-point, periodic, and pose (position + orientation) motions ("Learning Partially Contracting Dynamical Systems from Demonstrations" (2017); "Learning position and orientation dynamics from demonstrations via contraction analysis" (2019)). He has contributed broadly to *human intention inference* and human-in-the-loop control—developing EM- and multiple-model-based estimators and integrating gaze priors for early goal prediction to enable anticipatory and safe collaborative behaviors ("Human intention inference using expectation-maximization..." (2016); "Gaze and motion information fusion for human intention inference" (2018)). On the multi-agent front, he has developed unified frameworks and practical algorithms for task assignment, scheduling, and resilient coalition planning in heterogeneous robot teams (e.g., STRATA, GRSTAPS, D-ITAGS), along with MARL benchmarking and generalization strategies for cross-team policy transfer. More recently, his work explores *dexterous manipulation* and model-based representations (Koopman operator methods, neural geometric fabrics, ImMimic/CIMER) to improve sample efficiency and interpretability for multi-fingered hands and cross-domain imitation.

---

## Notable Works
- "Recent Advances in Robot Learning from Demonstration" (2020) — a widely cited synthesis that shaped the field's understanding of LfD paradigms, trade-offs, and practical considerations for deploying LfD in real robotic systems.  
- Development of contracting dynamical system primitives and related model-learning approaches for stable motion reproduction and pose dynamics (e.g., "Learning Partially Contracting Dynamical Systems from Demonstrations" (2017); "Learning position and orientation dynamics from demonstrations via contraction analysis" (2019)). These works provide foundations for learning stable, reactive motion policies from demonstrations.  
- Unified and resilient approaches for heterogeneous multi-robot coordination and tasking, including STRATA ("STRATA: A Unified Framework for Task Assignments in Large Teams of Heterogeneous Agents" (2020)), GRSTAPS ("GRSTAPS: Graphically recursive simultaneous task allocation, planning, and scheduling" (2022)), and D-ITAGS ("D-ITAGS: A Dynamic Interleaved Approach to Resilient Task Allocation, Scheduling, and Motion Planning" (2023)), which address allocation, planning, scheduling, and robustness under uncertainty.

---

## Academic Background
Harish Ravichandar is an active academic at the Georgia Institute of Technology, where he directs the STAR Lab and leads projects at the nexus of robot learning, dexterous manipulation, and multi-robot systems. His publication record—anchored by a highly cited 2020 review and a steady stream of methodological and systems papers—has garnered substantial community attention (total citations ≈ 2206). His work demonstrates sustained collaboration across subfields (LfD, dynamical systems, intention estimation, multi-robot tasking) and engagement with both theoretical and practical challenges in robotics. Through the STAR Lab he mentors students and collaborates with industry and academic partners to translate algorithmic advances (e.g., stable skill learning, intention-aware controllers, resilient heterogeneous coordination) into reproducible software and benchmark platforms.

## Overview
Cindy Xiong Bearfield is an **assistant professor** and principal investigator at the Georgia Institute of Technology, where she leads a research group focused on the intersection of **Information Visualization**, **visual perception**, and human-centered decision support. In her lab she investigates how people *see, interpret, and act on* visualized data, with particular emphasis on the role of perceptual and cognitive biases in shaping interpretation, the design of visual encodings and annotations to guide reasoning, and the affective and social dimensions of trust and transparency. Her work centers on <u>perceptual biases in graphical perception</u>, <u>communication breakdowns arising from mismatched mental models</u>, and <u>design interventions to improve data-driven decision making</u>.

---

## Research Areas
Xiong’s research spans several tightly connected areas in information visualization and vision science. She characterizes how low-level perceptual mechanisms and high-level beliefs interact to produce systematic interpretation errors (e.g., perceptual pull and biased average position estimates in bar and line graphs in "Biased average position estimates in line and bar graphs: Underestimation, overestimation, and perceptual pull" (2019) and "Perceiving graphs as ambiguous figures" (2018)). She studies cognitive biases in visual data communication — including the *curse of knowledge* and confirmation/causal reasoning biases — demonstrated in "The Curse of Knowledge in Visual Data Communication" (2019) and "Illusion of causality in visualized data" (2019). Xiong also develops and empirically evaluates design guidelines and interventions to improve comprehension and trust, from decluttering strategies ("Declutter and focus: Empirically evaluating design guidelines for effective data communication" (2021)) to the use and effects of metadata and textual annotations ("From invisible to visible: Impacts of metadata in communicative data visualization" (2022); "The role of text in visualizations..." (2023)). More recent work extends these threads to socio-technical concerns — trust frameworks for visualizations ("Vistrust" (2023); "How do we measure trust..." (2022)), fairness and explainability in ML visualizations ("My model is unfair..." (2023)), and interactions between generative models and provenance/robust watermarking ("Attack-resilient image watermarking using stable diffusion" (2024)). Across these topics she leverages psychophysical methods, crowdsourced behavioral studies, and mixed-methods design evaluations to map perceptual mechanisms to practical visualization tasks.

## Notable Works
- "The Curse of Knowledge in Visual Data Communication" (2019) — Demonstrated how prior salience and priming lead readers to see different, competing patterns in the same chart, revealing a key source of miscommunication between designers and audiences.
- "Illusion of causality in visualized data" (2019) — Empirically documented how common visual presentations of correlational data can induce erroneous causal inferences, and characterized conditions that exacerbate such reasoning errors.
- "How to evaluate data visualizations across different levels of understanding" (2020) — Proposed and validated a multi-level evaluation framework that connects low-level perceptual extraction to higher-level contextual reasoning and decision affordances, guiding more comprehensive empirical studies of visualization effectiveness.

## Academic Background
Cindy Xiong Bearfield completed advanced training in vision science and information visualization, with early affiliations that included research at UMass Amherst before joining the Georgia Institute of Technology as faculty. At Georgia Tech she is active in the visualization and human–computer interaction communities, collaborates broadly with cognitive scientists and ML researchers, and maintains a public research presence (http://cyxiong.com/). Her publication record (total citations: 1,387) reflects consistent contributions to empirical visualization, perceptual methods, and the study of trust and bias in data communication. Xiong has secured competitive research support and recognition for her work, mentors graduate students and postdoctoral researchers in her lab, and serves as a peer reviewer and program contributor to major visualization and HCI venues.
Information Visualization, Visual Perception, Decision Making

## Overview
Abrahim Ladha is a **Lecturer** at **Georgia Tech**, engaged with research and teaching activities that bridge *applied cryptography*, *blockchain engineering*, and *theoretical computer science*. His work is associated with research groups focused on **secure and private computation** and decentralized systems, and centers on building practical mechanisms for <u>auditable and resilient private computation</u> on public blockchains. Across his publications and code releases, Ladha emphasizes the design and implementation of systems that combine **multi-party computation (MPC)**, **garbled circuits/state machines**, and smart-contract-based deployment on platforms such as **Ethereum** to achieve _privacy-preserving_, _verifiable_, and _highly available_ distributed services.

---

## Research Areas
Ladha's research spans the intersection of **cryptographic engineering** and **distributed ledger technology** with a recurring focus on making private computation practical and auditable on public blockchains. His work on "Auditable, available and resilient private computation on the blockchain via MPC" develops techniques for combining <u>MPC protocols</u> with blockchain primitives to provide strong guarantees about availability, verifiability, and confidentiality in hostile or failure-prone environments. The complementary "GABLE" work — both the report and associated Solidity contract releases — explores the use of **garbled state machines and garbled circuits** executed or coordinated via smart contracts to enable private execution on otherwise public infrastructure. In addition to applied systems work, Ladha has contributed to more theoretical and recreational topics in algorithmic game design, as seen in "Exploring mod2 n-queens games", which investigates combinatorial game variants and their combinatorial and computational properties.

---

## Notable Works
- "Auditable, available and resilient private computation on the blockchain via MPC" (2022) — Presents a practical design for integrating **MPC** with blockchain platforms to achieve _auditable_ and _available_ private computation, addressing challenges of verifiability and fault tolerance on public programmable ledgers.
- "Exploring mod2 n-queens games" (2015) — Introduces and analyzes a two-player combinatorial game based on a parity-modified n-queens setup, contributing to the study of parity constraints in placement games and their computational implications.
- "The GABLE report: garbled autonomous bots leveraging Ethereum" (2020) — Describes the **GABLE** approach of using garbled circuits and garbled state machines in conjunction with **Ethereum** smart contracts, and is accompanied by released Solidity contract source code illustrating these techniques.

---

## Academic Background
Ladha's publication record dates back to at least 2015, reflecting a trajectory that covers both theoretical and systems-oriented inquiries. As a **Lecturer at Georgia Tech**, he contributes to the institute's teaching and research missions in areas intersecting security, cryptography, and distributed systems. His GABLE-related materials include a code release tied to work involving Sandia National Laboratories, indicating collaborative or applied ties to government research environments. While formal degree details and specific honors are not listed here, his contributions—spanning peer-reviewed reports, code artifacts, and cross-disciplinary investigations—demonstrate a blend of academic and practical engagement in advancing privacy-preserving computation and blockchain-based system design.

## Overview
Ashutosh Dhekne is a researcher at the **Georgia Institute of Technology**, based in the College of Computing, where he leads and collaborates on projects at the intersection of wireless systems, embedded sensing, and Internet-of-Things research. His lab/group work centers on applying and advancing **ultra-wideband (UWB)** and **wireless sensing** technologies to enable robust *mobility-aware* systems, practical *indoor localization*, and novel sensing modalities for everyday objects. Across his portfolio he emphasizes bridging theory and systems: from low-level radio techniques such as energy- and time-of-flight sensing to applied platforms for sports analytics and first-responder tracking—anchored by key concepts like <u>cross-technology communication</u>, <u>ranging and localization</u>, and <u>radio–inertial sensor fusion</u>.

---

## Research Areas
Dhekne’s research spans several tightly connected areas in wireless systems and sensing. He has pioneered methods for *cross-technology communication and energy sensing* (e.g., Esense) that enable communication between heterogeneous physical layers and devices outside conventional PHY range. He has applied and expanded **ultra-wideband (UWB)** techniques for material and state identification (LiquID’s liquid identification via UWB signal phase and attenuation), for high-precision *indoor localization* and anti-spoofing (UnSpoof, UWB-Auth, Utrack3D, and UWB ranging error metrics), and for compact wearable/object tracking via **UWB–IMU fusion** (ITrackU, PnPLoc, PnPLoc/Utrack3d). Other threads in his work include mobile and robotic wireless infrastructure (drone-based cell-tower extension, robotic WiFi APs), domain-specific IoT platforms and applications (iBall and sports-analytics platforms, LittleBeats infant wearable validations), and practical systems engineering contributions such as TDMA MAC design for rural WiFi meshes and open-source research platforms (uFiμ). Methodologically, his papers contribute new sensing modalities, fusion algorithms, time-difference-of-arrival and time-of-flight formulations, and system designs that prioritize deployability in real-world settings.

---

## Notable Works
- "Esense: Communication through energy sensing" (2009) — Introduced the Esense paradigm for enabling communication between devices with different or incompatible physical layers by interpreting energy profiles, a foundational contribution to cross-technology signaling and low-cost interoperability.
- "LiquID: A Wireless Liquid IDentifier" (2018) — Demonstrated that UWB wireless signals can identify liquids by exploiting distinct slowdown and attenuation signatures in phase and amplitude, opening practical sensing applications for UWB beyond localization.
- "Bringing IoT to Sports Analytics." (2017) — Developed an end-to-end approach (iBall) for embedding inexpensive sensors and radios in sports equipment to capture 3D trajectory and spin, showing how wireless localization and sensing can be applied to sports and real-time analytics.

---

## Academic Background
Ashutosh Dhekne has a sustained publication record dating back to 2009 and a cumulative citation footprint of approximately **1,209** citations, reflecting broad impact across wireless networking, sensing, and IoT. He is affiliated with the Georgia Institute of Technology’s College of Computing and collaborates widely across networking, HCI, robotics, and systems communities. His scholarly output includes peer-reviewed conference and journal papers, patent filings (e.g., an exemplary UWB localization patent), and open-source research artifacts (such as uFiμ), indicating a blend of theoretical contributions, systems implementation, and technology translation. His work has been supported through research collaborations and grants typical for academic-industry and government-sponsored projects in wireless systems and sensing, and he continues to mentor students and lead projects that move UWB and IoT technologies from laboratory prototypes toward practical deployment.

## Overview
HyunJoo Oh is an **Assistant Professor** in the School of **Industrial Design & Interactive Computing** at **Georgia Tech**, where she leads the CodeCraft Group (https://www.codecraft.group/). Her work sits at the intersection of **Human–Computer Interaction**, **digital fabrication**, and **design & craft** practices. HyunJoo pursues a *material-driven*, practice-based research agenda that foregrounds <u>paper- and craft-based computational materials</u>, <u>sustainable and transient electronics</u>, and <u>tangible interaction</u>. Her lab develops new fabrication techniques, computational design tools, and educational kits that enable expressive, low-cost, and often recyclable interactive artifacts.

---

## Research Areas
HyunJoo Oh’s research explores how everyday materials and craft techniques can be repurposed to create interactive systems and learning experiences. She develops methods for embedding sensing, actuation, and power-harvesting into paper and other thin-sheet materials (e.g., SPIN: Self-powered Paper INterfaces; PEP: 3D Printed Electronic Papercrafts), investigates cutting and folding strategies that add mechanical and electrical affordances to rigid and flexible sheets (e.g., kirigami explorations in “Sensing kirigami” and Kirisense), and creates rapid circuit fabrication approaches that make electronics accessible on diverse substrates (e.g., Silver Tape; Duco for large-scale direct-circuit-writing). A recurring theme is designing for sustainability and planned transiency—reimagining device lifetime and recyclability (e.g., “Functional destruction,” Transient IoT, Recy-ctronics)—while also developing tangible toolkits and pedagogy for learners and makers (e.g., FoldMecha, Cube-in, My:Talkies). Her work further addresses haptic and force-feedback design through planar compliant structures (Shape-Haptics, FlexHaptics) and explores texture and 2.5D interactions (SwellSense, Swell by Light), demonstrating a consistent focus on how material properties, craft processes, and computational behaviors co-design novel interactive experiences.

---

## Notable Works
- "Sensing kirigami" (2019) — A material-driven investigation of carbon-coated paper formed into kirigami structures, characterizing the electrical, haptic, and visual affordances of shaped paper and suggesting new tangible interaction modalities.  
- "PEP (3D Printed Electronic Papercrafts): An Integrated Approach for 3D Sculpting Paper-Based Electronic Devices." (2018) — Introduces a suite of design and fabrication techniques to integrate sensing, actuation, display, and other electronic functions into sculpted paper artifacts, expanding the expressive capabilities of paper-based interactive objects.  
- "Silver tape: Inkjet-printed circuits peeled-and-transferred on versatile substrates" (2020) — Proposes a fast, low-barrier fabrication method to transfer inkjet-printed silver traces from paper onto a variety of substrates, enabling rapid prototyping of circuits without complex post-processing.

---

## Academic Background
HyunJoo Oh’s publication record dates back to the early 2010s and spans interactive installations, tangible learning kits, and material-centered HCI research. She is the principal investigator of the CodeCraft Group at Georgia Tech and has published extensively in the HCI and tangible interaction communities, accumulating over **700 citations** for work that frequently appears at leading venues in interactive systems and design. Her portfolio includes both foundational systems and practice-based explorations that bridge design, education, and engineering. HyunJoo’s trajectory combines research, teaching, and community-facing making: she develops curricular toolkits and learning kits for children and novice makers (e.g., Cube-in, FoldMecha), leads student projects in physical computing and material exploration, and contributes to conversations on sustainability and circularity in electronics. At Georgia Tech she supervises graduate and undergraduate researchers, collaborates broadly across design and computing groups, and is active in workshops, committees, and cross-disciplinary initiatives that promote accessible, craft-informed approaches to interactive technology.

## Overview
Santosh S. Vempala is a Professor of Computer Science at the **Georgia Institute of Technology**, where he leads a research group at the intersection of theoretical computer science and data-driven methodology. His group focuses on the design and analysis of efficient algorithms for high-dimensional problems, with particular emphasis on **randomized algorithms**, **geometric and spectral methods**, and **robust foundations of data science**. His work develops both rigorous theory and practical tools for problems in *dimensionality reduction, clustering, sampling, optimization,* and *matrix approximation*, addressing the <u>algorithmic</u> and <u>geometric</u> underpinnings of modern data analysis.

---

## Research Areas
Vempala's research spans several interrelated areas in theoretical computer science and its applications. He has made foundational contributions to the theory and practice of *spectral algorithms* and *latent semantic analysis* (see "Latent semantic indexing: A probabilistic analysis", 1998), and to clustering and multiclass data analysis where spectral methods and bicriteria performance guarantees play a central role ("On clusterings: Good, bad and spectral", 2004). His work on *random projections* and low-rank approximations ("The random projection method", 2005; "Fast Monte-Carlo algorithms for finding low-rank approximations", 2004) establishes efficient dimensionality-reduction techniques with provable guarantees. In high-dimensional geometry and sampling, Vempala has developed deep results on log-concave distributions, random walks, and volume computation ("The geometry of logconcave functions and sampling algorithms", 2007; "Simulated annealing in convex bodies and an O*(n4) volume algorithm", 2006; "Geometric random walks: a survey", 2005). He has also contributed to optimization and online decision-making ("Efficient algorithms for on-line optimization", 2005; "Solving convex programs by random walks", 2004), robust statistics and learning under adversarial noise ("Agnostic estimation of mean and covariance", 2016), and algorithmic lower bounds and complexity for statistical problems ("Statistical algorithms and a lower bound for detecting planted cliques", 2017). Across these topics he blends probabilistic analysis, spectral techniques, and geometric intuition to address both practical algorithms (e.g., matrix and clustering algorithms) and fundamental limits (e.g., statistical query lower bounds, sampling convergence).

---

## Notable Works
- "Latent semantic indexing: A probabilistic analysis" (1998) — A seminal probabilistic analysis explaining when and why **latent semantic indexing (LSI)** successfully captures underlying document-topic structure via spectral methods; this work gave rigorous guarantees for a widely used information-retrieval technique.
- "On clusterings: Good, bad and spectral" (2004) — Introduced a natural bicriteria measure for clustering quality and analyzed simple recursive spectral heuristics, providing poly-logarithmic worst-case guarantees and advancing rigorous understanding of spectral clustering.
- "The geometry of logconcave functions and sampling algorithms" (2007) and related sampling/volume works (2004–2006) — A body of results establishing geometric properties of log-concave densities, rapid mixing of geometric random walks (including hit-and-run), and algorithmic advances for sampling, rounding, integration and convex optimization in high dimensions.

---

## Academic Background
Santosh Vempala is a senior faculty member in the School of Computer Science at the **Georgia Institute of Technology**, where he has built a long-standing program in algorithms, randomness, and the foundations of data science. Over a multi-decade career he has published influential papers across theoretical computer science, machine learning, optimization, and computational biology, accumulating over **26,300 citations**. His work appears in leading venues and journals and has had broad impact on both theory and applied methodology (from information retrieval and clustering to sampling and robust estimation). He is an active mentor of graduate students and postdocs and participates widely in the algorithms and learning communities through conference program committees and invited presentations. More information, publications, and resources from his group are available from his homepage: http://www.cc.gatech.edu/~vempala.

## Overview
Felix J. Herrmann is a **Professor** in the Schools of **Earth and Atmospheric Sciences** and **Computational Science and Engineering** at Georgia Tech, where he leads a research group focused on algorithmic and software foundations for large-scale seismic imaging and inversion. His work spans theoretical and practical aspects of computational geophysics, with particular emphasis on <u>waveform inversion</u>, <u>seismic data reconstruction</u>, and the development of high-performance software for PDE-constrained problems. Herrmann’s group combines **sparsity-promoting methods**, **randomized sampling**, and *high-performance domain-specific languages* (notably **Devito**) to enable scalable solutions for exploration seismology and emerging applications such as *digital twins for geological carbon storage* and **Bayesian inference** for uncertainty quantification.

---

## Research Areas
Herrmann’s research sits at the intersection of applied mathematics, signal processing, and high-performance scientific computing. He has been a leading proponent of using directional multiscale transforms (notably **curvelet** frames) and sparsity priors to address common seismic-data problems such as missing-trace interpolation, denoising, and primary–multiple separation (see e.g., "Non-parametric seismic data recovery with curvelet frames" (2008), "Seismic denoising with nonuniformly sampled curvelets" (2006), and "Curvelet-based seismic data processing: A multiscale and nonlinear approach" (2008)). Complementing sparsity-based approaches, his work develops randomized and compressive strategies for acquisition and inversion—randomized sampling, source-encoding, and compressive simulation—to reduce computational and acquisition costs ("Randomized sampling and sparsity" (2010); "Fast randomized full-waveform inversion with compressive sensing" (2012); "Designing simultaneous acquisitions with compressive sensing" (2009)). Herrmann has also advanced methods for robust and scalable full-waveform inversion (FWI), addressing nonconvexity and robustness via expanded search spaces, penalty and constraint formulations, and heavy‑tailed noise models ("Mitigating local minima in full-waveform inversion by expanding the search space" (2013); "A penalty method for PDE-constrained optimization in inverse problems" (2016); "Robust full-waveform inversion using the Student’s t-distribution" (2011)). On the software and computational side, he has co-led the development of **Devito**, a domain-specific language for automated high-performance finite-difference solvers, and contributed frameworks for symbolic and scalable implementations of inversion algorithms ("Devito (v3.1.0)..." (2019); "Architecture and performance of Devito" (2020); "A large-scale framework for symbolic implementations of seismic inversion algorithms in Julia" (2019)). More recent directions include uncertainty quantification and Bayesian approaches for PDE-constrained inverse problems, machine‑learning surrogates for large-scale PDEs (e.g., Fourier neural operators), and the concept of *digital twins* for geological carbon storage where accurate, fast forward models and quantified uncertainty are essential ("Uncertainty quantification for inverse problems..." (2018); "Model-parallel Fourier neural operators..." (2023)).

## Notable Works
- "Non-parametric seismic data recovery with curvelet frames" (2008) — seminal work demonstrating how **curvelet-based sparsity promotion** can robustly recover missing seismic traces and improve downstream imaging; established an influential paradigm for curvelet-domain data recovery and interpolation.
- "Devito (v3.1.0): an embedded domain-specific language for finite differences and geophysical exploration" (2019) / "Architecture and performance of Devito" (2020) — creation and engineering of **Devito**, enabling automated, high-performance finite-difference solvers that make large-scale FWI and migration workflows tractable and reproducible.
- "Mitigating local minima in full-waveform inversion by expanding the search space" (2013) — methodological advance in FWI that reduces sensitivity to poor starting models by reformulating the inversion search space, improving convergence in complex geological settings.

## Academic Background
Herrmann has an extensive publication record dating back to the late 1990s and is a highly cited authority in computational seismology (≈11,814 citations across his body of work). Based at Georgia Tech, he holds joint appointments bridging **earth sciences** and **computational science & engineering**, reflecting a career that combines rigorous mathematical modeling, algorithm design, and open-source software development. His leadership is evidenced by major community artifacts (Devito, Sparco) and numerous collaborative contributions on compressive acquisition, PDE-constrained optimization, and uncertainty quantification. Herrmann’s work has been supported by multidisciplinary grants and close partnerships across academia and industry, and he is routinely involved in mentoring graduate students and postdocs who go on to contribute to computational geophysics, numerical PDEs, and data-driven earth-system modeling.
