## Overview
Edmond Chow is a professor at the **Georgia Institute of Technology**, where he leads a research group in **high-performance scientific computing** within the College of Computing. His work centers on the development of scalable algorithms and software for large-scale scientific simulations and numerical linear algebra. He is best known for contributions that bridge *algorithm design* and *high-performance implementation*, with particular emphasis on <u>sparse linear algebra</u>, <u>preconditioning</u>, <u>parallel algorithms</u>, and <u>molecular dynamics simulations</u>. His research agenda addresses both theoretical algorithmic advances and practical optimizations for modern many-core and distributed architectures.

---

## Research Areas
Chow's research spans several interlocking areas in computational science. In *molecular dynamics* and biomolecular simulation he has produced highly cited work on scaling MD codes to commodity clusters ("Scalable algorithms for molecular dynamics simulations on commodity clusters", 2006) and on adapting algorithms to special-purpose machines such as Anton ("Millisecond-scale molecular dynamics simulations on Anton", 2009). In numerical linear algebra and preconditioning he has developed and analyzed a family of approximate inverse and incomplete LU techniques (e.g., "Approximate inverse preconditioners via sparse-sparse iterations", 1998; "Experimental study of ILU preconditioners for indefinite matrices", 1997), including modern parallel and GPU-oriented variants ("ParILUT---A new parallel threshold ILU factorization", 2018; "Asynchronous iterative algorithm for computing incomplete factorizations on GPUs", 2015). He has also addressed low-level kernels and performance engineering—optimizing sparse matrix-vector multiplication on many-core processors ("Efficient sparse matrix-vector multiplication on x86-based many-core processors", 2013) and proposing iterative approaches for sparse triangular solves ("Iterative sparse triangular solves for preconditioning", 2015). Other threads include scalable graph algorithms (distributed BFS on BlueGene/L), parallel multigrid and preconditioner library design (hypre), rank-structured kernel matrix methods (H2Pack, SMASH, proxy point techniques), and applications to quantum chemistry and Brownian dynamics where scalable Fock matrix construction and hydrodynamic interaction computations are central.

---

## Notable Works
- "Scalable algorithms for molecular dynamics simulations on commodity clusters" (2006) — introduced algorithmic and implementation techniques that enabled substantially larger and longer MD simulations on commodity hardware, influencing both software (Desmond) and special-purpose machine work.
- "Approximate inverse preconditioners via sparse-sparse iterations" (1998) and related ILU studies (1997–2003) — provided foundational analysis and practical algorithms for robust preconditioning of general sparse and indefinite systems, spawning many parallel and GPU adaptations.
- "Efficient sparse matrix-vector multiplication on x86-based many-core processors" (2013) and subsequent kernel-focused papers — identified practical bottlenecks and optimizations for SpMV and triangular solves on modern architectures, informing high-performance implementations used in iterative solvers and simulation codes.

---

## Academic Background
Edmond Chow is a senior faculty researcher at Georgia Tech with a long-standing record of interdisciplinary collaboration across academia, national laboratories, and industry (including collaborations with DE Shaw Research and large-scale computing initiatives). His publication portfolio exceeds 11,500 citations, reflecting broad impact in *scientific computing*, *numerical methods*, and *high-performance computing*. He has contributed to major community venues (including SC proceedings and foundational software efforts such as hypre and H2Pack), mentored students and postdocs, and played active roles in advancing scalable solver technology and performance engineering for emerging computer architectures.

## Overview
Elizabeth Qian is a researcher at the **Georgia Institute of Technology**, where she leads a research group focused on advancing *scientific machine learning* and computational methods for complex engineering systems. Her work centers on the development of principled, data-driven reduced-order models and surrogate modeling strategies that combine physics knowledge with modern learning techniques. She is best known for contributions such as **Lift & Learn** and operator-inference approaches that expose <u>low-dimensional structure</u> and <u>polynomial dynamical representations</u> in large-scale systems. Her research agenda emphasizes robust, scalable methods for <u>model reduction</u>, <u>multifidelity methods</u>, and <u>uncertainty quantification</u>, with applications ranging from turbulent flow simulation to multidisciplinary system design.

---

## Research Areas
Elizabeth Qian’s research spans several tightly connected areas at the intersection of applied mathematics, computational engineering, and machine learning. A central theme is *physics-informed model reduction*: methods that exploit governing equations to learn efficient surrogate models for high-dimensional nonlinear partial differential equations (e.g., the Lift & Learn framework that identifies lifting transformations to reveal quadratic structure). She has advanced both projection-based and data-driven approaches — notably **Operator Inference** and its variants (including energy-preserving and streaming formulations) — to produce stable, interpretable reduced operators for time-dependent dynamics. Another major thread is <u>multifidelity and uncertainty quantification</u>: she has developed multifidelity Monte Carlo estimators for variance and sensitivity indices and multifidelity regression techniques to learn from scarce data. Her work also addresses *Bayesian inverse problems and likelihood-informed model reduction*, ensemble Kalman approaches for inverse estimation, and practical cost-accuracy trade-offs in operator learning, aiming to make many-query tasks (optimization, control, design, and inference) tractable for large-scale engineering systems. Early-career contributions in autonomous systems and scalable parallel-in-time simulation illustrate her breadth and ability to translate methods across domains.

---

## Notable Works
- "Lift & learn: Physics-informed machine learning for large-scale nonlinear dynamical systems" (2020) — Introduced the **Lift & Learn** paradigm that uses known governing equations to identify coordinate transformations in which dynamics admit quadratic structure, enabling accurate, low-dimensional models for large-scale nonlinear systems; this paper is a cornerstone of her scientific machine learning program.
- "Autonomous battery swapping system for small-scale helicopters" (2010) — Early applied work demonstrating a practical autonomous system for UAV fleet management, highlighting systems-integration expertise and early interests in autonomy and reliable operation of unmanned vehicles.
- "The cost-accuracy trade-off in operator learning with neural networks" (2022) and "Multifidelity Monte Carlo estimation of variance and sensitivity indices" (2018) — Together these works quantify practical trade-offs in surrogate modeling: the former analyzes computational cost versus predictive accuracy for operator learning approaches, while the latter develops multifidelity Monte Carlo techniques for efficient uncertainty and sensitivity estimation in expensive simulations.

---

## Academic Background
Elizabeth Qian’s publication record (spanning from applied autonomy work in 2010 to contemporary advances in scientific machine learning and model reduction) indicates a deep training in engineering and computational science, with progressive specialization in reduced-order modeling, multifidelity methods, and uncertainty quantification. Based at the **Georgia Institute of Technology**, she leads an active research group and has authored multiple highly cited papers (total citations: **1056**), reflecting substantial impact across computational engineering and applied mathematics communities. Her trajectory shows a shift from systems-level experimental and autonomy research to foundational methodological contributions that integrate physics, numerical analysis, and machine learning — work that has been adopted in many-query applications such as PDE-constrained optimization, Bayesian inference, and large-scale simulation.

## Overview
Richard DeMillo is a **Professor of Computing** in the **College of Computing** at the **Georgia Institute of Technology**, where he leads work at the intersection of *software engineering*, *security*, and *educational innovation*. Over a career stretching back to the 1970s, his research has combined rigorous **theoretical computer science** with practical systems and tools; signature themes include <u>mutation analysis</u>, <u>automatic test-data generation</u>, <u>dynamic debugging and slicing</u>, and <u>fault-based attacks on cryptographic protocols</u>. His scholarship has influenced both academic research and industrial practice, and he has extended his interests into *higher-education reform and blended learning*. (Homepage: https://www.cc.gatech.edu/people/richard-demillo)

---

## Research Areas
DeMillo’s work spans several tightly connected areas in computer science. In **software testing and quality assurance** he pioneered and systematized <u>mutation analysis</u> and developed practical tools and environments (for example, the *Mothra* line of work and associated toolsets) to measure test adequacy and generate effective test suites — see papers such as "Mutation Analysis." and "An extended overview of the Mothra software testing environment." He advanced *constraint-based automatic test data generation*, formalizing ways to encode fault-detection conditions as algebraic constraints and building systems that produce test inputs to reveal specific classes of faults ("Constraint-based automatic test data generation"; "Experimental results from an automatic test case generator"). In **debugging and program understanding** he introduced and evaluated techniques for <u>dynamic slicing</u> and execution backtracking to localize faults more efficiently ("Debugging with dynamic slicing and backtracking"; "An execution-backtracking approach to debugging"). In **cryptography and security**, DeMillo helped expose a critical class of vulnerabilities arising from hardware and implementation faults, demonstrating how random faults can be used to break protocol implementations (e.g., "On the importance of checking cryptographic protocols for faults"; "On the importance of eliminating errors in cryptographic computations"). Beyond core technical contributions, he has addressed **higher-education transformation**, writing on how technology and new pedagogies can reshape access and cost ("Abelard to Apple: The fate of American colleges and universities"; "Revolution in higher education: How a small band of innovators will make college accessible and affordable"; "Blended learning in practice").

## Notable Works
- "Hints on test data selection: Help for the practicing programmer" (2006) — A highly cited synthesis of empirical and theoretical insights about test selection and the *coupling effect*, which shaped practical approaches to designing test suites that detect both simple and complex software faults. (2958 citations)
- "On the importance of checking cryptographic protocols for faults" (1997) — A foundational theoretical and practical treatment showing how *random hardware faults* can be exploited to break implementations of RSA, Rabin, and several authentication protocols, highlighting the need for robust fault-checking in cryptographic systems. (2623 citations)
- "Constraint-based automatic test data generation" (1991) — Introduced a mutation- and constraint-driven methodology for automatically generating test inputs tailored to reveal specific fault classes and provided tools and experimental validation of the approach. (1196 citations)

## Overview
Bobbie Lynn Eicher is a researcher based in the College of Computing at the **Georgia Institute of Technology**, working with interdisciplinary teams that bridge **artificial intelligence**, **cognitive science**, and *online education*. Her work centers on designing and evaluating AI systems and educational practices that scale to large student populations while preserving human-centered values. She is particularly known for projects that combine empirical evaluation with design—most notably contributions to the virtual teaching assistant **Jill Watson**—and for advancing concepts such as <u>mutual theory of mind</u>, <u>AI ethics grounded in empirical studies</u>, and <u>assessment and pedagogy at scale</u>.

---

## Research Areas
Eicher’s research spans the intersection of **AI and education**, with recurring emphases on the ethical deployment of automated agents, the cognitive foundations of human–AI collaboration, and the design of assessments and student supports for large-scale online programs. From empirical studies of a deployed virtual teaching assistant ("Jill Watson doesn't care if you're pregnant: Grounding AI ethics in empirical studies" (2018)) she investigates how automation affects student experience and instructor responsibilities. Her work on <u>mutual theory of mind</u> and related cognitive-science grounding ("Toward mutual theory of mind as a foundation for co-creation" (2017); "Giving AI a Theory of Mind" (2018)) explores how AI systems can model and communicate intentions to facilitate co-creation and tutoring. In the domain of online graduate education she analyzes student motivations, course reviews, syllabi, and assessment practices at scale ("Enrollment motivations in an online graduate CS program" (2020); "Components of assessments and grading at scale" (2021); "Taking stock of MOOCs and credit substitutability" (2022)), attending to demographic differences, equity implications, and the limits of automated evaluation. More recent work focuses on peer review quality and intelligent feedback mechanisms ("Intelligent Feedback and Evaluation for the Assessment and Improvement of Student Peer Reviews" (2024)) and broadening access to research experiences for online graduate students.

---

## Notable Works
- "Jill Watson doesn't care if you're pregnant: Grounding AI ethics in empirical studies" (2018) — an influential empirical examination of ethics in deployed educational AI, documenting real-world policy, transparency, and trust issues that arise when virtual teaching assistants are used at scale.  
- "Enrollment motivations in an online graduate CS program: Trends & gender-and age-based differences" (2020) — a quantitative and qualitative study that identifies distinct motivations across cohorts and demographic groups, informing program design and outreach for online CS degrees.  
- "Toward mutual theory of mind as a foundation for co-creation" (2017) — a theoretical and applied contribution linking cognitive-science concepts of theory of mind to the design of collaborative AI and intelligent tutoring systems.

## Overview
Srijan Kumar is an **Assistant Professor** of **Computer Science** at **Georgia Tech**, where he leads a research group focused on developing algorithmic and empirical methods at the intersection of **AI**, **Machine Learning**, and **Data Mining**. His work centers on *representation learning for dynamic systems*, with particular emphasis on modeling and predicting the behavior of users, items, and communities in online platforms. He has made influential contributions to understanding and mitigating online harms—such as <u>misinformation</u>, <u>fraudulent behavior</u>, and <u>abusive or toxic interactions</u>—and to the study of evolving graph-structured data, including <u>temporal interaction networks</u> and <u>signed networks</u>. His scholarship is widely cited (total citations: 9,437) and spans theoretical model design, benchmark creation, dataset releases, and applied studies of social media phenomena.

---

## Research Areas
Kumar’s research spans several tightly connected areas: (1) dynamic and temporal graph learning, where he develops methods for capturing evolving node and edge representations (e.g., "Predicting Dynamic Embedding Trajectory in Temporal Interaction Networks" (2019)); (2) analysis and modeling of signed and weighted relations in social systems, including edge weight prediction and continuous-time signed network embeddings ("Edge Weight Prediction in Weighted Signed Networks" (2016); "Signed link representation in continuous-time dynamic signed networks" (2022)); (3) online safety, trust, and deception—studies on misinformation, hoaxes, hate speech, sockpuppetry, and fraud detection that combine behavioral analysis with machine learning ("False information on web and social media: A survey" (2018); "Rev2: Fraudulent user prediction in rating platforms" (2018); "An Army of Me: Sockpuppets in Online Discussion Communities" (2017)); (4) robustness, adversarial attacks and defenses for graph and sequence models, including temporal adversarial attacks and personalized text-generation attacks ("Temporal dynamics-aware adversarial attacks on discrete-time dynamic graph models" (2023); "PETGEN" (2021)); and (5) applied evaluation and benchmarks for modern language and multimodal models with implications for safety and healthcare (recent contributions include work on LLM behavior for healthcare queries and multimodal safety guardrails). Across these topics he combines deep learning (including graph neural networks and transformers), careful temporal modeling, dataset/benchmark construction, and measurement studies of platform-scale social phenomena.

---

## Notable Works
- Predicting dynamic representations for interaction data: "Predicting Dynamic Embedding Trajectory in Temporal Interaction Networks" (2019) introduced methods to learn evolving embeddings of users and items from sequential interactions, enabling better prediction in domains such as e-commerce and social platforms and advancing the study of continuous-time dynamic graph representation learning.  
- Modeling signed and weighted social relations: "Edge Weight Prediction in Weighted Signed Networks" (2016) addressed the problem of predicting positive and negative weighted ties, providing modeling and inference techniques to capture trust/distrust and like/dislike relationships in social networks.  
- Characterizing and combating online false information: "False information on web and social media: A survey" (2018) synthesized empirical findings and detection approaches for misinformation and disinformation, framing the research agenda for automated detection, platform interventions, and human–machine collaboration against false content.

## Overview
Jun Xu is a Professor of Computer Science at the Georgia Institute of Technology, where he leads a research group within the College of Computing focused on the intersection of **computer networking** and **algorithmic foundations**. His work spans both theory and systems: he develops *efficient streaming algorithms*, designs practical network measurement and monitoring systems, and evaluates performance at scale. Central to his research are <u>per-flow traffic measurement</u>, <u>compact data structures for high-speed packet processing</u>, and <u>robust defenses and tracing techniques for network security</u>.

---

## Research Areas
Jun Xu’s research bridges **network algorithmics**, **data streaming algorithms**, and applied **performance evaluation** for real-world networks. He has pioneered compact sketching and bloom-filter variants for high-speed measurement (e.g., the Space-code Bloom Filter), developed streaming techniques for estimating flow size distributions and entropy of traffic to enable anomaly detection and traffic classification, and proposed packet-logging and traceback schemes to mitigate and localize DDoS attacks. His work also covers traffic matrix inference, flow- and counter-architecture designs (BRICK and related counter arrays), prefix-preserving anonymization of traffic traces, and scalable query/routing strategies for peer-to-peer networks. More recently, he has applied machine learning and syslog analysis to datacenter reliability (failure diagnosis and prediction), and explored algorithmic problems in representative databases, skylines, and approximate nearest neighbor search — demonstrating a consistent pattern of translating algorithmic insight into deployable network measurement and management tools.

---

## Notable Works
- "Space-code bloom filter for efficient traffic flow measurement" (2003) — Introduced the Space-code Bloom Filter, a memory- and computation-efficient sketch for accurate per-flow measurement at wire speeds, enabling finer-grained accounting and anomaly detection in high-speed networks.
- "Data streaming algorithms for efficient and accurate estimation of flow size distribution" (2004) — Developed streaming estimators and sampling strategies that substantially improve accuracy of flow-size distribution inference from limited observations, with applications to traffic engineering and workload characterization.
- "IP traceback-based intelligent packet filtering: A novel technique for defending against Internet DDoS attacks" (2003) — Proposed an active traceback-driven filtering approach that mitigates ongoing DDoS attacks by combining traceback information with selective packet filtering, contributing both practical defense mechanisms and theoretical foundations for large-scale traceback.

---

His publication record shows a consistent pattern of collaborative, interdisciplinary research connecting theoretical algorithm design with practical network measurement, security, and performance evaluation. He has supervised graduate students and collaborated with industry and academic partners on measurement-driven projects, and his contributions have been widely adopted and cited across the networking research community.

## Overview
Francisco Romero is a researcher at the **Georgia Institute of Technology**, working at the intersection of **computer systems**, **computer architecture**, *databases*, and **AI**. His work is situated within Georgia Tech’s systems and architecture communities and is centered on designing scalable, cost‑efficient platforms for ML-driven applications. Romero’s research emphasizes <u>automated, model‑agnostic inference serving</u>, <u>serverless and transient execution</u> for large‑scale workloads, and <u>performance‑aware video analytics</u>, combining systems‑level engineering with algorithmic and scheduling innovations to make ML and data systems easier to deploy and operate.

---

## Research Areas
Romero’s research spans practical and theoretical problems in modern infrastructure for AI and data processing. He has led work on inference serving that removes the need for exhaustive model‑variant tuning—pioneering approaches to *model‑less* selection and management (see “{INFaaS}: Automated model-less inference serving” and “A case for managed and model-less inference serving”). He explores serverless and transient execution paradigms to outsource everyday jobs to massively parallel, ephemeral containers and functions (e.g., “From laptop to lambda: Outsourcing everyday jobs to thousands of transient functional containers” and “Faa $ t: A transparent auto-scaling cache for serverless applications”), addressing latency, cost, and state management challenges. In heterogeneous and multi‑tenant environments he develops interference‑aware and online scheduling techniques to improve utilization and meet latency SLOs (e.g., “Mage: Online and interference-aware scheduling for multi-scale heterogeneous systems” and “Interference-aware scheduling for inference serving”). Another major thread is video analytics systems design—declarative model relationships, pipeline auto‑tuning, and vision‑language integration—expressed in projects such as “Llama: A heterogeneous & serverless framework for auto-tuning video analytics pipelines”, “Optimizing video analytics with declarative model relationships”, “VIVA: An End-to-End System for Interactive Video Analytics”, and “Zelda: Video analytics using vision-language models”. More recently, Romero has extended these systems perspectives to latency‑critical model selection and novel interfaces for IoT/LLM fusion (e.g., “Model selection for latency-critical inference serving” and “Flash-Fusion: Enabling Expressive, Low-Latency Queries on IoT Sensor Streams with LLMs”).

## Notable Works
- {INFaaS}: Automated model-less inference serving (2021, 453 citations) — Introduced a model‑agnostic inference serving design that automates selection among model variants to optimize cost and latency without requiring manual per‑model tuning, significantly improving ease‑of‑use and operational efficiency for large‑scale inference deployments.  
- From laptop to lambda: Outsourcing everyday jobs to thousands of transient functional containers (2019, 306 citations) — Demonstrated gg, a framework and CLI tools that harness cloud function services to parallelize common developer tasks across thousands of transient containers, achieving near‑interactive completion times for traditionally long‑running jobs.  
- Faa $ t: A transparent auto-scaling cache for serverless applications (2021, 179 citations) — Proposed a transparent caching layer tailored for FaaS platforms that enables stateful, low‑latency serverless applications by auto‑scaling cache capacity and hiding state management complexity from developers.

## Academic Background
Romero’s publication record, stretching from interdisciplinary applications in psycholinguistics and behavioral coding (2015) to cutting‑edge systems and AI platforms (2018–2025), reflects a trajectory through both applied ML and systems research. He is affiliated with the **Georgia Institute of Technology** and collaborates broadly across computer architecture, databases, and AI research communities. His work has accrued over *1,400 citations* (total citations: **1486**), indicating strong community impact across conference and journal venues. ## Overview
Judy Hoffman is an **Associate Professor** in Computer Science at **UC Irvine**, where she leads a research group focused on advancing *robust and transferable visual representations*. Her lab develops methods that address real-world distribution shifts through a combination of **adversarial learning**, *domain adaptation*, and efficient deep architectures. Across foundational algorithmic contributions and large-scale benchmarks, her work targets the problem of creating <u>domain-invariant</u> and <u>transferable</u> models that operate reliably across simulated and real environments, across modalities, and under resource constraints.

---

## Research Areas
Hoffman's research spans **computer vision**, **deep learning**, and **machine learning** with a concentration on methods for cross-domain generalization and practical deployment. She has produced influential work on <u>unsupervised and multi-source domain adaptation</u> (e.g., adversarial approaches that align source and target distributions), on strategies for <u>transferring supervision across modalities</u> (cross-modal distillation and modality hallucination), and on building benchmarks and datasets that drive progress in transfer learning (the VisDA challenges and synthetic-to-real benchmarks). Her papers also explore efficient model design (Token Merging for faster transformers), dense-prediction adaptation (FCNs in the wild), sim-to-real transfer for robotics and visuomotor control, and fairness and evaluation in detection systems (predictive inequity and the TIDE toolbox). Across these topics she emphasizes both algorithmic formulations—such as adversarial discriminative domain adaptation and cycle-consistent adaptation—and practical evaluation on large-scale synthetic and real datasets.

## Notable Works
- "Adversarial discriminative domain adaptation" (2017) — introduced a practical adversarial framework (ADDA) for unsupervised domain adaptation that learns discriminative, domain-aligned representations via adversarial training, substantially influencing subsequent adversarial alignment methods.  
- "Decaf: A deep convolutional activation feature for generic visual recognition" (2013) — demonstrated that deep convolutional activations trained on large supervised tasks can serve as *generic feature extractors* for many downstream vision problems, helping to establish transfer learning best practices in vision.  
- "Cycada: Cycle-consistent adversarial domain adaptation" (2018) — combined cycle-consistent image-to-image translation with feature- and pixel-level adversarial adaptation to tackle synthetic-to-real and cross-domain transfer problems, unifying ideas of representation alignment and input-space translation for improved domain generalization.

## Academic Background
Judy Hoffman is an Associate Professor at UC Irvine and maintains an active group webpage (http://judyhoffman.io/) documenting her publications and projects. Her publication record—totaling over **37,948 citations**—reflects a sustained impact on the fields of *domain adaptation*, *transfer learning*, and *robust visual representation learning*. She has driven both methodological advances (adversarial and cycle-consistent adaptation, modality distillation, token-merging for efficient transformers) and community resources (VisDA benchmarks and synthetic-to-real evaluation suites). Her work has bridged theory and application, informing robotics (sim-to-real visuomotor transfer), large-scale dataset challenges, and fairness analyses for object detection. Through high-impact papers, benchmark contributions, and cross-disciplinary collaborations, she is a recognized leader in building models and tools that make vision systems more generalizable, efficient, and reliable.

## Overview
Ahmed Saeed is an **Assistant Professor** in the College of Computing at **Georgia Tech**, where he leads a research group in *networking and systems* (see his homepage: https://www.cc.gatech.edu/~amsmti3). His work sits at the intersection of **computer networks**, **systems**, and *mobile computing*, with a sustained emphasis on <u>device-free passive localization</u>, <u>traffic shaping and congestion control</u>, and <u>scalable network/system mechanisms for datacenters and wide-area networks</u>. Saeed's research combines measurement-driven systems-building, algorithmic design, and formal/automated reasoning to produce practical, deployable techniques for wireless environments, datacenter stacks, and next-generation networking platforms.

---

## Research Areas
Saeed's body of work spans several tightly related areas in networking and systems. In wireless sensing and localization he has pioneered work on **device-free passive (DfP) localization** and motion detection—most notably developing large-scale systems that exploit commodity WLAN infrastructure (e.g., "Nuzzer", "RASID", and "Ichnaea") to localize or detect people who do not carry devices. In networked systems and datacenter networking he has investigated scalable end-host mechanisms for traffic shaping and packet scheduling (e.g., "Carousel", the time-indexed traffic-shaping patents, and "Eiffel"), and explored the interaction between congestion control, overload control, and core scheduling for µs-scale RPCs and microservice fabrics (e.g., "Overload Control for µs-scale RPCs with Breakwater", "Annulus", "Protego", and recent work on joint core scheduling and overload control). He also contributes to formal and automated methods for networking—both tooling and verification—through work such as "Toward formally verifying congestion control behavior" and NSF-style collaborative projects on behavioral analysis of network control protocols. Complementary research threads include mobile visual sensing and UAV-enabled testbeds ("Argus", "Up and away", target coverage papers), spectrum/white-space awareness and geo-database studies, and recent investigations into the performance and modeling of Low Earth Orbit (LEO) satellite networks.

---

## Notable Works
- "Nuzzer: A large-scale device-free passive localization system for wireless environments" (2012) — introduced a practical, WLAN-based approach to localize and track entities without attached devices, demonstrating how commodity WiFi infrastructure can be repurposed for large-scale *device-free passive (DfP) localization* (450 citations).
- "RASID: A robust WLAN device-free passive motion detection system" (2012) — developed robust algorithms and system techniques for motion detection using existing wireless deployments, advancing the reliability and applicability of DfP systems in realistic environments (296 citations).
- "Carousel: Scalable traffic shaping at end hosts" (2017) — proposed end-host, scalable traffic-shaping designs that enable practical pacing and rate-limiting for both datacenter and WAN traffic, influencing subsequent work on time-indexed shaping structures and scalable sender-side mechanisms (180 citations).

---

## Academic Background
Ahmed Saeed is an Assistant Professor at Georgia Tech's College of Computing, where he directs a research group working on networking and systems. His publication record (total citations ≈ 1,922) reflects long-term collaborations across wireless sensing, datacenter networking, congestion/overload control, and formal analysis of network algorithms. He has been PI/co-PI on multi-institutional research projects (including NSF-style "Collaborative Research: CNS Core" efforts) and holds multiple systems and method disclosures related to scalable traffic shaping. Prior to his faculty appointment he conducted extensive research in mobile and wireless networking and systems (details and CV available on his homepage). Saeed maintains active collaborations with academic and industry partners on problems ranging from UAV-enabled sensing and TV white-space awareness to LEO-satellite network characterization and high-performance end-host network stacks.

## Overview
Alberto Dainotti is a senior researcher and faculty member in the College of Computing at **Georgia Tech**, where he leads a research group focused on *large-scale Internet measurement, network systems, and security*. His work centers on developing practical measurement platforms and analysis techniques to understand and improve the robustness of the global Internet. Key themes in his research include <u>traffic classification</u>, <u>realistic traffic generation and measurement</u>, <u>Internet outages and censorship</u>, and <u>BGP and routing security</u>. He is the author and maintainer of several widely used open-source tools and datasets that bridge the gap between academic measurement and operational practice (see his homepage: https://www.cc.gatech.edu/~adainotti6/).

---

## Research Areas
Dainotti’s research spans three tightly coupled areas: Internet measurement, networking systems, and security. In the domain of **traffic classification**, he has produced influential survey and methodology work (e.g., “Issues and future directions in traffic classification” and “Reviewing traffic classification”), developed hybrid classification approaches such as *PortLoad*, and explored early and multi-classification strategies to improve accuracy and timeliness. In the area of **traffic generation and active measurement**, he is a primary developer of D-ITG (“Multi-protocol and multi-platform traffic generation and measurement” and the D-ITG manual) and has critically examined the reliability of software-based generators (“Do you trust your software-based traffic generator?”). For **Internet-wide security and resilience**, his work characterizes large-scale threats—ranging from botnet-driven scans (“Analysis of a"/0" Stealth Scan from a Botnet”) and DoS ecosystems (“Millions of targets under attack”) to political and operational disruptions (“Analysis of country-wide internet outages caused by censorship”, “Destination unreachable: improving inference of IPv4 address space utilization”, and studies of DNS and BGP behaviors). He has also advanced operationally-minded defenses and tooling for routing security (e.g., *BGPStream*, *ARTEMIS*) and researched mitigation and measurement techniques such as blackholing, CGN inference, and anycast measurement.

---

## Notable Works
- "Issues and future directions in traffic classification" (2012) — A comprehensive synthesis of challenges and research directions that shaped subsequent work on automated and hybrid classification methods; widely cited as a reference point for both *researchers* and *operators*.  
- "A tool for the generation of realistic network workload for emerging networking scenarios" (2012) — Foundational work on realistic traffic generation (D-ITG), providing a flexible platform for reproducing multidimensional Internet workloads used by the research community for experiments and validation.  
- "Analysis of country-wide internet outages caused by censorship" (2011) — A pioneering measurement study that combined multiple large-scale data sources to characterize government-ordered and event-driven connectivity disruptions, informing later work on shutdown detection and political uses of network interference.

---

## Academic Background
Alberto Dainotti is a long-standing contributor to the Internet measurement and security communities with a prolific publication record (total citations: **6673**) and a track record of producing reusable software and datasets that serve both academic and operational audiences (notably D-ITG, BGPStream, and related tools). His body of work reflects sustained collaboration with network operators, measurement platforms, and interdisciplinary teams addressing policy and political dimensions of network disruption (e.g., studies of censorship and election-related DoS). He has helped shape community practice through open-source releases, engineering-focused papers (e.g., tool manuals and frameworks), and involvement in conference organization and program activities (e.g., lessons from organizing PAM 2020). His affiliations with Georgia Tech’s College of Computing position him at the intersection of systems design, empirical measurement, and Internet security research.

## Overview
Anqi Wu is an **Assistant Professor** in the School of **Computational Science and Engineering** at **Georgia Tech**, where she leads a research group that develops principled machine learning methods for problems at the interface of *computational and statistical neuroscience*, computer vision for behavior, and sequential decision-making. Her work centers on scalable probabilistic modeling and representation learning for neural and behavioral data, with particular emphasis on <u>latent variable models</u>, <u>Gaussian processes</u>, and <u>Bayesian uncertainty quantification</u>. She combines tools from **variational inference**, **graphical models**, and **deep learning** to extract low-dimensional structure from high-dimensional neural recordings and behavioral videos and to build interpretable models of neural dynamics and behavior.

---

## Research Areas
Anqi Wu’s research spans methods and applications that bridge modern machine learning and systems neuroscience. Methodologically she has advanced **variational inference** for deep probabilistic models (see her work on *deterministic variational inference for robust Bayesian neural networks*), developed novel Gaussian process formulations for discovering nonlinear latent structure in multivariate spike trains, and exploited gradient/Hessian information in Bayesian optimization and quadrature. In the domain of neural data analysis she has focused on the extraction and evaluation of low-dimensional neural dynamics—both by proposing new latent variable models (e.g., GP-based nonlinear latent models and GP-RNN hybrids) and by convening community benchmarks for model evaluation (the *Neural Latents Benchmark ’21*). On the behavioral side her group has contributed semi-supervised and graph-based deep models for animal pose and behavioral video analysis (e.g., *Deep Graph Pose*, semi-supervised VAEs for behavioral video variability), and she has extended these modeling ideas to problems in inverse reinforcement learning and reward discovery for characterizing animal and robot behavior. Across topics a consistent theme is principled uncertainty modeling, scalability to large recordings or video datasets, and an emphasis on interpretable, low-dimensional representations of complex biological signals.

---

## Notable Works
- "Deterministic variational inference for robust bayesian neural networks" (2018) — introduced a deterministic variational approach that improves robustness and scalability of **Bayesian neural networks**, making uncertainty-aware deep models more practical for real-world tasks and influencing subsequent work on reliable variational methods.  
- "Gaussian process based nonlinear latent structure discovery in multivariate spike train data" (2017) — proposed a doubly nonlinear latent variable model using **Gaussian processes** to extract smooth, nonlinear latent trajectories from multi-neuron spike data, advancing methods for uncovering <u>latent neural dynamics</u>.  
- "Neural latents benchmark'21: evaluating latent variable models of neural population activity" (2021) — led/participated in a community evaluation that established standardized tasks and metrics for comparing latent variable models of neural population activity, improving reproducibility and benchmarking practices in computational neuroscience.

---

## Academic Background
Anqi Wu’s publication record dates back to at least 2011 and demonstrates a long-standing interdisciplinary trajectory across machine learning, time-series classification, and computational neuroscience. She currently holds a faculty appointment in **Computational Science and Engineering** at **Georgia Tech**, where she directs a research group focused on probabilistic modeling of neural and behavioral data. Her scholarship (over 1,300 citations) includes contributions to both foundational methodology (variational inference, Gaussian processes, structured sparsity) and neuroscience-driven applications (spike-train analysis, neural dynamics, behavioral video analysis, and inverse reinforcement learning). Her body of work reflects sustained collaborations across machine learning, neuroscience, and robotics communities, and she is recognized for bringing rigorous, scalable probabilistic tools to core problems in neural data science and behavior modeling.

Jennifer G. Kim is a faculty researcher at the **Georgia Institute of Technology**, leading a research group that operates at the intersection of **Human‑Computer Interaction (HCI)** and **Computer‑Supported Cooperative Work (CSCW)**. Her lab conducts *design‑oriented, empirically driven* research that centers on technologies to support people with diverse cognitive and communication needs, with particular emphasis on supporting <u>independence</u> and workplace inclusion for autistic and neurodivergent individuals. Her work combines user‑centered design, mixed‑methods evaluation, and computational approaches to investigate <u>neurodiversity in the workplace</u>, <u>virtual reality (VR) and multimodal sensing</u>, and <u>social dynamics in online support systems</u>.

---

## Research Areas
Kim’s scholarship spans complementary streams within HCI/CSCW. One major thread examines technologies that foster independent living and successful transitions to adulthood for youth on the autism spectrum—this includes work on social network features and interactive systems that scaffold life‑skills practice (e.g., SocialMirror and circle‑based SNS designs) and person‑centered transition planning. A second area investigates workplace inclusion and training, where she explores how VR training, physiological sensing, and representational design can improve understanding and accommodation of autistic employees (e.g., VR workplace playbooks, VISTA, and self‑efficacy studies). A third strand interrogates online health and support ecosystems: Kim has analyzed credibility and *social translucence* in medical crowdfunding, identity presentation by beneficiaries, and how collective endorsements and non‑monetary contributions shape participation. Methodologically, her publications draw on <u>co‑design</u>, longitudinal and in‑situ sensing (skin conductance, multimodal sensor fusion), *mixed‑methods* evaluation, visual analytics (BEDA, V‑DAT), and increasingly, investigations into the role of AI and LLMs for sensitive support domains (e.g., chatbots for eating disorder recovery and LLM‑assisted job support). Across these areas she consistently integrates participatory design with rigorous qualitative and quantitative analysis.

---

## Notable Works
- "Designing a social network to support the independence of young adults with autism" (2012, 118 citations) — formative design and evaluation of SNS features that scaffold life‑skill independence, shaping subsequent work on circle‑based supports and SocialMirror.
- "The power of collective endorsements: credibility factors in medical crowdfunding campaigns" (2016, 90 citations) — a mixed‑methods examination of how endorsements and social signals affect credibility, belonging, and donor behavior in online medical fundraising.
- "Personalized academic research paper recommendation system" (2013, 83 citations) — a computational and user‑centered approach to paper recommendation that addresses discovery challenges faced by researchers amid growing publication volumes.

---

## Academic Background
Jennifer G. Kim has a sustained publication record in top HCI and CSCW venues since the early 2010s and a cumulative citation impact (806 total citations) reflecting both theoretical and applied influence across disability, health, and CSCW research communities. Her training is grounded in computing and human‑centered disciplines, and her work is characterized by interdisciplinary collaboration with clinicians, educators, rehabilitation specialists, and designers. She has held research and academic appointments culminating in her current role at Georgia Tech, and her projects have attracted attention for their translational impact on assistive technologies, workplace inclusion, and digital health communities. Kim’s contributions have been recognized through competitive peer review, invited talks, and collaborative grants (nationally and across academic–clinical partnerships), and she maintains an active public portfolio and lab description on her homepage.

## Overview
Richard Fujimoto is a **Regents' Professor** in the College of Computing at the **Georgia Institute of Technology**, where he leads a long-standing research program and group focused on *modeling and simulation*. His work centers on the theory, algorithms, and systems that enable large-scale, high-performance simulation, with particular emphasis on <u>parallel and distributed simulation</u>, <u>parallel discrete event simulation (PDES)</u>, and techniques for achieving scalable, correct execution such as <u>optimistic synchronization (Time Warp)</u> and <u>reverse computation</u>. He has also extended simulation research into applied domains including <u>network and vehicular systems</u>, the design of runtime infrastructure for federated simulations (e.g., the High Level Architecture), and the exploitation of modern computing platforms such as cloud and heterogeneous multiprocessor environments.

---

## Research Areas
Fujimoto's research spans both foundational and applied areas of simulation. On the foundations side he has advanced the theory and practice of **parallel discrete event simulation**, exploring both conservative and optimistic synchronization approaches (notably *Time Warp*), algorithms for computing global virtual time, and performance/analytical models of PDES. He has promoted alternative rollback methods such as *reverse computation* to reduce state-saving overhead and contributed hardware- and software-oriented implementations (e.g., the GTW time warp system and proposals for a rollback chip). In distributed simulation and interoperability he has been a leading voice on the **DoD High Level Architecture (HLA)**, contributing work on time management, repeatability, and RTI design. On the systems and applications side, his work addresses the scalability of packet- and packet-level network simulation, federated approaches to simulating very large networks, and data dissemination and analytical propagation models for **vehicular (V2V) networks** (e.g., MDDV and spatial propagation analyses). More recently he has examined how cloud and emerging computing platforms affect parallel and distributed simulation, and he has helped identify grand challenges and research agendas for modeling and simulation of complex systems.

---

## Notable Works
- "Parallel discrete event simulation" (1990) — A seminal tutorial and synthesis that shaped understanding of PDES, summarizing synchronization strategies, performance drivers, and practical issues that guided two decades of subsequent research.
- "Parallel and Distributed Simulation Systems" (2000) — A comprehensive text/survey that consolidates foundational theory, system design, and application experience in the PDES community, widely cited as a reference for researchers and practitioners building large-scale simulation systems.
- "Efficient optimistic parallel simulations using reverse computation" (1999) — Introduced and evaluated *reverse computation* as an effective alternative to traditional state saving for optimistic simulation (Time Warp), demonstrating practical performance benefits and influencing later implementations and optimizations.

---

## Academic Background
Richard Fujimoto has a multi-decade academic career anchored at the Georgia Institute of Technology, where he holds the rank of **Regents' Professor** and has led influential research and training in modeling and simulation since the 1980s and 1990s. His publication record — including widely cited tutorials, textbooks, system descriptions (GTW, RTI-Kit), and applied studies in network and vehicular simulation — and his role organizing community activities (workshops, Dagstuhl reports, NSF-sponsored planning efforts) reflect sustained leadership in the field. With nearly 20,000 citations, his contributions have shaped standards and practice in distributed simulation (notably the DoD HLA), motivated new synchronization and rollback techniques, and guided the development of simulation tools for large-scale network and transportation studies. As a senior faculty member he has mentored many students and postdoctoral researchers, held key roles in community workshops and editorial venues, and received institutional recognition through his Regents' Professorship and long-standing affiliation with Georgia Tech's College of Computing.

## Overview
Christopher J. MacLellan is an **Assistant Professor** at the **Georgia Institute of Technology**, where he leads a research group focused on building computational models and interactive systems at the intersection of *cognitive science*, *artificial intelligence*, and *education*. His work centers on developing and evaluating theories and systems that enable <u>intelligent tutoring</u>, <u>incremental concept formation</u>, and effective <u>human–AI teaming</u>. He combines mechanistic cognitive models (e.g., simulated learners and concept formation algorithms) with applied systems (authoring tools and tutor platforms) to_close the loop_ between learning theory and educational technology.

---

## Research Areas
MacLellan’s research spans several tightly connected areas: the design and evaluation of **intelligent tutoring systems** and authoring tools (e.g., SimStudent and Apprentice-style tutor builders), the development of *cognitive models* and simulated learners that both explain human learning and drive adaptivity in tutors, methods for <u>incremental concept formation</u> in structured and visual domains (e.g., TRESTLE and Cobweb-based extensions), and the study of **human–machine teaming**—how agents can be designed to augment team competencies and collaborate effectively with people. His recent work also explores how modern generative models can be integrated into tutor authoring and feedback generation (e.g., research on LLM-based feedback and generative approaches to educator-driven tutor creation), and on evaluation frameworks and testbeds for treating AI agents both as tutors and as students (e.g., Tutorgym and Val). Across these topics he emphasizes connecting computational models to empirical educational data and practical authoring workflows, as exemplified in papers such as "The Apprentice Learner Architecture: Closing the loop between learning theory and educational data" (2016), "Domain-general tutor authoring with apprentice learner models" (2022), and "Improving teamwork competencies in human-machine teams: Perspectives from team science" (2021).

---

## Notable Works
- "Improving teamwork competencies in human-machine teams: Perspectives from team science" (2021) — A perspective piece that synthesizes team science and AI methods to identify design principles and computational techniques for enabling effective *human–machine teaming*, shaping subsequent work on asymmetric cooperative games and team-centric evaluation.
- "The Apprentice Learner Architecture: Closing the loop between learning theory and educational data" (2016) — Introduces an architecture that operationalizes learning theories in simulated learners to both test cognitive hypotheses and automate elements of tutor authoring, bridging theory-driven models and applied ITS development.
- "Domain-general tutor authoring with apprentice learner models" (2022) — Demonstrates how apprentice-style simulated learners can be used to support *domain-general* tutor authoring, reducing the expertise barrier for creating adaptive instructional systems and informing later efforts in generative and educator-driven tutor interfaces.

---

## Academic Background
MacLellan’s body of work reflects interdisciplinary training across **cognitive systems, artificial intelligence,** and **learning sciences**; he is currently an Assistant Professor at Georgia Tech, where he directs research on computational models of learning and applied educational technologies. Over the past decade he has authored influential contributions on simulated learners, concept formation (e.g., TRESTLE, Cobweb4V), tutor authoring (SimStudent, Apprentice-based toolchains), and human–AI teaming, accumulating over 980 citations for his work to date. He maintains an active lab and code/paper repository (see his homepage: https://chrismaclellan.com/) and collaborates broadly with researchers in education, HCI, and AI. His publications span theory development, algorithmic innovations, and practical systems for authoring and evaluating tutors, positioning him at the nexus of theory-driven cognitive modeling and deployable educational AI.

## Overview
Joseph Jaeger is an **Assistant Professor** in the School of Computer Science at the **Georgia Institute of Technology**, where he leads a research group in applied and theoretical **cryptography** and **security** (homepage: http://www.cc.gatech.edu/~josephjaeger). His work spans both *practical systems* and *provable-security theory*, with particular emphasis on <u>efficient encrypted search at scale</u>, <u>secure messaging and ratcheting</u>, and <u>security under resource-bounded/adaptive adversaries</u>. Jaeger’s publications have attracted broad attention (total citations: **1,836**) for combining rigorous definitions and reductions with implementations and system analyses.

---

## Research Areas
Jaeger’s research sits at the intersection of systems-oriented cryptography and formal security. He has advanced the theory and practice of <u>searchable encryption</u>—designing dynamic, scalable constructions and practical data structures for servers holding tens of billions of encrypted record–keyword pairs ("Dynamic searchable encryption in very-large databases: Data structures and implementation"). He has formalized and proven security goals for modern secure messaging by studying <u>ratcheted encryption</u> and channel safety under state compromise ("Ratcheted encryption and key exchange: The security of messaging", "Optimal channel security against fine-grained state compromise: The safety of messaging"). Other threads include the study of <u>algorithm-substitution attacks</u> and covert subversion ("Mass-surveillance without the state: Strongly undetectable algorithm-substitution attacks"), the development of *memory-tight* reductions and proofs that account for attacker memory limits ("The memory-tightness of authenticated encryption", "Hiding in plain sight: memory-tight proofs via randomness programming"), and work on concrete/expected-time security and post-quantum considerations ("Expected-time cryptography: generic techniques...", "Quantum key-length extension"). Throughout, Jaeger frequently blends new definitional frameworks (e.g., adaptive compromise models, multi-user/multi-challenge notions) with concrete constructions and implementation-minded evaluations.

---

## Notable Works
- "Dynamic searchable encryption in very-large databases: Data structures and implementation" (2014) — a foundational practical/theoretical treatment of *dynamic symmetric searchable encryption* that scales to tens of billions of record–keyword pairs and provides asymptotically optimal server-side costs, bridging cryptographic definitions and deployable data structures.  
- "Pact: Privacy sensitive protocols and mechanisms for mobile contact tracing" (2020) — an influential, policy- and systems-oriented set of protocols and mechanisms aimed at enabling large-scale, privacy-preserving contact tracing during the COVID-19 pandemic, demonstrating Jaeger’s ability to apply cryptographic thinking to urgent real-world problems.  
- "Ratcheted encryption and key exchange: The security of messaging" (2017) — a rigorous formalization and provable-security treatment of ratcheting techniques used in modern secure messaging, providing syntax, definitions, and constructions that clarify and strengthen the foundations of end-to-end message security.

## Academic Background
Jaeger is an Assistant Professor at Georgia Tech’s School of Computer Science, where he directs a research program combining cryptographic theory and system-level security. His publication record shows sustained presence in premier cryptography venues (CRYPTO, EUROCRYPT, TCC and related conferences) and collaboration across systems and theory communities. Jaeger’s work has been widely cited and has influenced both academic directions (memory-tight proofs, adaptive compromise models, authenticated encryption analyses) and practical systems (searchable encryption deployments, messaging protocol analyses, privacy-sensitive contact-tracing proposals). He holds advanced training in computer science with a focus on cryptography and has a track record of mentoring students and contributing to community resources (e.g., protocol design and publicly available implementations) that bridge theory and practice.

## Overview
Joy Arulraj is an **Assistant Professor** in the School of Computer Science at the **Georgia Institute of Technology**, where she leads a research group in *database systems and data management*. Her work advances the design, implementation, and automation of modern **database management systems (DBMSs)**, with a particular emphasis on systems that exploit emerging hardware and automated tuning. She is best known for work on **self-driving DBMSs**, the architecture of **non-volatile memory (NVM)**-aware systems, and performance diagnosis and verification tools for database software. Her research explores the interaction between hardware, concurrency control, storage/recovery, and automated system management to deliver robust, high-performance data platforms that simplify administration and improve reliability. Key recurring themes in her portfolio include <u>durability and recovery</u>, <u>concurrency control and multiversioning</u>, and <u>automated performance detection and tuning</u>.

---

## Research Areas
Arulraj’s research spans core and applied topics in *database systems and data management*. She has made foundational contributions to the design of DBMSs for emerging persistent memory technologies, addressing storage layouts, logging, and recovery (e.g., "Let's talk about storage & recovery methods for non-volatile memory database systems"; "How to build a non-volatile memory database management system"; "Non-volatile memory database management systems"). She investigates transactional concurrency and isolation for high-performance in-memory systems, including empirical studies of multi-version concurrency control ("An empirical evaluation of in-memory multi-version concurrency control") and designs for latch-free, parallel indexes tuned for persistent memory ("Bztree: A high-performance latch-free range index for non-volatile memory"). Another thrust of her work targets automation and reliability: she has advanced the notion of *self-driving* or autonomic DBMSs that reduce human-in-the-loop tuning ("Self-Driving Database Management Systems") and developed tools for automated detection and diagnosis of performance regressions and anti-patterns in DBMSs ("APOLLO", "SQLCheck", "Automatic detection of performance bugs in database systems using equivalent queries"). More recently, Arulraj has extended her systems expertise to the video analytics domain and AI-augmented querying—building end-to-end VDBMSs and query-optimization techniques for visual data (EVA, Figo, Thia, SketchQL)—as well as exploring GPU-accelerated and edge-cloud data management designs. Across these areas she blends experimental systems-building, empirical evaluation, and formal/automated analysis (e.g., query equivalence verification) to improve performance, correctness, and usability of data systems.

---

## Notable Works
- "Self-Driving Database Management Systems." (2017) — Articulated a roadmap for automating physical design, tuning, and configuration in DBMSs and influenced subsequent work on predictive and automatic adaptation of database components.
- "Bztree: A high-performance latch-free range index for non-volatile memory." (2018) — Introduced a latch-free index structure designed to exploit persistent memory and modern CPU parallelism, demonstrating both high throughput and fast recovery properties for NVM-resident databases.
- "Let's talk about storage & recovery methods for non-volatile memory database systems." (2015) — An influential analysis of how NVM changes fundamental assumptions in DBMS logging, storage hierarchy, and recovery, guiding architecture decisions for NVM-aware systems.

---

## Academic Background
Joy Arulraj is a prolific researcher in the databases community with a broad publication record across top systems and data venues. Based at the Georgia Institute of Technology, she has produced work that spans doctoral-dissertation–scale system design, multi-institution collaboration, and multiple highly cited conference papers and systems. Her publications have accrued over **3,096 citations**, reflecting substantial community impact. Arulraj routinely contributes to program committees and workshops in data management and systems, mentors graduate students and collaborators, and has secured research support typical of experimental systems groups (industry and governmental sponsors). Her scholarly trajectory is characterized by sustained leadership on problems at the intersection of storage hardware, concurrency, recovery, and automated database operation.

## Overview
Will Powell is a Research Technologist at **Georgia Institute of Technology**, working within the computer systems and high-performance computing community. He is a core contributor to the development and operation of the **Rogues Gallery** experimental testbed and supports interdisciplinary teaching and research activities that bridge *systems engineering*, *HPC pedagogy*, and *experimental architectures*. His primary focus is on the practical challenges of deploying, managing, and onboarding users to emerging compute platforms — with emphasis on <u>experimental post-Moore architectures</u>, <u>HPC systems management</u>, and <u>novel server and networking integration</u>.

---

## Research Areas
Powell’s work centers on the operational and educational aspects of heterogeneous and emerging computing platforms. He investigates the lifecycle of experimental devices (what his papers call the <u>Rogues Gallery</u>) including device provisioning, runtime management, and integration into shared research and teaching infrastructures. His publications address topics such as user onboarding to Arm-based HPC (notably the A64FX via Open OnDemand), incorporation of FPGAs and RISC-V/Arm systems into HPC curricula, and the systems-management practices needed to support these platforms at scale. He also engages with practical server and networking concerns that arise when coupling non-traditional accelerators into campus and departmental clusters, and with pedagogical models (e.g., the VIP course) that create hands-on, multidisciplinary student experiences with novel hardware.

## Notable Works
- "Wrangling rogues: A case study on managing experimental post-moore architectures" (2019) — a detailed case study of the Rogues Gallery testbed that documents operational lessons and management strategies for integrating heterogeneous, “rogue” devices into a shared research environment.  
- "Onboarding users to a64fx via open ondemand" (2022) — a practical contribution describing methods to simplify user access to Arm HPC systems (A64FX) through web-accessible tooling (Open OnDemand), lowering the barrier for researchers to use non-x86 architectures.  
- "Enhancing hpc education and workflows with novel computing architectures" (2022) — a paper that articulates curricular and workflow adaptations needed to incorporate Arm, RISC-V, and FPGA-based platforms into HPC education and research workflows, including instrumentation of teaching pipelines and resource allocation strategies.

## Academic Background
Will Powell is affiliated with Georgia Tech as a Research Technologist in computer systems and HPC, supporting both research testbeds and curriculum-driven projects. His publication record documents sustained engagement with the campus-wide Vertically Integrated Projects (VIP) Program and collaborations across systems, networking, and educational groups. While formal degree information and specific honors are not listed on public pages, his role and authored case studies indicate extensive hands-on experience in systems management, cluster operations, and HPC pedagogy. Powell’s work has contributed to the Georgia Tech community’s ability to evaluate and teach emerging architectures, and his scholarship has accumulated citation impact across operational and educational venues (total citations: 13). His homepage at Georgia Tech documents ongoing involvement in experimental platform administration and interdisciplinary student engagement.

## Overview
Michael Donald Bailey is a faculty researcher at the **Georgia Institute of Technology**, where he leads a research group focused on the intersection of **software systems** and **Internet-scale security and measurement**. His lab combines *large-scale measurement*, *systems design*, and *empirical security analysis* to study real-world threats and defenses. Central to his work are investigations of <u>botnets and distributed denial-of-service (DDoS)</u>, <u>Internet-wide scanning and measurement</u>, and the <u>ecosystems that underpin secure communications (e.g., TLS/PKI)</u>. Bailey’s publications emphasize reproducible, measurement-driven insights that inform both academic understanding and operational practice.

---

## Research Areas
Bailey’s research spans multiple, tightly related areas in software systems and network security. He is best known for empirical and measurement-driven studies of large-scale threats—most prominently work on the Mirai botnet and long-running analyses of DDoS amplification vectors—which combine network telemetry with protocol and system analysis. He has applied Internet-wide scanning to reveal systemic vulnerabilities (e.g., studies underpinning the analysis of Heartbleed and the development of search tools for scan-backed discovery), and has produced influential measurements of the HTTPS certificate ecosystem and certificate misissuance. Other topics in his corpus include automated malware classification and anti-analysis techniques, covert channels in virtualized/cloud environments, IPv6 deployment and security policy, and security failures in modern web and IoT ecosystems (voice assistants, in-browser cryptojacking, smart contracts). Across these areas he frequently pairs novel measurement methodologies (darknet monitoring, wide-scale scans, ground-truth datasets) with systems-building (detectors, linters, and defensive architectures) to both characterize problems and propose practical mitigations.

## Notable Works
- "Understanding the Mirai Botnet" (2017) — A high-impact retrospective analysis that reconstructed Mirai’s propagation and operational characteristics, providing foundational insights into how IoT devices powered massive DDoS attacks and informing subsequent defensive strategies.  
- "The matter of heartbleed" (2014) — An Internet-scale investigation of the Heartbleed vulnerability that quantified exposure, measured remediation behavior, and illustrated how rapid, empirical scanning can shape understanding of protocol-level failures.  
- "Automated classification and analysis of internet malware" (2007) — Early, influential work on automated analysis pipelines for malware that advanced scalable approaches to detection, labeling, and characterization of large malware datasets.

## Academic Background
Bailey is a seasoned researcher in computer science and software systems affiliated with Georgia Tech’s College of Computing. Over his career he has produced a body of highly cited, widely used measurement studies and systems papers and has contributed to community-shaping efforts such as the Menlo Report on ethics for ICT research. His work has appeared in top systems and security venues and has been repeatedly adopted by practitioners and researchers alike (evidenced by substantial citation impact and broad topical influence). He maintains an active research webpage and group at Georgia Tech (http://mbailey.cc.gatech.edu/), collaborates across academia, industry, and operational communities, and regularly participates in program committees, workshops, and community standards and best-practice discussions that bridge measurement, systems, and security.

## Overview
Constantine Dovrolis is a **Professor of Computer Science** at **Georgia Tech** (College of Computing), where he leads a research group that combines systems experimentation, measurement, and modeling. His work spans large-scale **network measurement** and **network science**, with a sustained emphasis on practical tools and methodologies for understanding Internet performance. More recently he has extended his interests into *neuro-inspired machine learning* and *continual learning*, bridging networking and learning systems. His signature contributions focus on <u>available bandwidth estimation</u>, <u>packet-dispersion and capacity measurement</u>, and <u>adaptive HTTP video streaming behavior</u> — research that produced widely used tools (e.g., **Pathload**) and influential measurement methodologies (e.g., **SLoPS**).

---

## Research Areas
Dovrolis’s research portfolio centers on empirical and analytic studies of network behavior, measurement methodology, and protocol/system design. He has developed and evaluated active measurement techniques for **available bandwidth** (see "Bandwidth estimation: metrics, measurement techniques, and tools" (2003) and the SLoPS methodology in "End-to-end available bandwidth: Measurement methodology, dynamics, and relation with TCP throughput" (2002)), and he has systematically characterized the limits and pitfalls of packet-dispersion methods ("What do packet dispersion techniques measure?" (2001); "Packet-dispersion techniques and a capacity-estimation methodology" (2004)). Another major thread is video delivery and adaptive streaming: his experimental evaluations of rate-adaptation algorithms ("An experimental evaluation of rate-adaptation algorithms in adaptive streaming over HTTP" (2011)) and investigations into player interactions and stabilization mechanisms ("What happens when HTTP adaptive streaming players compete for bandwidth?" (2012); "Server-based traffic shaping for stabilizing oscillating adaptive streaming players" (2013)) have shaped thinking about both client algorithms and server/CDN strategies. Complementing measurement work, he has studied Internet structure and economics (e.g., the evolution of the AS ecosystem in "Ten years..." (2008), "Twelve years..." (2011), and the shift toward a peering mesh in "The internet is flat" (2010)), routing and BGP robustness ("Beware of BGP attacks" (2004)), and infrastructure issues such as router buffer sizing and its performance trade-offs. More recently, his interests include **neuro-inspired continual learning** (e.g., "Nispa: Neuro-inspired stability-plasticity adaptation for continual learning in sparse networks" (2022)), demonstrating a willingness to apply network-science thinking and principled measurement to problems in machine learning and computational science.

## Notable Works
- "Bandwidth estimation: metrics, measurement techniques, and tools" (2003) — A comprehensive synthesis that framed the available-bandwidth problem for the research community, evaluated competing metrics and techniques, and motivated practical measurement tools; this work has been foundational for subsequent active measurement tools and studies (widely cited and influential).
- "End-to-end available bandwidth: Measurement methodology, dynamics, and relation with TCP throughput" (2002) — Introduced the **SLoPS** (Self-Loading Periodic Streams) methodology for end-to-end available bandwidth measurement and analyzed dynamics and relation to TCP performance; helped establish rigorous methodology for active probing.
- "An experimental evaluation of rate-adaptation algorithms in adaptive streaming over HTTP" (2011) — A systematic experimental study of adaptive video players that exposed key performance behaviors and trade-offs, informing both client adaptation design and CDN/server-side strategies for video delivery.

## Academic Background
Professor Dovrolis is a long-standing faculty member in the Georgia Tech College of Computing, with a prolific record of experimental, measurement-driven research and community-facing tools. His work has accumulated over sixteen thousand citations across topics in networking, systems, and, more recently, machine learning, reflecting broad impact across measurement methodology, Internet architecture, and multimedia delivery. He has contributed to community resources and measurement platforms (for example, involvement with Measurement Lab and multiple widely used measurement tools), mentored numerous graduate students and collaborators, and regularly publishes in top networking and systems venues. His interdisciplinary keywords — **Neuro-inspired Machine Learning**, **Network Science**, and **Computational Science** — capture both his traditional networking focus and his more recent engagements at the intersection of learning and complex systems.

## Overview
Alan Ritter is a faculty researcher at the **Georgia Institute of Technology**, maintaining an active research group focusing on *Natural Language Processing* and *Machine Learning*. His work centers on building robust, practical language technologies for noisy, real-world text, with particular emphasis on <u>social media language understanding</u>, <u>open‑domain dialogue generation</u>, and <u>information extraction from user‑generated content</u>. Ritter’s research blends empirical engineering of scalable systems with methodological advances in learning paradigms such as reinforcement learning and adversarial training, and he curates resources and shared tasks to support the broader NLP community.

---

## Research Areas
Ritter’s research spans several interlocking areas in modern NLP and AI. He has made foundational contributions to conversational systems, exploring long‑term optimization of agent behavior through **deep reinforcement learning** for dialogue (e.g., “Deep reinforcement learning for dialogue generation”, 2016) and to *adversarial training* approaches for open‑domain response generation (“Adversarial learning for neural dialogue generation”, 2017). A second major strand addresses the challenges of noisy, informal text on social media: he led influential empirical studies and practical systems for Twitter processing, including **named entity recognition** in tweets (“Named Entity Recognition in Tweets: An Experimental Study”, 2011), part‑of‑speech tagging and normalization, and methods for event and life‑event extraction from Twitter (“Open domain event extraction from twitter”, 2012; “Major life event extraction from twitter”, 2014). Ritter also explores continual and broad learning paradigms (notably work on *never‑ending learning*, 2015), cross‑lingual and transfer methods, privacy and robustness of large models, and interdisciplinary applications such as extracting wet‑lab protocol structure and cybersecurity signal detection from social streams.

## Notable Works
- “Deep reinforcement learning for dialogue generation” (2016) — Introduced methods for optimizing conversational agents with reinforcement learning to account for future dialogue outcomes, helping move neural dialogue systems beyond one‑step greedy generation.
- “Named Entity Recognition in Tweets: An Experimental Study” (2011) — Systematic empirical study and methodological advances for performing NER over noisy social media text, establishing benchmarks and techniques widely used in downstream social media NLP.
- “Never Ending Learning” (2015) — Contributed to the vision and methodology for lifelong learning systems that continuously accumulate knowledge from diverse sources, influencing subsequent work on persistent, open‑world learning.
